text:
You are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.

## Workspace layout

Everything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in
`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:
`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.

- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for
  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at
  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.
- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.
  Read-only.
- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness
  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.
- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the
  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be
  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.

## Tool conventions

- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and
  plain text).
- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables
  (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx).
- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`
  summarizing your work.
- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.

## Context management (automatic)

To keep your context small and your runs cheap, the harness automatically
elides the *raw text* of large `read`/`bash` outputs after they have been on
screen for a few turns, replacing each with a short `[context-saver: …]` stub
that names the file or command. The document was fully read when it was
current — nothing was hidden from your initial pass — only the bulk is dropped
from later turns. Two habits keep this lossless:

- As you read a document, **record the specific facts, figures, dates, and
  verbatim clauses you will need** into your notes or draft. Don't rely on the
  full text staying resident.
- If you later need the exact text of a doc that now shows a `[context-saver:
  …]` stub, simply **`read` that file again** — the full content returns.

## File-format skills

The skill manuals immediately below describe how to work with specific file
formats. Read them before tackling the task.


## Skill: docx

---
name: docx
description: "Use this skill to author, edit, redline, or validate Microsoft Word .docx files. Covers creating new documents from markdown or templates, editing existing documents in place, generating tracked-changes redlines, adding comments, and accepting/rejecting revisions. For READING existing .docx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'draft a memo', 'mark up the agreement', 'redline this', 'add comments to', 'fill the engagement letter template'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .xlsx, .pptx, or .doc (legacy Word)."
---

# DOCX authoring, editing, redlining

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .docx, use the harness `read` tool. It already returns structured text via pandoc. This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *validating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a new doc from markdown | `scripts/generate_from_md.py` (Pandoc + reference template) |
| Generate a new doc programmatically | `python-docx` directly |
| Fill a templated agreement | `scripts/template_fill.py` (docxtpl / Jinja) |
| Edit an existing doc | `scripts/unpack.py` → mutate XML → `scripts/pack.py` |
| Produce a tracked-changes redline | `scripts/redline.py` |
| Add comments to a passage | `scripts/comments_add.py` |
| Accept all redlines | `scripts/accept_changes.py` |
| Validate a docx before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` (mandatory final step) |

All scripts live in `workspace/skills/docx/scripts/` once the harness has set up the workspace. Invoke them via `bash`.

## Creating a new document

Pick by what you have:

- **Markdown content + a styled firm template** → `generate_from_md.py input.md out.docx template.docx`. Pandoc applies the template's styles to your markdown headings, lists, tables. Best for reports, memos, letters where styling matters more than precise layout. Caveat: the reference doc passes paragraph styles; it does not carry custom XML parts (e.g., comment threads).
- **A template with named placeholders** → `template_fill.py template.docx context.json out.docx`. docxtpl renders Jinja2 expressions inside the template. Best for engagement letters, NDAs, structured agreements.
- **Programmatic build** → write Python using `python-docx`. Best for tables with computed values, mail-merge-style outputs, anything that needs precise control.

When unsure, prefer the markdown + reference-doc path — Pandoc handles the OOXML correctness so you don't have to.

## Editing an existing document

Three-step pattern:

```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.docx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/word/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.docx
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Key files inside the unpacked tree:

- `word/document.xml` — body content (paragraphs, runs, tables)
- `word/styles.xml` — paragraph + run style definitions
- `word/numbering.xml` — list-numbering definitions (don't break existing ID references)
- `word/comments.xml` — comment thread content (created by `comments_add.py`)
- `word/header*.xml`, `word/footer*.xml` — running headers/footers
- `[Content_Types].xml` — MIME registration for every part. Edit when you add a new part type.
- `_rels/` and `word/_rels/` — relationships between parts. Edit when you reference a new image, comment, or external resource.

### Run-merging gotcha

Word writes adjacent runs (`<w:r>`) with identical formatting separately. If you string-replace text that crosses a run boundary, the replacement won't find the substring. `unpack.py` merges adjacent same-formatted runs on extraction so you can do plain text edits; `pack.py` is permissive about whatever run structure you write back.

### Smart-quote escaping

Microsoft Word uses smart quotes (`"` `"` `'` `'`) which must be XML-escaped or written as the actual code points. `unpack.py` substitutes them with XML entities for editing safety; `pack.py` reverses the substitution before zipping. Don't manually re-escape — let the scripts handle it.

### Whitespace preservation

Trailing whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements is significant. Both `unpack.py` and `pack.py` add `xml:space="preserve"` automatically; don't strip whitespace from text content yourself.

## Redlines (tracked changes)

```bash
python scripts/redline.py original.docx revised.docx redlined.docx \
  --author "Reviewer" --date "2026-04-30"
```

Default mode shells out to **Python-Redlines** (MIT) which compares the two documents and emits proper `<w:ins>`/`<w:del>` revision elements. Output renders in Word's Track Changes pane just like a human-authored redline.

### Manual mode

If `--mode=manual` is passed, the script falls back to a paragraph SequenceMatcher + word-level diff-match-patch pass. Use this when:
- Python-Redlines fails on a particular doc structure (rare).
- You want to control which paragraphs are diffed.
- The change is purely formatting (`<w:rPrChange>` runs) rather than text.

### What gets tracked

- Insertions and deletions of text
- Insertions and deletions of paragraphs (deleted-whole-paragraph case requires `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>` or you get an empty paragraph after acceptance)
- Run-property changes via `<w:rPrChange>` (formatting-only revisions)

### What doesn't get tracked

- Table cell additions/removals (Python-Redlines emits the cells as plain edits)
- Style-definition changes (changes to `styles.xml` aren't revision-trackable)
- Image swaps

## Comments

```bash
python scripts/comments_add.py document.docx comments.json
```

`comments.json` is a list of `{anchor_text, author, comment}` objects. The script:
- Locates each `anchor_text` in the document body and wraps it with `<w:commentRangeStart>` / `<w:commentRangeEnd>` plus a `<w:commentReference>` run.
- Creates or appends to `word/comments.xml` (with proper id assignment).
- Patches `[Content_Types].xml` and `word/_rels/document.xml.rels` if commenting is being added for the first time.

Anchor matching is exact-string. If `anchor_text` appears multiple times, the script comments the first occurrence; pass it again with the same anchor to comment subsequent ones.

`<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` must be siblings of the `<w:r>` runs they bracket — never nested inside a run. If you write comments by hand, follow this rule.

## Accept / reject changes

```bash
python scripts/accept_changes.py redlined.docx accepted.docx
```

Uses LibreOffice headless via a documented StarBasic macro:

```basic
Sub AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.store() End Sub
```

LibreOffice must be installed and on PATH (`soffice` binary). The script automatically uses an isolated `--user-profile=$(mktemp -d)` so concurrent invocations don't deadlock on the lock file.

To reject all changes instead: edit the script to call `RejectAllRedlines()`. To accept selectively: this isn't supported — open the doc in Word.

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring the task complete.**

```bash
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Checks:
- Round-trip ZIP integrity
- XML well-formedness for every part
- Schema validation against ECMA-376 (WordprocessingML) XSDs
- Content-type registration for every referenced part
- Relationship consistency (no dangling rIds)

Exit code 0 = valid. Non-zero exit code with line-number diagnostics = fix and re-pack.

## Common pitfalls

- **Legacy `.doc` (binary) is not supported.** Convert with `soffice --convert-to docx input.doc` first.
- **List numbering breaks after edits.** Numbering lives in `word/numbering.xml` keyed by `numId`. If you delete a list, also delete its numId reference; if you reorder, don't change IDs.
- **Headers and footers are separate parts** (`word/header1.xml`, etc.). Edits to body content don't touch them.
- **Pandoc reference-doc passes paragraph styles only.** Custom XML parts (comments, tracked changes baseline) are not carried over.
- **Don't pretty-print whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements.** Pretty-printing breaks runs that depend on exact spacing.
- **Tables: dual width specs.** Each cell needs both `columnWidths` (in `<w:tblGrid>`) and per-cell `<w:tcW w:type="dxa">`. Percentages (`pct`) render fine in Word but break in Google Docs.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- Producing PDFs from .docx: pipe through `soffice --convert-to pdf` after this skill is done.
- Signing / encryption / DRM.
- Word macros (`.docm` with VBA).



## Skill: pptx

---
name: pptx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft PowerPoint .pptx files. Covers generating decks from scratch (HTML+PptxGenJS, Marp markdown-to-slides, or python-pptx), editing existing decks in place, and validating output. For READING existing .pptx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a deck', 'create slides', 'edit slide N', 'add a chart slide'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .xlsx, or .ppt (legacy)."
---

# PPTX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .pptx, use the harness `read` tool (markitdown extracts slide text). This skill is for *writing* and *editing*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a deck from scratch (HTML/CSS) | `scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js` |
| Generate a deck from markdown | `scripts/generate_marp.sh` |
| Build slides programmatically | `python-pptx` directly |
| Edit a shape on an existing slide | `scripts/edit_shape.py` (JSON patch) |
| Add or remove a slide | unpack → edit → pack |
| QA a deck deterministically | `scripts/deterministic_qa.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Generation modalities

**HTML/CSS via PptxGenJS** (preferred for visual fidelity):
```bash
node scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js deck.json out.pptx
```
`deck.json` describes slides as a JSON tree; the script invokes PptxGenJS (and html2pptx for HTML inputs) to produce a fully-editable .pptx. Best for branded decks with gradients, custom fonts, complex shapes.

**Markdown via Marp**:
```bash
bash scripts/generate_marp.sh deck.md out.pptx
```
Best for content-heavy decks (lectures, reports) where markdown is more natural than JSON.

**Programmatic via python-pptx**:
Best when shapes are computed (e.g., one slide per data row). Requires manual EMU positioning.

## Editing existing decks

Three-step pattern, like docx:
```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.pptx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/ppt/slides/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.pptx
python scripts/validate.py output.pptx
```

For surgical shape edits without unpacking, use `edit_shape.py`:
```bash
python scripts/edit_shape.py input.pptx \
  --slide 2 --shape "Title 1" --op set_text --value "New title"
```

JSON patch ops: `set_text`, `set_position` (EMU), `set_size`, `recolor`, `delete`.

## OOXML gotchas specific to pptx

- **Use `defusedxml.minidom`, NOT `xml.etree.ElementTree`.** ElementTree corrupts presentation namespaces during round-tripping. `unpack.py` and `pack.py` use minidom; if you write your own XML manipulation, do the same.
- **EMU units everywhere.** 1 inch = 914400 EMU. Slide positions, sizes, font sizes (in pt × 100) all use derived EMU values.
- **Placeholders vs free shapes.** Placeholders inherit from slide masters; free shapes don't. Editing a placeholder's text is `<a:t>` content; editing its layout requires master-slide changes.
- **Don't pretty-print pptx XML on pack.** Whitespace-significant runs (`<a:r>`) break if reformatted. `pack.py` preserves the original whitespace.
- **Slide cloning is more than a file copy.** Use `unpack` + `pack` — manual file copies miss the rIds in `_rels/` and the `Content_Types.xml` registration.

## Deterministic QA loop

After every generation, run:

```bash
python scripts/thumbnail.py deck.pptx thumbs/   # PDF + JPEGs per slide
python scripts/deterministic_qa.py deck.pptx > qa.json
```

`deterministic_qa.py` checks:
- Shape bounding boxes don't extend past slide edges
- No two shapes overlap with > 50% area intersection
- Font sizes ≥ 11pt for body text, ≥ 18pt for titles
- All placeholders are filled (no `Click to add title` defaults)
- Bullet lists don't exceed 7 items per slide

Output is JSON listing each violation with slide number and shape id. Fix violations and re-render.

(A vision-model QA pass is intentionally out of scope for v1. Add `--use-vision` later if deterministic checks miss layout issues.)

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring done.** Schema-validates against ECMA-376 PresentationML XSDs, checks rId consistency, content-type registration.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- SmartArt creation (limited python-pptx support).
- Complex embedded charts beyond what python-pptx exposes.
- Slide transitions / animations (rarely matter for legal output).
- Vision-model layout review (deferred to v2).



## Skill: xlsx

---
name: xlsx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft Excel .xlsx files. Covers building workbooks with formulas, editing existing files, recalculating formulas, and scanning for #REF!/#DIV/0!/#VALUE! errors. For READING existing .xlsx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a model', 'create a spreadsheet', 'fill the schedule', 'recalculate'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .pptx, or .xls (legacy Excel)."
---

# XLSX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .xlsx, use the harness `read` tool (pandas extracts every sheet as a markdown table). This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *recalculating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Build a workbook from scratch | `openpyxl` directly, or `scripts/build_workbook.py` for banker conventions |
| Edit cells in an existing file | `openpyxl.load_workbook(...)` → mutate → save |
| Recalculate formulas (full fidelity) | `scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py` |
| Recalculate formulas (no LibreOffice) | `scripts/recalc_pure_python.py` |
| Scan for formula errors | `scripts/scan_errors.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Banker conventions (mandatory for financial models)

Apply these to every workbook unless the task explicitly overrides:

- **Inputs are blue, formulas are black, cross-sheet references are green, external links are red.** Use `Font(color='0000FF')` etc.
- **Negatives in parentheses, not minus signs.** Use number format `#,##0;(#,##0)`.
- **Red negatives in P&L tables.** Use `#,##0;[Red](#,##0)`.
- **Accounting format for currency.** `_-* #,##0_-;-* #,##0_-;_-* "-"_-;_-@_-` (or the localized equivalent).
- **Multiples shown as `0.0x`**, not `0.0` followed by an "x" character. Format: `0.0"x"`.
- **Underline-only on totals**, not bold-and-underline. Use `Border(bottom=Side(style='thin'))`.
- **No merged cells in input ranges.** Merged cells break formulas that reference them; reserve merging for headers and titles only.
- **Units in adjacent cells**, not in the cell with the value. `($M)` next to the value, not `"$1,234M"` as a string.

`scripts/build_workbook.py` applies these conventions automatically given a JSON spec.

## Formula authoring

- **Always emit formulas, never calculated values.** If the user wants `revenue × growth`, write `=B2*C2`, not `1234.56`. The recalc step materializes values.
- **Use named ranges** for cross-sheet inputs. `wb.defined_names["assumptions"] = DefinedName(...)`. Easier to audit.
- **Document units in adjacent cells** so the model is self-explanatory.
- **No volatile functions in hot paths.** `OFFSET`, `INDIRECT`, `NOW`, `TODAY` recalculate on every change and slow large workbooks.

## Recalculation — choose your engine

`openpyxl` writes formula *strings*; it does not evaluate them. You must recalculate before delivery, otherwise consumers will see `=B2*C2` literal text where they expect numbers (in some readers) or stale cached values (in others).

**LibreOffice path** (`recalc_libreoffice.py`) — ground truth:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Drives LibreOffice headless via the StarBasic macro `ThisComponent.calculateAll(); ThisComponent.store()`. Slow (~5–10s per workbook) but matches Excel for nearly every function. Use this when the workbook contains modern Excel features.

**Pure-Python path** (`recalc_pure_python.py`) — fast, partial:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_pure_python.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Uses `xlcalculator` to evaluate every formula in pure Python. Fast (~0.5s per workbook). Covers ~80% of common functions: arithmetic, `SUM`, `IF`, `VLOOKUP`, `INDEX`/`MATCH`, basic string/date functions.

**Does NOT support**: `XLOOKUP`, `LET`, dynamic arrays (`FILTER`, `SEQUENCE`, `UNIQUE`), `LAMBDA`, `BYROW`, `TEXTJOIN` with refs, structured table references, most modern (post-2019) Excel features.

If you used any of those, run the LibreOffice path. The pure-Python path is for CI environments without LibreOffice.

## Error scan

After every recalc, scan for formula errors:

```bash
python scripts/scan_errors.py output.xlsx > errors.json
```

Reports every cell whose computed value matches `#REF!`, `#DIV/0!`, `#VALUE!`, `#NAME?`, `#NULL!`, `#NUM!`, or `#N/A`. Output is JSONL with `{sheet, address, value}` per line.

If errors exist, fix and re-recalc. Don't ship a workbook with `#REF!`s — it's the most common reason a deliverable fails QA.

## Validation gate

`scripts/validate.py output.xlsx` schema-validates against ECMA-376 SpreadsheetML XSDs and confirms ZIP integrity, content-type registration, sheet relationships.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- **PivotTables** — `openpyxl` round-trips existing pivots but cannot create or modify them. If a task requires pivot creation, escalate (Windows-only via COM, not portable).
- **DAX measures and Power Pivot** — `openpyxl` can't write these. Same limitation.
- **VBA macros (`.xlsm`)** — out of scope for this skill. Macros require Excel runtime.
- **Conditional formatting beyond simple cell-value rules** — `openpyxl` supports basic CF; complex rules (top-N, data bars across sheets) often fail to round-trip.
- **Charts beyond the python-pptx-equivalent set** — line/bar/scatter work; combo charts and trendlines round-trip unreliably.
Review the Phase I ESA and supporting environmental documents for the proposed acquisition and prepare an issues memo with severity ratings. Output: `environmental-issues-memo.docx`.

**[PHASE I ENVIRONMENTAL SITE ASSESSMENT]{.underline}**

**2850 Millrace Road** **Dayton, Ohio 45414** **Montgomery County**

**Prepared for:**

Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC 400 Commerce Tower, Suite 1250 Columbus, Ohio 43215

**Prepared by:**

Terravista Environmental Consulting Inc. 770 Greenway Drive, Suite 300 Cincinnati, Ohio 45202

**Project Manager:**

Rachel Ostrander, P.G. Ohio Licensed Professional Geologist No. PG-2847

**Report Date:** April 18, 2025

**Project Number:** TEC-2025-0347

This document contains proprietary and confidential information prepared exclusively for the use of Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC. Distribution, reproduction, or disclosure to third parties is not authorized without the prior written consent of Terravista Environmental Consulting Inc. and Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC, except as required by law or regulation, or as expressly permitted by the client engagement agreement dated February 24, 2025.

**[1.0 EXECUTIVE SUMMARY]{.underline}**

Terravista Environmental Consulting Inc. (Terravista) was retained by Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC (the \"User\" or \"Client\") to perform a Phase I Environmental Site Assessment (Phase I ESA) of the property located at 2850 Millrace Road, Dayton, Ohio 45414 (the \"Subject Property\" or \"Site\"). The Subject Property consists of approximately 14.7 acres of improved industrial land situated in Montgomery County, Ohio, currently owned and operated by Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc. (Valtec). The assessment was performed in connection with the Client\'s proposed acquisition of the Subject Property from Valtec.

This Phase I Environmental Site Assessment has been prepared in conformance with ASTM E1527-13, *Standard Practice for Environmental Site Assessments: Phase I Environmental Site Assessment Process*, and is intended to satisfy the All Appropriate Inquiries (AAI) requirements set forth in 40 C.F.R. Part 312. The assessment included review of standard environmental record sources, historical use information from multiple sources, a site reconnaissance conducted on April 2, 2025, and interviews with knowledgeable parties, including current site management personnel. No sampling or testing of environmental media was performed as part of this assessment.

The Subject Property is developed with five industrial and administrative buildings totaling approximately 187,000 square feet. Current operations include stamped metal parts manufacturing, chromium and nickel electroplating, powder coating, and associated warehouse and administrative functions. The property has been used for industrial purposes since approximately 1942, when the western portion was first developed by the Dayton Forge & Die Company. The property has been under the ownership of Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc. since 1987.

**Recognized Environmental Conditions (RECs)**

Based on the findings of this assessment, two recognized environmental conditions were identified for the Subject Property:

**REC-1: Western Yard Area --- Former Above-Ground Storage Tanks.** An area of stressed and absent vegetation and darkened soil was observed in the western portion of the site, encompassing an area of approximately 40 feet by 60 feet. This area corresponds to the location of former above-ground storage tanks associated with the Dayton Forge & Die Company, as depicted on the 1942 Sanborn Fire Insurance Map with an \"oil storage\" annotation. The physical evidence of stressed vegetation and darkened soil, combined with the historical presence of petroleum storage tanks, is indicative of a potential release of petroleum products to soil. This condition constitutes a recognized environmental condition as defined in the applicable ASTM standard. A Phase II investigation is recommended to characterize potential petroleum impacts in this area.

**REC-2: Building 2 --- Electroplating Operations and Floor Staining.** Floor staining was observed on the concrete floor in Building 2 near the chemical drum storage area, covering an area of approximately 15 feet by 20 feet. Building 2 houses active chromium and nickel electroplating operations that have been ongoing since approximately 1987 under Valtec\'s ownership. The observed staining may be indicative of historical spills or releases of plating chemicals to the floor surface. The facility manager attributed the staining to routine operational spillage and stated that floor drains in the plating area are connected to an on-site industrial wastewater pretreatment system that discharges to the municipal publicly owned treatment works (POTW) under NPDES permit number 2PB00417. This condition is classified as a recognized environmental condition, and further investigation is recommended.

**Historical Recognized Environmental Conditions (HRECs)**

Two historical recognized environmental conditions were identified:

**HREC-1: Former Underground Storage Tanks (Parcel A).** Three underground storage tanks (two 10,000-gallon diesel USTs and one 4,000-gallon waste oil UST) were removed from the southern portion of the site in 2004. A closure report was prepared by Redland Environmental Services in November 2004, and a No Further Action (NFA) letter was subsequently issued under the Ohio EPA Voluntary Action Program (VAP) on September 22, 2011, covering a 2.3-acre area designated as Parcel A. This condition has been addressed to the satisfaction of the Ohio EPA VAP and meets the definition of a historical recognized environmental condition.

**HREC-2: Former Central Valley Chemical Co. Operations (Building 3).** Central Valley Chemical Co. operated a chemical blending facility in Building 3 from approximately 1975 to 1981, handling industrial cleaning solvents and degreasers. This condition has been addressed through the Ohio EPA Voluntary Action Program, and a No Further Action letter was issued on September 22, 2011. Based on the issuance of the NFA letter, this past condition meets the definition of a historical recognized environmental condition.

**Controlled Recognized Environmental Conditions (CRECs)**

No controlled recognized environmental conditions were identified for the Subject Property.

**Data Gaps**

Ohio EPA file review records were requested on March 10, 2025, but had not been received as of the report date. This report will be supplemented if and when records are received. Sanborn Fire Insurance Maps for the years 1910 and 1920 were not available for the Subject Property area; however, based on historical aerial photographs, the property was likely in agricultural use during that period. An aerial photograph gap exists between 1980 and 1994; however, other historical sources provide reasonable coverage for this period.

Based on the findings of this assessment, it is the opinion of the undersigned Environmental Professional that this Phase I ESA constitutes All Appropriate Inquiries in accordance with 40 C.F.R. Part 312 and is sufficient to qualify the User for protections under CERCLA, including the bona fide prospective purchaser defense, subject to the User\'s compliance with continuing obligations.

**[2.0 INTRODUCTION]{.underline}**

**2.1 Purpose.** Terravista Environmental Consulting Inc. (Terravista) was retained by Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC (the \"User\" or \"Client\") to conduct a Phase I Environmental Site Assessment (Phase I ESA) of the property located at 2850 Millrace Road, Dayton, Ohio 45414 (the \"Subject Property\" or \"Site\"). The Subject Property comprises approximately 14.7 acres of improved industrial land in Montgomery County, Ohio.

This Phase I Environmental Site Assessment has been prepared in conformance with ASTM E1527-13, *Standard Practice for Environmental Site Assessments: Phase I Environmental Site Assessment Process*, and is intended to satisfy the All Appropriate Inquiries (AAI) requirements set forth in 40 C.F.R. Part 312. The assessment was performed using the methodology and scope of inquiry described in the applicable standard, including the evaluation of standard environmental record sources, historical use information, site reconnaissance observations, and interviews with persons knowledgeable about the Subject Property.

The purpose of this Phase I ESA is to identify recognized environmental conditions (RECs), controlled recognized environmental conditions (CRECs), and historical recognized environmental conditions (HRECs) in connection with the Subject Property. These terms are defined in the applicable ASTM standard and are used throughout this report consistent with those definitions. As defined, a recognized environmental condition means the presence or likely presence of any hazardous substances or petroleum products in, on, or at a property: (1) due to a release to the environment; (2) under conditions indicative of a release to the environment; or (3) under conditions that pose a material threat of a future release to the environment.

This assessment is being performed to support the Client\'s environmental due diligence in connection with a proposed acquisition of the Subject Property from Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc. (the current owner and operator), and to assist in establishing protections under the Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act of 1980, as amended (CERCLA), including the bona fide prospective purchaser (BFPP) defense under 42 U.S.C. § 9607(r). The BFPP defense, as codified in CERCLA Section 107(r), provides certain liability protections to purchasers of contaminated property who, among other requirements, conduct all appropriate inquiries prior to acquisition and comply with ongoing obligations following acquisition. The performance of a Phase I ESA in conformance with the applicable ASTM standard is recognized by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) as satisfying the all appropriate inquiries requirement.

Terravista understands that the Client intends to rely on this report, together with other due diligence information, in making its decision regarding the proposed acquisition. The Client\'s environmental counsel, Nadia Khoury of Bridgewell & Hatch LLP, has been identified as the primary legal contact for the engagement.

**2.2 Scope of Services**

The scope of services for this Phase I ESA included the following principal components, as described in ASTM E1527-13:

1\. **Records Review.** A review of standard environmental record sources, including federal, state, tribal, and local governmental databases, to identify known or potential sources of contamination at or in the vicinity of the Subject Property. The review included searches of databases maintained by the United States Environmental Protection Agency, Ohio Environmental Protection Agency, and other relevant agencies, using the standard ASTM-specified minimum search distances for each database category.

2\. **Historical Use Research.** Research into the historical uses of the Subject Property and surrounding area using historical sources including Sanborn Fire Insurance Maps, historical aerial photographs, city directories, building department records, and prior environmental reports. The historical use research was intended to develop a reasonable understanding of past uses of the Subject Property that may have involved the storage, handling, use, or disposal of hazardous substances or petroleum products.

3\. **Site Reconnaissance.** A visual inspection of the Subject Property and its immediate surroundings, conducted by the Environmental Professional, to observe current conditions and identify evidence of recognized environmental conditions. The site reconnaissance was conducted on April 2, 2025.

4\. **Interviews.** Interviews with current and past owners, operators, occupants, and other persons knowledgeable about the Subject Property, including local government officials, to obtain information relevant to the identification of recognized environmental conditions.

5\. **Evaluation and Reporting.** Evaluation of the information gathered from the foregoing inquiries and preparation of this written report documenting the findings, opinions, and conclusions of the assessment.

The assessment included consideration of the Subject Property and properties located within the standard ASTM search radii, as applicable to each database category. No sampling, testing, or analysis of soil, groundwater, surface water, air, or building materials was performed as part of this Phase I ESA. The assessment does not include evaluation of business environmental risk, nor does it include assessment of ecological or natural resource issues unless such issues are relevant to the identification of recognized environmental conditions.

**2.3 Significant Assumptions**

This Phase I ESA was performed in accordance with generally accepted professional standards and practices in the environmental consulting industry. The assessment is subject to the following significant assumptions:

Terravista has relied upon information provided by the Client, the property owner and its representatives, governmental agencies, and commercial database providers. This information is assumed to be accurate and complete unless there is reason to believe otherwise. Terravista has not independently verified the accuracy of all information provided by third parties, nor has it conducted a comprehensive audit of regulatory compliance by the current or former property owners or operators.

The findings and opinions expressed in this report are based on conditions observed on the date of the site reconnaissance (April 2, 2025) and on information that was reasonably ascertainable as of the report date (April 18, 2025). Environmental conditions at the Subject Property may change over time due to natural processes, human activities, or other factors. This assessment does not guarantee the absence of recognized environmental conditions, nor does it predict future environmental conditions.

This Phase I ESA does not include testing or evaluation of building materials for asbestos-containing materials (ACM), lead-based paint (LBP), radon, mold, or other biological agents. These items are considered non-scope considerations under the applicable ASTM standard and are not required components of the all appropriate inquiries process. Where such materials were observed or suspected during the site reconnaissance, they are noted in Section 8.6 of this report as additional observations outside the ASTM scope.

The assessment is limited to the identification of recognized environmental conditions, controlled recognized environmental conditions, and historical recognized environmental conditions as defined in the applicable ASTM standard. The assessment does not constitute a comprehensive regulatory compliance audit, an assessment of occupational health and safety conditions, or a determination of liability under any environmental statute or regulation.

**2.4 Limitations and Exceptions**

The following limitations and exceptions apply to this Phase I ESA:

**Ohio EPA File Review.** A records request was submitted to the Ohio Environmental Protection Agency, Division of Environmental Response and Revitalization (DERR), on March 10, 2025, requesting available facility files for the Subject Property and for the adjacent property at 2900 Millrace Road. Ohio EPA records were not available for review at the time of this assessment. We will supplement this report if and when records are received. The records request letter and confirmation of submission are provided in Appendix G.

**Sanborn Fire Insurance Maps.** Sanborn Fire Insurance Maps for the years 1910 and 1920 were not available for the Subject Property area from the sources consulted. Based on historical aerial photographs, the earliest of which dates to 1940, the Subject Property and the surrounding area appear to have been in agricultural use during the early 1900s, with initial industrial development occurring in the early 1940s. The unavailability of the 1910 and 1920 Sanborn maps is therefore considered to represent a minor data gap that does not materially affect the findings of this assessment.

**Building Department Records.** Records were requested from the City of Dayton Building Department for available years. Records were available from 1987 to present. Building department records prior to 1987 were not available from the City of Dayton. Permits and records for the period 1987 to present were reviewed and are summarized in Section 5.2.4. The available records cover the period of Valtec\'s ownership and operations at the property and document renovations and new construction during that period.

**Aerial Photograph Gap.** Historical aerial photographs were available for the years 1940, 1951, 1962, 1971, 1980, 1994, 2005, and 2018. No aerial photographs were available for the period between 1980 and 1994. Other historical sources, including Sanborn maps (1970 edition), city directories, and building department records beginning in 1987, provide reasonable coverage for this period and mitigate the significance of this gap.

**General Limitations.** The assessment is based on readily observable conditions and reasonably ascertainable information. Subsurface conditions, concealed features, and conditions on portions of the property that were not accessible or visible during the site reconnaissance may not be reflected in this report. The assessment is further limited by the scope of the applicable ASTM standard and the terms of the professional services agreement between Terravista and the Client.

**[3.0 SITE DESCRIPTION]{.underline}**

**3.1 Location and Legal Description**

The Subject Property is located at 2850 Millrace Road, Dayton, Ohio 45414, in Montgomery County. The site is identified in the Montgomery County Auditor\'s records under Tax Parcel Identification Number R72-04410-0025. The total area of the parcel is approximately 14.7 acres (approximately 640,000 square feet). The Ohio EPA Facility Identification Number assigned to the property is 0912345678.

The Subject Property is situated in an industrial and commercial corridor along Millrace Road, approximately 4.5 miles north-northwest of downtown Dayton. The property is served by public water and sanitary sewer provided by the City of Dayton and the Montgomery County Water Reclamation District, respectively. The site is zoned for heavy industrial use (M-2) under the City of Dayton zoning ordinance.

The property is roughly rectangular in configuration, with approximately 850 feet of frontage along Millrace Road to the east. The property extends approximately 750 feet to the west from the Millrace Road right-of-way. Vehicular access is provided by two gated driveways from Millrace Road. The site location is depicted on Figure 1 (Site Location Map) and Figure 2 (Site Plan), which are provided in Appendix A.

**3.2 Site and Vicinity Characteristics**

The Subject Property is situated on relatively flat terrain with a slight southwesterly slope of approximately two percent grade across the site. Surface grades fall generally from the northeastern corner to the southwestern corner, consistent with regional topography in the Great Miami River valley. Stormwater from the site drains via sheet flow and engineered stormwater inlets in a generally southwesterly direction toward an unnamed tributary of the Great Miami River, which is located approximately 650 feet southwest of the southwestern property boundary.

The surrounding land use is predominantly industrial and commercial in character. Immediately to the east, across Millrace Road, is the Triton Metals Processing Inc. facility at 2900 Millrace Road, an industrial metals processing operation. To the south of the Subject Property lie commercial and light industrial properties along Commerce Park Drive. Industrial properties are situated to the west along Millrace Road, including metal fabrication and distribution warehouses. To the north, the area consists of mixed industrial uses and vacant land, with several undeveloped parcels observable from the Subject Property. The nearest residential development is located approximately 0.6 miles to the northeast.

Regional topography is controlled by the Great Miami River valley and its associated buried valley aquifer system. The Great Miami River is located approximately 1.2 miles to the west-southwest of the Subject Property. Elevation at the site is approximately 770 feet above mean sea level.

**3.3 Current Use of the Property**

The Subject Property is currently owned and operated by Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc. (Valtec), which has occupied the property since 1987. Valtec operates a stamped metal parts manufacturing facility, with ancillary electroplating, powder coating, and warehouse operations. The facility employs approximately 165 workers across two shifts (day and afternoon). Current operations are conducted within five industrial and administrative buildings, described as follows:

**Building 1 (Main Manufacturing).** Building 1 is the primary manufacturing facility, encompassing approximately 82,000 square feet. The building was originally constructed in 1942 by the Dayton Forge & Die Company and was substantially renovated in 1990 by Valtec. Current operations include high-volume metal stamping using progressive die presses, secondary forming operations, and parts finishing. Raw materials include steel and aluminum sheet and coil stock. Metalworking fluids, including water-soluble cutting oils and stamping lubricants, are used in the manufacturing process. Building 1 is equipped with overhead bridge cranes and extensive machine tool infrastructure.

**Building 2 (Electroplating/Finishing).** Building 2 houses the facility\'s electroplating and metal finishing operations, encompassing approximately 34,500 square feet. The building was constructed in 1955 and renovated in 2003. Active operations include a chromium electroplating line utilizing hexavalent chromium chemistry and a separate nickel plating line. Chemical storage within Building 2 includes acids (sulfuric, hydrochloric, and chromic acid), alkaline cleaners, nickel sulfate solutions, and various proprietary plating additives. The electroplating lines are served by an on-site industrial wastewater pretreatment system that provides pH adjustment, hexavalent chromium reduction, metals precipitation, and clarification prior to discharge to the City of Dayton municipal POTW under NPDES pretreatment permit number 2PB00417.

**Building 3 (Warehouse).** Building 3, encompassing approximately 28,000 square feet and constructed in 1960, is currently used for general warehouse storage of finished goods, raw materials, and packaging supplies. Historically, this building was used by Central Valley Chemical Co. for chemical blending operations (approximately 1975 to 1981). Under Valtec\'s ownership, the building has been used exclusively for non-chemical warehouse purposes. No hazardous materials are currently stored in Building 3.

**Building 4 (Powder Coating).** Building 4, approximately 22,500 square feet, was constructed by Valtec in 1995 specifically for powder coating operations. The building houses a multi-stage wash system, a dry-off oven, an automated powder coating booth, and a curing oven. Powder coating materials consist of thermosetting polymer powders applied electrostatically to metal parts. No liquid solvents are used in the powder coating process.

**Building 5 (Office/Administration).** Building 5, approximately 20,000 square feet, was constructed in 1968 and renovated in 2012. The building serves as the facility\'s administrative headquarters, housing executive offices, engineering, accounting, human resources, and a small employee cafeteria. No manufacturing or chemical storage activities occur in Building 5.

The combined building footprint is approximately 187,000 square feet. Remaining site area is occupied by asphalt-paved parking lots (approximately 120 parking spaces), concrete loading docks, paved truck courts, and landscaped and unpaved peripheral areas.

Valtec is classified as a Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA) Small Quantity Generator (SQG) under EPA Identification Number OHD098712345. Valtec holds NPDES pretreatment permit number 2PB00417 for the discharge of treated industrial wastewater to the municipal POTW. Hazardous waste generated at the facility includes spent plating solutions, wastewater treatment sludge (F006), and miscellaneous solvent-contaminated rags and wipes. Hazardous waste is stored in the designated satellite accumulation areas and the central 90-day accumulation area near Building 2 prior to off-site shipment by licensed hazardous waste transporters.

**3.4 Descriptions of Structures and Improvements**

The five buildings on the Subject Property are of varying ages and construction types, reflecting the incremental development of the property from the 1940s through the 1990s. A summary of building characteristics is provided below.

**Building 1** is a single-story steel-frame structure with steel siding and a built-up asphalt roof. The building rests on a reinforced concrete slab-on-grade foundation. The original 1942 construction utilized riveted steel-frame construction consistent with wartime industrial building practices. The 1990 renovation included replacement of exterior wall cladding, re-roofing, upgraded electrical and mechanical systems, and installation of a new concrete floor slab in approximately 60 percent of the building footprint. A mezzanine office area of approximately 3,500 square feet is located at the southeastern corner. Building eave height is approximately 28 feet. Three overhead roll-up doors provide equipment and material access.

**Building 2** is a single-story steel-frame structure with concrete block perimeter walls and a flat built-up roof. The building rests on a reinforced concrete slab-on-grade foundation with integral containment trenches and sumps for the plating lines. The 2003 renovation included upgrades to the chemical containment systems, ventilation, and the wastewater pretreatment system. Building eave height is approximately 22 feet. The concrete floor features acid-resistant epoxy coatings in the plating areas, though the coating shows wear in several locations. Floor drains are present throughout the plating area.

**Building 3** is a single-story pre-engineered metal building with a low-slope metal roof and a concrete slab-on-grade foundation. The building was constructed in 1960 and has not undergone significant renovation. The concrete floor exhibits multiple cracks and patches, and approximately three inches of differential settlement was observed at the northeast corner. Two loading docks are located on the north side of the building.

**Building 4** is a single-story pre-engineered metal building with insulated metal wall panels and a standing-seam metal roof, constructed in 1995. The building is in generally good condition, consistent with its age. The concrete floor is in good condition with no significant cracking or staining observed. One drive-through overhead door and two standard overhead doors provide access.

**Building 5** is a two-story conventional wood and steel-frame structure with brick veneer exterior walls and a flat commercial roof. The 2012 renovation modernized the interior, including new HVAC systems, finishes, and Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) accessibility improvements.

Site improvements include approximately 3.5 acres of asphalt-paved parking and drive areas, concrete loading docks at Buildings 1, 2, and 3, a six-foot-high chain-link perimeter security fence with barbed wire top, exterior security lighting, and above-grade utility connections. The former UST excavation areas south of Building 1 have been backfilled and paved with asphalt. Landscaped areas along the Millrace Road frontage include mown grass and ornamental plantings.

**3.5 Current Uses of Adjoining Properties**

The properties immediately adjoining the Subject Property are used as follows:

**East (across Millrace Road).** The property at 2900 Millrace Road is occupied by Triton Metals Processing Inc., an industrial metals processing facility. The Triton facility appears to include multiple industrial buildings and outdoor storage areas. Triton\'s operations were not investigated in detail as part of this assessment; the facility is discussed further in the context of the environmental database review in Section 5.1.1.

**South.** Properties along Commerce Park Drive to the south are occupied by commercial and light industrial uses, including a commercial printing company, a plumbing supply distributor, and an electrical contracting firm. These properties appear to be of generally newer construction (estimated 1980s--1990s) and are separated from the Subject Property by a paved access drive and a narrow vegetated buffer.

**West.** Industrial properties to the west along Millrace Road include metal fabrication shops, a building materials distributor, and a truck maintenance yard. These properties are separated from the western boundary of the Subject Property by a gravel road and intermittent vegetation.

**North.** The area to the north of the Subject Property includes mixed industrial uses and undeveloped parcels. A paved but lightly trafficked access road separates the Subject Property from the northern properties. At least two parcels to the north appear to be vacant and unimproved.

**Subject Property Listings**

The Subject Property (2850 Millrace Road) was identified in the following databases:

*RCRA Small Quantity Generator (SQG).* Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc. is listed as a RCRA Small Quantity Generator under EPA Identification Number OHD098712345. The listing reflects Valtec\'s current operations and its generation of hazardous waste, including spent plating solutions and wastewater treatment sludge (EPA waste code F006). No violations were identified in the database listing. This is a routine regulatory classification consistent with the facility\'s current operations and does not, in itself, constitute a recognized environmental condition.

*Ohio EPA Spills and Releases Database.* The Subject Property is listed in connection with a 2002 release of approximately 150 gallons of hydraulic oil to soil adjacent to Building 1. The release was reported to Ohio EPA and the City of Dayton Fire Department. According to the database entry and information provided by the facility manager, the release was from a ruptured hydraulic line on a metal stamping press. The spill was contained on-site and remediated through excavation and off-site disposal of approximately 15 cubic yards of impacted soil. No further action was required by Ohio EPA. This release is considered a de minimis condition and is further discussed in Section 8.4.

*Ohio EPA UST Database.* The Subject Property is listed in connection with three underground storage tanks: two 10,000-gallon steel diesel underground storage tanks (installed approximately 1965) and one 4,000-gallon steel waste oil underground storage tank (installed approximately 1972). All three tanks are reported as removed in September 2004. The UST closure is further discussed in Section 5.2.5 (Previous Environmental Reports).

**Adjoining and Nearby Property Listings**

The following properties within the standard ASTM search radii were identified in the environmental databases as warranting discussion:

*Triton Metals Processing Inc., 2900 Millrace Road (immediately east, across Millrace Road).* The Triton Metals facility is listed in the following databases:

> • **CERCLIS/SEMS:** Triton Metals Processing Inc. is listed as a preliminary assessment (PA) site under EPA Identification Number OHD000812456. The database listing indicates a preliminary assessment was completed; no further federal action documented.
>
> • **RCRA Large Quantity Generator (LQG):** Triton Metals is listed as a RCRA Large Quantity Generator. No RCRA enforcement actions were identified in the database listing.

*Former Dry Cleaner, 145 Commerce Park Drive (approximately 0.3 miles south-southeast of the Subject Property).* A former dry cleaning operation at 145 Commerce Park Drive is listed on the Ohio EPA Master Sites List as a confirmed release site with a tetrachloroethylene (PCE) groundwater contamination plume. The site status is listed as \"Under Review\" as of January 2023. According to the database listing, the release was confirmed in 2004, and a remedial investigation has been conducted, though remediation is ongoing. The former dry cleaner is located approximately 0.3 miles south-southeast of the Subject Property, in a direction that is hydraulically downgradient based on reported groundwater flow to the south-southwest. Based on the downgradient position of the dry cleaner relative to the Subject Property, this listing is not expected to represent a recognized environmental condition for the Subject Property.

*Former Gasoline Station, 2780 Millrace Road (approximately 0.2 miles northwest of the Subject Property).* A former retail gasoline station at 2780 Millrace Road is listed in the Ohio EPA LUST database. According to the database, a petroleum release was confirmed in 2009, remediation was completed, and a No Further Action letter was issued by Ohio EPA Bureau of Underground Storage Tank Regulations (BUSTR) in February 2016. The former gasoline station is located approximately 0.2 miles northwest of the Subject Property and is in a hydraulically upgradient position relative to the site based on regional groundwater flow direction (south-southwest). Given the closed status of the LUST case, the distance from the Subject Property, and the absence of any evidence of off-site migration toward the Subject Property, this listing is not considered a recognized environmental condition for the Subject Property. This listing is further addressed in Section 8.4 as a de minimis condition.

No other listings within the standard ASTM search radii were identified that, in the professional judgment of the Environmental Professional, represent recognized environmental conditions for the Subject Property. A complete listing of all database results is provided in the GeoSearch report in Appendix B. The Environmental Database Search Map is provided as Figure 5 in Appendix A.

**5.1.2 Additional Environmental Record Sources.** In addition to the GeoSearch environmental database report, Terravista reviewed the following additional environmental record sources:

*Ohio EPA Online Databases.* Terravista accessed the Ohio EPA Division of Environmental Response and Revitalization (DERR) online facility database, the Ohio EPA e-Check system, and the Ohio EPA Division of Air Pollution Control permit database. The DERR facility listing for the Subject Property (Facility ID 0912345678) confirmed the RCRA SQG status and the UST/LUST history. No active enforcement actions, consent orders, or director\'s final findings and orders were identified in the online records. The Division of Air Pollution Control records indicate that Valtec holds an active permit-to-install and operate (PTIO) for the chromium electroplating line and the powder coating operation.

*Ohio EPA File Review Request.* A formal file review request was submitted to the Ohio EPA Southwest District Office on March 10, 2025, seeking available facility files for the Subject Property (2850 Millrace Road) and the adjacent Triton Metals Processing facility (2900 Millrace Road). A copy of the request letter and confirmation of submission is provided in Appendix G. As of the report date (April 18, 2025), the requested records had not been received from Ohio EPA. This report will be supplemented upon receipt of the requested records. The environmental database search, historical records review, and other sources consulted as part of this assessment are considered to provide a reasonable basis for the findings and opinions contained herein, notwithstanding the pending Ohio EPA file review.

*Montgomery County Auditor Records.* Terravista reviewed Montgomery County Auditor online property records for the Subject Property and adjoining parcels. The records confirmed the ownership, parcel boundaries, and assessed valuations consistent with information provided by the User.

**5.2 Historical Use Information**

**5.2.1 Sanborn Fire Insurance Maps.** Terravista reviewed Sanborn Fire Insurance Maps for the Subject Property and surrounding area from the collections of the Cincinnati Public Library and the Ohio History Connection. Maps were available for the following years: 1927, 1942, 1950, 1960, and 1970. Maps for the years 1910 and 1920 were not available for the Subject Property area, as discussed in Section 2.4 (Limitations and Exceptions). The Sanborn map findings are summarized below, and representative map excerpts are provided in Appendix C and Figure 3.

**1927 Sanborn Map.** The 1927 Sanborn map coverage does not extend to the Subject Property area. The nearest mapped area is approximately 0.5 miles to the south along Commerce Park Drive (then known as Mill Street). The Subject Property area is outside the mapped limits and is not depicted. Based on available aerial photographs (earliest 1940), the site was likely undeveloped and in agricultural use at this time.

**1942 Sanborn Map.** The 1942 Sanborn map shows the western approximately 6 acres of the Subject Property as developed with industrial buildings. The buildings are annotated as belonging to the \"Dayton Forge & Die Co.\" and include labels for \"machine shop,\" \"forge building,\" and \"pattern storage.\" Of particular note, three above-ground storage tanks are depicted in the western yard area, with the annotation \"oil storage.\" The tanks are shown as cylindrical features with estimated diameters ranging from approximately 10 to 15 feet. The eastern portion of the Subject Property is shown as undeveloped land with no annotations. Adjoining properties to the east (the location of the present-day Triton Metals facility) are shown as partially developed with small industrial structures.

**1950 Sanborn Map.** The 1950 Sanborn map shows a configuration substantially similar to the 1942 map. The Dayton Forge & Die Co. operations continue to be depicted on the western portion of the Subject Property, with the same building footprints and tank annotations. No significant changes are apparent. The eastern portion of the property remains undeveloped.

**1960 Sanborn Map.** The 1960 Sanborn map shows significant expansion of development across the full Subject Property. The western portion continues to show the Dayton Forge & Die Co. buildings. The eastern portion now shows additional industrial buildings, including a structure in the northeastern area annotated as \"industrial chemicals storage.\" This building corresponds to the approximate location of the present-day Building 3. The map depicts the property as part of a larger industrial complex, with multiple buildings and paved areas. The three ASTs in the western yard area are no longer depicted on this edition of the map, suggesting they may have been removed by this time.

**1970 Sanborn Map.** The 1970 Sanborn map shows the Subject Property fully built out and labeled as \"Millrace Industrial Park.\" Tenant annotations include \"auto parts reconditioning\" (in the approximate location of Building 2), \"chemical mfg.\" (in the approximate location of Building 3), and \"stamping\" (in the approximate location of Building 1). The \"chemical mfg.\" annotation at Building 3 is consistent with city directory listings for Central Valley Chemical Co. at this address during the 1970s.

The absence of Sanborn maps for 1910 and 1920 is noted as a minor data gap. Based on the 1940 aerial photograph (Section 5.2.2) and the 1927 Sanborn map coverage, the Subject Property was likely in agricultural use through the late 1930s, with initial industrial development occurring in the early 1940s coinciding with wartime manufacturing expansion. The unavailability of the 1910 and 1920 maps is not considered to materially affect the findings of this assessment.

**5.2.2 Historical Aerial Photographs.** Terravista reviewed historical aerial photographs for the Subject Property and surrounding area from the following sources: University of Dayton Aerial Photograph Collection, USGS Earth Explorer archive, Ohio Department of Transportation aerial photograph library, and Google Earth Pro historical imagery. Photographs were available for the following years: 1940, 1951, 1962, 1971, 1980, 1994, 2005, and 2018. No photographs were available for the years between 1980 and 1994. The findings are summarized below, and a compilation of representative aerial photographs is provided in Appendix D and Figure 4.

**1940.** The earliest available aerial photograph shows the Subject Property area as predominantly wooded and agricultural. A cleared area is visible on the western portion of the property, with what appear to be early-stage construction or grading activities. No permanent structures are clearly identifiable, though the western clearing is consistent with the initial development by Dayton Forge & Die Company described in other historical sources. Surrounding properties show scattered agricultural parcels with emerging industrial development along the Millrace Road corridor.

**1951.** The 1951 aerial photograph shows industrial development on the western approximately 6 acres of the Subject Property. At least two large building footprints are visible, consistent with the Dayton Forge & Die Company operations shown on the 1942 Sanborn map. Above-ground features in the western yard area are visible and appear consistent with storage tank installations. The eastern portion of the property is partially cleared but does not show structures. Surrounding industrial development along Millrace Road has expanded from the 1940 conditions.

**1962.** The 1962 aerial photograph shows the full Subject Property under industrial development. Multiple building footprints are visible across the site, consistent with the Sanborn map depictions. The property appears to be fully enclosed by fencing. Paved and unpaved areas are discernible. The above-ground tank features visible in the 1951 photograph are no longer apparent in the western yard area, consistent with the 1960 Sanborn map observation that the ASTs had been removed by that time.

**1971.** The 1971 aerial photograph is of particular note. The photograph shows the site in a configuration generally consistent with the 1962 photograph, with the full complement of industrial buildings. However, outdoor drum storage is clearly visible on the north side of Building 3, in an area of approximately 40 feet by 80 feet. An estimated 100 or more drums are visible in tightly arranged rows on what appears to be an unpaved surface. This observation is significant because it corresponds to the period during which Central Valley Chemical Co. was operating a chemical blending facility in Building 3 (city directory listings place Central Valley Chemical at this address from approximately 1975 to 1981, but the 1971 photograph suggests drum storage operations may have commenced earlier). No secondary containment or cover structures are apparent in the photograph.

**1980.** The 1980 aerial photograph shows the Subject Property in a configuration generally similar to the 1971 photograph. However, the outdoor drum storage area on the north side of Building 3 has been cleared --- no drums are visible. The area appears to have been graded or paved. No other significant changes from the 1971 conditions are apparent.

**1994.** The 1994 photograph (the first available after the 14-year gap) shows the Subject Property in its current general configuration. Building 4 (Powder Coating, constructed 1995) is not yet present; its future location is visible as an open paved area east of Building 1. The overall site layout, including building footprints and paved areas, is consistent with current conditions.

**2005.** The 2005 aerial photograph shows the Subject Property with Building 4 now present. The site configuration is essentially the same as the current conditions. No significant changes or areas of concern are apparent.

**2018.** The 2018 aerial photograph shows the site in its current configuration, consistent with the conditions observed during the April 2, 2025, site reconnaissance.

The gap between 1980 and 1994 is acknowledged as a data gap. However, other historical sources --- including Sanborn maps (1970 edition), city directories, and City of Dayton building department records (beginning in 1987) --- provide reasonable coverage for this period and indicate a transition from multi-tenant industrial park use to Valtec\'s single-owner operations beginning in 1987.

**5.2.3 City Directory Review.** Terravista reviewed Polk city directories and Haines reverse directories for the Dayton metropolitan area for selected years from 1940 through 2020. The directories were accessed through the Dayton Metro Library Local History Collection. Directory listings for the Subject Property address (2850 Millrace Road and variants) are summarized below:

> • **1942--1967:** Dayton Forge & Die Company, listed at 2850 Millrace Road. Described as \"forge work, die making, machine shop work.\"
>
> • **1968--1986:** Millrace Industrial Park, listed at 2850 Millrace Road. Multiple tenant listings, including:
>
> • Central Valley Chemical Co. (approximately 1975--1981), described as \"industrial chemicals, cleaning compounds.\"
>
> • Buckeye Auto Parts Reconditioning (approximately 1971--1983), described as \"auto parts rebuilding.\"
>
> • Various short-term tenants including a pallet manufacturing company and a tool and die shop (specific dates vary).
>
> • **1987--2020:** Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc., listed at 2850 Millrace Road. Described as \"metal stamping, plating, finishing.\"

The city directory review is generally consistent with the Sanborn map, aerial photograph, and title records discussed elsewhere in this report. The listing of Central Valley Chemical Co. from approximately 1975 to 1981 is significant in connection with the historical use of Building 3, as discussed in Sections 5.2.1 and 8.3.2.

**5.2.4 Building Department Records.** Records were requested from the City of Dayton Building Department for available years. Records were available from 1987 to present. Building permits and inspection records for the Subject Property for the period 1987 to present were reviewed and are summarized below:

> • **1990:** Building permit issued for interior renovation of Building 1 (Main Manufacturing), including new concrete floor slab, electrical upgrades, and structural modifications. Permit No. BD-1990-08247.
>
> • **1995:** Building permit issued for new construction of Building 4 (Powder Coating), approximately 22,500 square feet. Permit No. BD-1995-03618. Occupancy certificate issued January 1996.
>
> • **2003:** Building permit issued for renovation of Building 2 (Electroplating/Finishing), including upgrades to chemical containment systems, ventilation, and wastewater pretreatment system. Permit No. BD-2003-11054.
>
