No building permits related to underground or above-ground storage tank installations or removals were identified in the 1987-to-present records, although the three USTs were removed in 2004 (the removal may have been conducted under Ohio EPA BUSTR authority rather than a local building permit). No permits related to environmental remediation activities were identified.

Building department records prior to 1987 were not available from the City of Dayton. The available records cover the period of Valtec\'s ownership and document all major construction and renovation activities at the property during that period.

**5.2.5 Previous Environmental Reports.** The following previous environmental reports were provided by the User and reviewed by Terravista as part of this assessment. Copies of these reports are included in Appendix F.

**UST Closure Report (Redland Environmental Services, November 12, 2004)**

Redland Environmental Services prepared a UST Closure Report dated November 12, 2004, documenting the removal of three underground storage tanks from the Subject Property in September 2004. The tank removal was performed in the southern portion of the site, south of Building 1, in the area now covered by asphalt paving. The three tanks and their closure details are summarized below:

*Tank 1: 10,000-gallon steel diesel UST.* This tank was installed in approximately 1965. Upon excavation, the tank exhibited moderate external corrosion. Approximately two inches of sludge material was present in the tank bottom. Soil samples were collected from the tank excavation at four cardinal compass points at the tank base and one sample at the excavation bottom center. Analytical results for diesel range organics (DRO) ranged from non-detect to 340 mg/kg. The highest DRO concentration of 340 mg/kg was below the Ohio EPA BUSTR action level of 500 mg/kg for commercial/industrial land use. No further action was recommended for the Tank 1 excavation.

*Tank 2: 10,000-gallon steel diesel UST.* This tank was also installed in approximately 1965. Upon excavation, the tank exhibited heavy external corrosion with several visible holes in the tank shell, indicating a potential historical release. Initial soil samples collected at the tank base revealed DRO concentrations of up to 1,250 mg/kg, exceeding the Ohio EPA BUSTR action level of 500 mg/kg. Redland performed over-excavation of approximately 85 cubic yards of petroleum-impacted soil, which was transported to a licensed disposal facility. Confirmation soil samples collected following over-excavation indicated DRO concentrations of 280 mg/kg, below the applicable action level. Groundwater was not encountered during the excavation. No further action was recommended for the Tank 2 excavation following the over-excavation.

*Tank 3: 4,000-gallon steel waste oil UST.* This tank was installed in approximately 1972. Tank 3 was removed during the same mobilization. The closure report documents the physical removal of Tank 3; however, analytical results for soil samples collected from the Tank 3 excavation were not included in the report. A notation in the report margin indicates laboratory results were pending at the time of report issuance. The physical condition of Tank 3 upon removal was described as moderate corrosion with no visible holes. Approximately one inch of residual sludge was noted in the tank bottom.

The closure report was prepared by Redland Environmental Services under the supervision of Mark Trent, P.E. The report states that all three tanks were properly decommissioned, removed, and disposed of in accordance with Ohio EPA BUSTR regulations. Following the tank removals, Valtec indicated that Ohio EPA issued closure letters for all three tanks in 2005. Documentation of the closure letters was provided for Tank 1 and Tank 2 (the two 10,000-gallon diesel USTs). Documentation of the closure letter for Tank 3 was not provided.

**Ohio EPA Voluntary Action Program NFA Letter (Apex Environmental Solutions, September 22, 2011)**

A No Further Action (NFA) letter dated September 22, 2011, was issued by Certified Professional (CP) Jonathan Wertz of Apex Environmental Solutions under the Ohio EPA Voluntary Action Program (VAP). The NFA letter covers a 2.3-acre area designated as \"Parcel A\" in the southern portion of the Subject Property. The NFA letter describes the subject area as the \"former diesel underground storage tank area and associated petroleum-impacted soils.\"

According to the NFA letter, a Phase II Environmental Site Assessment was conducted by Apex Environmental Solutions in 2009--2010, including soil borings, monitoring well installations, soil sampling, and groundwater sampling within the 2.3-acre Parcel A area. The investigation evaluated residual petroleum impacts from the former diesel UST operations. The NFA letter states that the results of the Phase II investigation and risk assessment demonstrated that residual contamination within Parcel A meets the Ohio EPA generic numerical cleanup standards applicable to commercial/industrial land use, as specified in Ohio Administrative Code Chapter 3745-300.

The NFA letter was issued by a Certified Professional under the authority of Ohio Revised Code Chapter 3746 and Ohio Administrative Code Chapter 3745-300. As is standard under the Ohio VAP, the NFA letter was not subjected to independent Ohio EPA review or approval prior to issuance; however, the NFA letter was filed with Ohio EPA and is available through the VAP public record system. The NFA letter expressly states that it applies to the 2.3-acre Parcel A as depicted on the attached survey exhibit. The NFA letter does not address environmental conditions in areas of the Subject Property outside the Parcel A boundary.

A copy of the NFA letter is provided in Appendix F. The Parcel A boundary is depicted on Figure 7 in Appendix A.

**5.3 Hydrogeologic Setting**

The Subject Property is located within the Great Miami River buried valley aquifer system, which is one of the largest and most productive glacial aquifer systems in the state of Ohio. The buried valley system consists of glacial outwash sand and gravel deposits that fill a pre-glacial bedrock valley. The aquifer system underlies much of the Miami Valley region and provides the primary source of public and private water supply for the Dayton metropolitan area.

Based on United States Geological Survey (USGS) topographic maps, regional hydrogeological reports, and Ohio Department of Natural Resources (ODNR) publications, groundwater in the vicinity of the Subject Property flows generally to the south-southwest, consistent with the regional topographic gradient toward the Great Miami River. Depth to groundwater at the Subject Property is estimated at approximately 15 to 25 feet below the ground surface, based on regional water level data and information provided in the 2004 UST Closure Report (which noted that groundwater was not encountered during UST excavation activities at depths of approximately 12 to 15 feet below ground surface). The precise depth to groundwater may vary seasonally and in response to local pumping conditions.

The Subject Property is not located within a designated sole-source aquifer area. The site is within the service area of the City of Dayton public water supply system, which is sourced from the Great Miami Buried Valley Aquifer through a system of wellfields located primarily to the south and southwest of the city. The nearest active Dayton public water supply wells are located approximately 4 miles south of the Subject Property. Municipal water supply is provided to the Subject Property and surrounding industrial/commercial properties by the City of Dayton.

The subsurface geology beneath the site consists of glacial outwash deposits, predominantly sand and gravel, overlying Ordovician-age limestone and shale bedrock. The glacial deposits in this area are typically 80 to 120 feet thick. The surficial soils are classified as the Eldean series (well-drained soils formed in loamy alluvium over sand and gravel), according to the USDA Natural Resources Conservation Service (NRCS) Web Soil Survey. These soils are characterized by moderate permeability and are generally capable of transmitting infiltrating water to the underlying glacial aquifer.

The nearest surface water body is an unnamed tributary of the Great Miami River, located approximately 650 feet southwest of the Subject Property. The Great Miami River itself is located approximately 1.2 miles to the west-southwest.

**[6.0 SITE RECONNAISSANCE]{.underline}**

**6.1 Methodology and Conditions**

A site reconnaissance of the Subject Property was conducted on April 2, 2025, by Rachel Ostrander, P.G., Project Manager and Environmental Professional with Terravista Environmental Consulting Inc. Ms. Ostrander was accompanied during the site visit by Gary Hinton, Facility Manager for Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc. The site visit commenced at approximately 9:00 a.m. and concluded at approximately 1:30 p.m. Weather conditions during the visit were partly cloudy with temperatures ranging from approximately 50 to 58 degrees Fahrenheit. No precipitation occurred during the site visit, though light rainfall had been reported in the area two days prior. Access was provided to all five buildings and to all exterior areas of the Subject Property, including the perimeter areas, loading docks, parking lots, and the western yard area.

Observations were documented through written field notes and digital photographs. A photographic log with photograph locations keyed to the site plan is provided as Figure 6 in Appendix A, and the photographs are reproduced in Appendix E.

**6.2 General Site Conditions**

The Subject Property presents as an active, generally well-maintained industrial facility. The site consists of approximately 14.7 acres developed with five industrial and administrative buildings totaling approximately 187,000 square feet, together with asphalt-paved parking and drive areas, concrete loading docks, landscaped frontage, and perimeter chain-link security fencing. The buildings, paved areas, and site improvements are consistent in character and condition with the property\'s use as an operating manufacturing facility.

At the time of the site visit, normal manufacturing operations were underway in Buildings 1, 2, and 4, and warehouse activities were ongoing in Building 3. Administrative operations were being conducted in Building 5. Truck traffic was observed at the loading docks for Buildings 1 and 3. No unusual odors, other than those associated with the electroplating operations in Building 2, were noted in the outdoor areas.

**6.3 Exterior Observations**

**6.3.1 Western Yard Area.** An area of stressed and absent vegetation was observed in the western portion of the Subject Property, approximately 150 feet west of Building 1 and approximately 75 feet east of the western property boundary. The affected area was roughly rectangular, measuring approximately 40 feet by 60 feet (approximately 2,400 square feet). Within this area, ground cover vegetation (grass and weeds) was sparse to absent, and the exposed soil appeared noticeably darker in color than the surrounding soils. The darkened soil had a reddish-brown to black discoloration. No liquid sheen, odor, or free product was observed in the area at the time of the visit.

This area of stressed vegetation and darkened soil corresponds to the approximate location of the three above-ground storage tanks depicted on the 1942 Sanborn Fire Insurance Map, which were annotated as \"oil storage\" for the Dayton Forge & Die Company. The 1960 Sanborn map does not depict these tanks, suggesting they were removed between 1950 and 1960. The physical evidence observed during the site reconnaissance --- stressed/absent vegetation and darkened soil --- is consistent with the historical presence of petroleum storage tanks and suggests a potential release of petroleum products to soil. This area is not currently paved, covered, or otherwise improved.

**6.3.2 Former UST Area.** The former underground storage tank area is located in the southern portion of the Subject Property, immediately south of Building 1. Gary Hinton identified the general locations of the three former USTs, which were removed in September 2004 as described in the Redland Environmental Services UST Closure Report. The former excavation areas have been backfilled, compacted, and paved with asphalt. The asphalt surface appeared to be in fair condition, with minor cracking consistent with age and normal wear. No staining, odors, stressed vegetation, or other evidence of environmental impact was observed in the vicinity of the former UST locations. This area falls within the 2.3-acre Parcel A that is covered by the 2011 Ohio EPA VAP NFA letter.

**6.3.3 Northwest Property Boundary.** During the perimeter walk conducted as part of the site reconnaissance, a partially buried 55-gallon drum was observed near the northwest property boundary, approximately 30 feet from the perimeter fence line. The drum was oriented vertically and was protruding approximately 6 inches above the ground surface, with the remainder of the drum buried in the soil. The exposed portion of the drum exhibited significant corrosion, including surface rust and pitting, and no visible label, markings, or identification numbers were discernible. No lid or bung was visible above ground. The soil immediately surrounding the drum did not exhibit obvious discoloration or staining, and no odor was detected. Vegetation in the immediate vicinity of the drum appeared similar to surrounding ground cover.

Gary Hinton stated that he was unaware of the origin of the drum and had not previously observed it. The area is located in an unpaved, semi-vegetated portion of the property near the northwest boundary that does not receive regular foot or vehicle traffic.

**6.3.4 Building 3 Exterior.** Staining was observed on the exterior concrete loading dock on the north side of Building 3. The staining extended over an area of approximately 8 feet by 12 feet on the concrete dock surface. The staining appeared dark gray to black in color and, where wet from residual moisture, exhibited a faint petroleum-like sheen. The staining was concentrated in the area immediately adjacent to the loading dock edge and extended partially onto the concrete apron below the dock. This location corresponds to the area where the 1971 aerial photograph showed outdoor drum storage of an estimated 100 or more drums. Gary Hinton stated that the loading dock has been used for general warehouse loading activities under Valtec\'s operations and that the staining predates Valtec\'s acquisition of the property.

**6.3.5 Electrical Transformers.** Four pad-mounted electrical transformers were observed on the Subject Property during the site reconnaissance. Three of the transformers, located adjacent to Buildings 1, 4, and 5, were labeled with \"Non-PCB\" stickers applied by the utility company, Dayton Power & Grid Co. The non-PCB labels appeared to be in good condition and were clearly legible.

One transformer, located adjacent to the east side of Building 3, had no PCB label or marking of any kind. The transformer appeared to be of older construction than the other three units, with a weathered green-painted steel cabinet and visible surface corrosion on the base. The transformer appeared to be in service and connected to the building electrical system. No evidence of leaking or seepage was observed at any of the four transformer locations.

**6.3.6 Stormwater and Surface Water.** Stormwater from the Subject Property drains via sheet flow and engineered stormwater inlets in a generally southwesterly direction, consistent with the site\'s topographic gradient. Several catch basins and stormwater inlets were observed in the paved parking and drive areas. No standing water, unusual discharges, or evidence of contaminated stormwater runoff was observed during the site visit. The unnamed tributary of the Great Miami River is located approximately 650 feet southwest of the southwestern property boundary; this feature was not directly observed from within the Subject Property due to distance and intervening vegetation but is identified on topographic maps and aerial photographs.

**6.4 Interior Observations**

**6.4.1 Building 1 (Main Manufacturing).** Building 1 is the primary manufacturing building, encompassing approximately 82,000 square feet. At the time of the site visit, stamped metal parts manufacturing was actively underway, with multiple progressive die stamping presses in operation. The building interior included overhead bridge cranes, parts storage racking, raw materials staging areas, and ancillary workstations for secondary forming operations. Metalworking fluids (water-soluble cutting oils) were observed in use at the stamping presses, contained within integral machine sumps and drip trays.

The concrete floor appeared to be in reasonable condition, with typical wear consistent with heavy industrial use. No unusual or widespread staining was observed on the floor surface. A small area of oil staining (approximately 4 feet by 6 feet) was noted near a hydraulic power unit adjacent to one of the stamping presses; this staining appeared to be associated with routine equipment maintenance and was not extensive. The building was originally constructed in 1942 and renovated in 1990. The renovated portions of the floor (approximately 60 percent of the building area) were in visibly better condition than the unrenovated portions. No unusual odors beyond those typical for a metalworking facility were noted. The building ventilation system appeared to be operational.

**6.4.2 Building 2 (Electroplating/Finishing).** Building 2 houses the facility\'s chromium and nickel electroplating operations and encompasses approximately 34,500 square feet. The building was constructed in 1955 and renovated in 2003. At the time of the site visit, the chromium electroplating line was actively in operation. The electroplating area occupies the western approximately two-thirds of the building, with the eastern one-third used for parts preparation, rinsing, and finishing operations.

The chromium electroplating line consists of a series of process tanks, including alkaline cleaning, acid etching, chromium plating (utilizing hexavalent chromium chemistry), and multi-stage rinsing tanks. A separate nickel plating line was also observed, located along the south wall of the building. Both plating lines are equipped with local exhaust ventilation and mist suppressant systems. A strong chemical odor was noted in the electroplating area, consistent with the use of acid-based plating solutions. Ms. Ostrander observed that the ventilation system was operating during the visit.

A chemical storage area is located in the southeastern portion of Building 2. Approximately 40 drums of various sizes (30-gallon and 55-gallon) were observed, containing plating chemicals, acids (sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid, and chromic acid), alkaline cleaners, and proprietary plating solution additives. The drums were stored on wooden pallets on the concrete floor. A secondary containment berm was present around the perimeter of the drum storage area.

Floor drains were observed throughout the plating area. Gary Hinton stated that all floor drains in Building 2 are connected to the on-site industrial wastewater pretreatment system, which provides hexavalent chromium reduction, pH adjustment, metals precipitation, and clarification prior to discharge to the municipal POTW under NPDES pretreatment permit number 2PB00417. Hinton stated the pretreatment system was upgraded during the 2003 renovation.

Staining was observed on the concrete floor in the area near the drum storage and the western end of the chromium plating line. The staining covered an area of approximately 15 feet by 20 feet (approximately 300 square feet) and appeared dark in color, with greenish-blue and dark brown hues. The staining was heaviest in the area immediately between the drum storage pallets and the first chromium plating tank. Gary Hinton indicated the staining was the result of \"routine operational spillage\" associated with the transfer and handling of plating chemicals, and he stated that spills are cleaned promptly using absorbent materials and that the floor is cleaned on a regular basis. The acid-resistant epoxy coating on the concrete floor in the plating area showed visible wear and deterioration in several locations, including the area of the staining.

The industrial wastewater pretreatment system was observed in a treatment room in the northeastern corner of Building 2. The system includes equalization tanks, a chromium reduction reactor, pH adjustment tanks, a flocculation/precipitation tank, a plate-and-frame filter press for sludge dewatering, and a treated effluent discharge line. Sludge generated from the pretreatment system (EPA waste code F006) is accumulated in roll-off containers in the designated 90-day hazardous waste accumulation area adjacent to the treatment room.

**6.4.3 Building 3 (Former Chemical Blending, Now Warehouse).** Building 3 encompasses approximately 28,000 square feet and was constructed in 1960. The building is currently used for general warehouse storage of finished goods, packaging materials, and miscellaneous equipment and supplies. At the time of the site visit, the building interior was approximately 70 percent occupied by palletized storage. Hinton stated that no hazardous materials are currently stored in Building 3, and no chemical containers, drums, or other hazardous material storage were observed during the walkthrough.

The concrete floor in Building 3 exhibited multiple cracks and patches, particularly in the north-central and northeastern portions of the building. Approximately 3 inches of differential settlement was observed at the northeast corner, evidenced by a visible step in the floor slab. The floor cracks appeared to be of varying age, with some appearing to have been filled with cementitious patching material and others remaining unfilled. No active staining, odors, or evidence of chemical use was observed on the floor surface. Several floor patches of different colors and textures were noted, suggesting multiple repair episodes over the building\'s history.

The building\'s historical use by Central Valley Chemical Co. as a chemical blending facility from approximately 1975 to 1981 is documented through city directory listings and Sanborn map annotations, as discussed in Section 5.2. The physical condition of the floor (cracking, settlement, and multiple patches) is consistent with the building\'s age and historical industrial use.

**6.4.4 Building 4 (Powder Coating).** Building 4 encompasses approximately 22,500 square feet and was constructed in 1995. The building houses the facility\'s powder coating operations, including a multi-stage aqueous wash system, a dry-off oven, an automated electrostatic powder coating booth, and a curing oven. At the time of the site visit, the powder coating line was actively in operation.

The building interior appeared clean and well-maintained. Powder coating materials (thermosetting polymer powders) were stored in the original manufacturer\'s containers on shelving in a designated storage area. No liquid solvents or hazardous chemicals were observed in Building 4. The concrete floor was in good condition, with no significant cracking, staining, or deterioration observed. The building ventilation and dust collection systems appeared to be in good operating condition. No environmental concerns were noted during the Building 4 walkthrough.

**6.4.5 Building 5 (Office/Administration).** Building 5 encompasses approximately 20,000 square feet and serves as the facility\'s administrative headquarters. The building was constructed in 1968 and renovated in 2012. The building houses executive offices, engineering offices, accounting and human resources departments, conference rooms, and a small employee cafeteria. The 2012 renovation modernized the interior finishes, HVAC systems, and electrical systems. No manufacturing, chemical storage, or hazardous material handling activities occur in Building 5. No environmental concerns were observed during the walkthrough.

**[7.0 INTERVIEWS]{.underline}**

**7.1 Owner/Operator Interview**

An interview was conducted with Gary Hinton, Facility Manager for Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc., during the site reconnaissance on April 2, 2025. The interview was supplemented by a telephone follow-up call on April 8, 2025. Mr. Hinton has served as Facility Manager at the Subject Property since 2004 and has been employed by Valtec since 1998, initially in a maintenance supervisor role. Mr. Hinton\'s statements are summarized below.

Mr. Hinton confirmed that Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc. has owned and operated the Subject Property since 1987. Current operations at the facility include stamped metal parts manufacturing (Building 1), chromium and nickel electroplating and metal finishing (Building 2), general warehouse storage (Building 3), powder coating (Building 4), and administrative offices (Building 5). Valtec is the sole occupant of the property, and no portions of the site are leased to third parties.

Mr. Hinton confirmed Valtec\'s status as a RCRA Small Quantity Generator under EPA Identification Number OHD098712345. He stated that hazardous waste generated at the facility consists primarily of spent plating solutions, wastewater treatment sludge (F006), and miscellaneous solvent-contaminated wipes and absorbents. Hazardous waste is managed in accordance with applicable RCRA regulations and is shipped off-site for treatment and disposal by licensed hazardous waste transporters.

Regarding the industrial wastewater pretreatment system, Mr. Hinton stated that the system was originally installed by Valtec in the late 1980s and was upgraded during the 2003 renovation of Building 2. The system treats process wastewater from the electroplating lines and discharges treated effluent to the City of Dayton municipal POTW under NPDES pretreatment permit number 2PB00417. Hinton stated the facility has maintained compliance with its permit discharge limits.

Mr. Hinton confirmed that three underground storage tanks were removed from the southern portion of the property in September 2004 --- two 10,000-gallon diesel USTs and one 4,000-gallon waste oil UST. He stated that Ohio EPA issued closure letters for all three tanks in 2005. When asked to provide documentation of the closure letters, Mr. Hinton was able to locate closure letters for Tank 1 and Tank 2, the two diesel USTs. He stated that the closure letter for Tank 3 (the waste oil tank) could not be located but that he was \"confident\" it was issued along with the other two.

Regarding the 2002 hydraulic oil release, Mr. Hinton stated that approximately 150 gallons of hydraulic oil was released to soil adjacent to Building 1 from a ruptured hydraulic line on a stamping press. The spill was reported to Ohio EPA and the City of Dayton Fire Department. Approximately 15 cubic yards of impacted soil was excavated and disposed of off-site at a licensed facility. No further action was required by Ohio EPA.

Mr. Hinton stated he was aware that Building 3 was previously occupied by Central Valley Chemical Co., which he described as a \"chemical blending\" company. He stated he has no detailed knowledge of Central Valley\'s specific operations, the chemicals they handled, or any environmental issues associated with their operations. He stated that when Valtec acquired the property in 1987, Building 3 was vacant and was subsequently put into use for warehouse storage.

Regarding the partially buried 55-gallon drum observed near the northwest property boundary during the site reconnaissance, Mr. Hinton stated he was unaware of the drum\'s presence or origin. He speculated it may have been present since before Valtec\'s acquisition but could not confirm this.

Mr. Hinton stated that he is not aware of any pending or threatened environmental enforcement actions, notices of violation, citizen complaints, or regulatory inquiries related to the Subject Property, other than routine regulatory interactions associated with the facility\'s RCRA, NPDES, and air quality permits.

**7.2 Key Site Manager Interview**

Martin Schell, Chief Executive Officer of Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc., was contacted by telephone on April 10, 2025. Mr. Schell has served as CEO of Valtec since 2001 and has been with the company since 1992. Mr. Schell confirmed the general site history and operations as described by Mr. Hinton. He stated that Valtec acquired the property in 1987 from Millrace Industrial Park LLC as part of Valtec\'s establishment of its manufacturing operations.

Regarding the 2011 VAP NFA letter, Mr. Schell stated that Valtec obtained the NFA letter to \"resolve the UST issue\" and to provide environmental assurances to potential future purchasers. He confirmed that the NFA letter covers the 2.3-acre area south of Building 1 where the USTs were located. He described the Phase II investigation that supported the NFA as \"thorough\" and stated that the results demonstrated residual contamination was within acceptable limits for industrial use.

Mr. Schell stated he has no knowledge of environmental issues at the Subject Property beyond those previously disclosed, including the UST history, the 2002 oil spill, and the general awareness of the property\'s prior industrial use by Dayton Forge & Die and the various Millrace Industrial Park tenants. He stated Valtec has maintained environmental compliance throughout its tenure and has not been the subject of environmental enforcement actions or fines.

**7.3 Local Government Officials**

Terravista contacted the City of Dayton Fire Department, Fire Prevention Bureau, on March 20, 2025, to request records of hazardous materials incidents, complaints, or inspections at the Subject Property. The Fire Department indicated that their records identified one incident at the property --- the 2002 hydraulic oil release, which was responded to by the Fire Department and documented as a contained release with no off-site impacts. No other hazardous materials incidents were on file for the property.

Terravista also contacted the Montgomery County Combined Health District (Environmental Health Division) on March 25, 2025, to request records of complaints or inspections related to the Subject Property. The Health District indicated that no environmental complaints or inspection records related to the property were identified in their files. The Health District representative stated that hazardous materials and waste complaints are typically referred to Ohio EPA.

**7.4 Others Interviewed**

No additional interviews were conducted as part of this assessment. Terravista attempted to identify and contact representatives of former owners and operators of the Subject Property, including Dayton Forge & Die Company, Millrace Industrial Park LLC, and Central Valley Chemical Co. Based on available records, Dayton Forge & Die Company is believed to have been dissolved in the early 1970s, Millrace Industrial Park LLC was dissolved in 1992, and Central Valley Chemical Co. ceased operations at the Subject Property in approximately 1981 and is believed to be defunct. No current contact information for representatives of these former entities was identified through the title records, city directory review, Ohio Secretary of State business filing records, or internet searches conducted by Terravista. As a result, interviews with former owners and operators were not completed.

**[8.0 FINDINGS AND OPINIONS]{.underline}**

Based on the records review, historical use research, site reconnaissance, and interviews conducted as part of this Phase I Environmental Site Assessment, Terravista has developed the following findings and opinions regarding recognized environmental conditions, controlled recognized environmental conditions, historical recognized environmental conditions, and other environmental conditions at the Subject Property.

**8.1 Recognized Environmental Conditions (RECs)**

Two recognized environmental conditions were identified for the Subject Property, as described below.

**8.1.1 REC-1: Western Yard Area --- Former Above-Ground Storage Tanks.** An area of stressed and absent vegetation and darkened soil was observed in the western portion of the Subject Property during the April 2, 2025, site reconnaissance. The affected area measures approximately 40 feet by 60 feet and is located approximately 150 feet west of Building 1 and 75 feet east of the western property boundary. The darkened soil observed within this area is notably different in color and texture from the surrounding native soils, exhibiting a reddish-brown to black discoloration.

The location of this area of environmental concern corresponds to the former above-ground storage tank area associated with the Dayton Forge & Die Company, as depicted on the 1942 Sanborn Fire Insurance Map. The 1942 Sanborn map shows three cylindrical above-ground storage tanks in this area with the annotation \"oil storage.\" The 1950 Sanborn map shows a similar configuration. By 1960, the tanks are no longer depicted on the Sanborn maps, indicating they were removed between 1950 and 1960. The 1951 aerial photograph shows features consistent with above-ground tanks in this area; the 1962 aerial photograph does not.

The presence of stressed/absent vegetation and darkened soil at the location of former petroleum storage tanks is indicative of a potential release of petroleum products to soil from the former AST operations. This condition constitutes a recognized environmental condition as defined in the applicable ASTM standard. The observed physical evidence --- stressed vegetation over a defined area coinciding with historical petroleum storage --- suggests that petroleum products may have been released to the subsurface during the approximately 15-to-20-year period during which the ASTs were present and operational.

Terravista recommends that a Phase II Environmental Site Assessment be conducted for the western yard area, including the advancement of soil borings and the collection and analysis of soil samples to characterize the nature and extent of potential petroleum impacts from the former above-ground storage tank operations. Analytical testing should include petroleum hydrocarbon parameters appropriate for the suspected source materials, including diesel range organics (DRO) and gasoline range organics (GRO), with comparison to applicable Ohio EPA generic cleanup standards.

**8.1.2 REC-2: Building 2 --- Electroplating Operations and Floor Staining.** Staining was observed on the concrete floor in Building 2 near the chemical drum storage area during the April 2, 2025, site reconnaissance. The staining covers an area of approximately 15 feet by 20 feet (approximately 300 square feet) and is dark in color, with greenish-blue and dark brown hues. The stained area is located between the drum storage pallets and the western end of the chromium electroplating line. Building 2 has housed active chromium and nickel electroplating operations since approximately 1987, when Valtec commenced operations at the property. The building was constructed in 1955 and renovated in 2003.

The observed floor staining may be indicative of historical spills or releases of plating chemicals, including chromium-containing solutions, nickel solutions, and various acids, to the concrete floor surface. The facility manager, Gary Hinton, stated that the staining is the result of routine operational spillage associated with the transfer and handling of plating chemicals, and that spills are cleaned promptly using absorbent materials. Mr. Hinton further stated that floor drains in the plating area are connected to the on-site industrial wastewater pretreatment system, which discharges to the municipal POTW under NPDES pretreatment permit number 2PB00417, and that the pretreatment system was upgraded during the 2003 renovation.

Notwithstanding the operational controls described by the facility manager, the presence of visible floor staining in an area associated with active electroplating operations indicates that releases of plating chemicals have occurred at the floor surface. Given the nature of the chemicals involved in electroplating operations --- including hexavalent chromium, nickel compounds, and various acids --- and the potential for migration through the concrete slab over time, this condition represents a recognized environmental condition.

Terravista recommends that subsurface investigation be conducted in the vicinity of Building 2, including the collection and analysis of soil samples beneath and adjacent to the building footprint, to evaluate potential impacts from electroplating operations. Investigation should focus on the area of observed staining and the drum storage area.

**8.2 Controlled Recognized Environmental Conditions (CRECs)**

No controlled recognized environmental conditions were identified for the Subject Property. The 2011 VAP NFA letter for Parcel A (2.3 acres, southern portion) includes activity and use limitations restricting future use to commercial/industrial purposes; however, the User\'s planned use of the property (light industrial/warehouse redevelopment) is consistent with this restriction. As the planned use does not conflict with the applicable AULs, this condition does not meet the definition of a CREC under the applicable ASTM standard.

**8.3 Historical Recognized Environmental Conditions (HRECs)**

Two historical recognized environmental conditions were identified for the Subject Property, as described below.

**8.3.1 HREC-1: Former Underground Storage Tanks (Parcel A).** Three underground storage tanks --- two 10,000-gallon steel diesel USTs and one 4,000-gallon steel waste oil UST --- were removed from the southern portion of the Subject Property in September 2004. The UST closure was documented in a closure report prepared by Redland Environmental Services (November 12, 2004), which confirmed the physical removal of all three tanks and the over-excavation of petroleum-impacted soil from the Tank 2 excavation. Following the tank removals, a Phase II Environmental Site Assessment was conducted by Apex Environmental Solutions in 2009--2010, and a No Further Action (NFA) letter was issued under the Ohio EPA Voluntary Action Program (VAP) on September 22, 2011, by Certified Professional Jonathan Wertz.

The NFA letter covers a 2.3-acre area designated as Parcel A in the southern portion of the site, described as the \"former diesel underground storage tank area and associated petroleum-impacted soils.\" The NFA determination was based on a Phase II investigation and risk assessment demonstrating that residual petroleum contamination within Parcel A meets the Ohio EPA generic numerical cleanup standards applicable to commercial/industrial land use.

Based on the removal of the USTs, the documented closure activities, the subsequent Phase II investigation, and the issuance of the VAP NFA letter, this condition meets the definition of a historical recognized environmental condition --- i.e., a past release of hazardous substances or petroleum products that has been addressed to the satisfaction of the applicable regulatory authority, such that no further investigation or remediation is warranted. The NFA letter was issued in accordance with Ohio Revised Code Chapter 3746 and Ohio Administrative Code Chapter 3745-300 and remains in effect.

**8.3.2 HREC-2: Former Central Valley Chemical Co. Operations (Building 3 Area).** Central Valley Chemical Co. operated a chemical blending facility in Building 3 at the Subject Property from approximately 1975 to 1981, based on city directory listings and Sanborn map annotations. Historical records indicate that Central Valley Chemical handled industrial cleaning solvents and degreasers, including chlorinated solvents, during its operations. An Ohio EPA enforcement action was taken against Central Valley Chemical in 1981, and the company subsequently ceased operations at the Subject Property. The specific details and disposition of the enforcement action were not available for review as part of this assessment (Ohio EPA file records remain pending).

The 1971 aerial photograph shows outdoor drum storage of an estimated 100 or more drums on the north side of Building 3, in an area that corresponds to the present-day loading dock area where staining was observed during the site reconnaissance. The 1980 aerial photograph shows the drum storage area cleared. Physical observations in Building 3 during the site reconnaissance included multiple floor cracks and patches and approximately 3 inches of differential settlement at the northeast corner. Staining was observed on the exterior loading dock on the north side of Building 3, covering an area of approximately 8 feet by 12 feet.

The Subject Property received a No Further Action letter under the Ohio EPA Voluntary Action Program on September 22, 2011. The VAP NFA letter, issued by Certified Professional Jonathan Wertz of Apex Environmental Solutions, indicates that environmental conditions at the property have been investigated and that residual contamination meets applicable generic cleanup standards for commercial/industrial use. Based on the issuance of the NFA letter, this condition meets the ASTM definition of a historical recognized environmental condition --- i.e., a past release that has been addressed to the satisfaction of the applicable regulatory authority.

Central Valley Chemical Co.\'s operations involved the handling and storage of chlorinated solvents and industrial degreasers, which are regulated as hazardous substances. The outdoor drum storage practices observed in the 1971 aerial photograph, the Ohio EPA enforcement action in 1981, and the physical evidence of floor deterioration and exterior staining are all indicators of a historical release. However, the VAP NFA letter provides a regulatory determination that the environmental conditions at the property have been investigated and meet applicable cleanup standards for commercial/industrial use. The NFA letter was issued by a Certified Professional under the authority of the Ohio VAP, which is recognized as a valid mechanism for addressing environmental conditions at contaminated properties in Ohio. Accordingly, this condition is classified as a historical recognized environmental condition (HREC) based on the regulatory closure provided by the 2011 NFA letter.

**8.4 De Minimis Conditions**

The following conditions were identified during the assessment but are considered de minimis conditions that do not rise to the level of recognized environmental conditions:

**2002 Hydraulic Oil Release.** Approximately 150 gallons of hydraulic oil was released to soil adjacent to Building 1 in 2002 from a ruptured hydraulic line on a stamping press. The release was reported to Ohio EPA and the City of Dayton Fire Department, contained on-site, and remediated through excavation and off-site disposal of approximately 15 cubic yards of impacted soil. No further action was required by Ohio EPA. The release was small in volume, was promptly remediated, and no residual contamination is expected at concentrations exceeding applicable standards. This condition is considered de minimis.

**RCRA Small Quantity Generator Status.** Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc. is classified as a RCRA Small Quantity Generator under EPA Identification Number OHD098712345. This is a routine regulatory classification reflecting the facility\'s generation of hazardous waste in the normal course of its manufacturing and electroplating operations. No RCRA violations were identified in the environmental database search. The SQG classification, standing alone, does not constitute a recognized environmental condition.

**Former Gasoline Station at 2780 Millrace Road.** A former retail gasoline station at 2780 Millrace Road, located approximately 0.2 miles northwest of the Subject Property in a hydraulically upgradient position, is listed in the Ohio EPA LUST database with a confirmed petroleum release. Remediation was completed and a No Further Action letter was issued by Ohio EPA BUSTR in February 2016. Given the closed status of the LUST case, the distance from the Subject Property, and the upgradient position relative to groundwater flow, this listing is not considered a recognized environmental condition for the Subject Property.

**8.5 Data Gaps**

The following data gaps were identified during the assessment:

**Ohio EPA File Review Records.** A records request was submitted to Ohio EPA on March 10, 2025, seeking available facility files for the Subject Property (2850 Millrace Road) and the adjacent Triton Metals Processing Inc. facility (2900 Millrace Road). Records had not been received as of the report date (April 18, 2025). This represents a data gap; however, the environmental database search and other records reviewed as part of this assessment are considered to provide a reasonable basis for the findings and opinions contained herein. This report will be supplemented upon receipt of the requested records. The Ohio EPA records, when received, may provide additional information regarding the facility\'s regulatory history, the 1981 enforcement action against Central Valley Chemical Co., and the Triton Metals facility that could affect the findings of this assessment.

**Sanborn Fire Insurance Maps (1910, 1920).** Sanborn Fire Insurance Maps for the years 1910 and 1920 were not available for the Subject Property area from the sources consulted (Cincinnati Public Library and Ohio History Connection). Based on historical aerial photographs (1940) and the 1927 Sanborn map coverage, the Subject Property was likely in agricultural use during the 1910--1920 period, with initial industrial development occurring in the early 1940s. This data gap is not considered to materially affect the findings of this assessment.

**Aerial Photograph Gap (1980--1994).** No historical aerial photographs were available for the period between 1980 and 1994. Other historical sources, including the 1970 Sanborn map, city directories, and City of Dayton building department records (beginning in 1987), provide reasonable coverage for this period and indicate a transition from multi-tenant industrial park use to Valtec\'s single-owner operations beginning in 1987. This data gap is not considered to materially affect the findings of this assessment.

**8.6 Additional Observations (Non-ASTM Scope)**

The following observations are provided for informational purposes and relate to items that are outside the scope of the ASTM E1527-21 Phase I ESA standard. These items are not evaluated as part of the recognized environmental condition assessment and are not required components of all appropriate inquiries under 40 C.F.R. Part 312.

**Potential Asbestos-Containing Materials.** Building 1 (constructed 1942, renovated 1990) and Building 2 (constructed 1955, renovated 2003) are of an age where asbestos-containing building materials may have been incorporated during original construction. Suspect materials may include pipe insulation, floor tiles, roofing materials, and other building components. No asbestos survey or sampling was conducted as part of this Phase I ESA. The Client may wish to commission a separate asbestos survey if renovation, demolition, or other disturbance of suspect materials is anticipated.

**Potential Lead-Based Paint.** Buildings 1, 2, 3, and 5, all constructed prior to 1978, may contain lead-based paint on interior and exterior surfaces. No lead-based paint survey or sampling was conducted as part of this assessment. The Client may wish to commission a separate lead-based paint assessment if renovation, demolition, or other disturbance of painted surfaces is anticipated.

These non-scope items are noted for informational purposes only and are not included in the recognized environmental condition assessment.

Based on the findings of this assessment, two recognized environmental conditions (RECs) and two historical recognized environmental conditions (HRECs) have been identified for the Subject Property. No controlled recognized environmental conditions (CRECs) were identified. Three de minimis conditions were identified that do not rise to the level of recognized environmental conditions. Three data gaps were identified; none are considered to materially affect the overall findings of this assessment, although the pending Ohio EPA file review may yield additional information relevant to the findings.

**9.2 Recommendations.** Based on the findings and opinions presented in this Phase I ESA, Terravista recommends the following:

1\. **Phase II ESA --- Western Yard Area (REC-1).** A Phase II Environmental Site Assessment should be conducted for the western yard area to characterize the nature and extent of potential petroleum impacts from the former Dayton Forge & Die above-ground storage tanks. The investigation should include the advancement of soil borings and the collection of soil samples for laboratory analysis of petroleum hydrocarbon parameters, including diesel range organics (DRO) and gasoline range organics (GRO). Analytical results should be compared to applicable Ohio EPA generic cleanup standards for commercial/industrial use. Depending on the results, groundwater sampling may also be warranted to evaluate potential off-site migration.

2\. **Phase II ESA --- Building 2 (REC-2).** Subsurface investigation should be conducted in the vicinity of Building 2 to evaluate potential impacts from decades of electroplating operations. The investigation should include the collection of soil samples beneath and adjacent to the Building 2 footprint, with analytical testing for metals (including hexavalent chromium, total chromium, and nickel) and other parameters appropriate for the electroplating source materials. The investigation should focus on the area of observed floor staining and the drum storage area.

3\. **Ohio EPA File Review Supplement.** This report should be supplemented upon receipt of the Ohio EPA file review records requested on March 10, 2025. The supplemental review may yield additional information relevant to the findings and classifications contained herein, including information regarding the 1981 Ohio EPA enforcement action against Central Valley Chemical Co. and the regulatory history of the adjacent Triton Metals Processing Inc. facility.

4\. **Ongoing AAI Compliance.** The User should ensure compliance with continuing obligations under CERCLA, including reasonable steps to prevent future releases, cooperation with regulatory authorities, compliance with institutional controls, and appropriate care with respect to hazardous substances on the property.

**9.3 AAI Compliance Statement**

It is the opinion of the undersigned Environmental Professional that this Phase I Environmental Site Assessment constitutes All Appropriate Inquiries in accordance with 40 C.F.R. Part 312 and is sufficient to qualify the User for the protections afforded under the Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act of 1980 (CERCLA), including the bona fide prospective purchaser defense under 42 U.S.C. § 9607(r), subject to the User\'s compliance with continuing obligations as set forth in 42 U.S.C. § 9607(q). This opinion is based on the information available as of the report date and is subject to the limitations, exceptions, and data gaps identified in this report.

This Phase I Environmental Site Assessment was conducted in conformance with the scope and limitations of ASTM Standard Practice E1527-21, *Standard Practice for Environmental Site Assessments: Phase I Environmental Site Assessment Process*, and the United States Environmental Protection Agency\'s Standards and Practices for All Appropriate Inquiries, 40 C.F.R. Part 312.

**[1.0 Executive Summary]{.underline}**

This Phase I Environmental Site Assessment (ESA) was conducted by Greenfield Environmental Consultants Inc. (Greenfield) on behalf of Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC (the User) in connection with the proposed acquisition of the property located at 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Lucas County, Ohio 43611 (the Subject Property). This assessment was performed in conformance with ASTM Standard Practice E1527-21 and is intended to satisfy the All Appropriate Inquiries (AAI) requirements established under the Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (CERCLA) §101(35)(B) and codified at 40 C.F.R. Part 312. The report is authorized for reliance by Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC and Great Lakes Capital Bank.

**Property Summary.** The Subject Property consists of three contiguous parcels (Lucas County Parcel Nos. 18-04562-001, 18-04562-002, and 18-04562-003), totaling approximately 14.7 acres. The property is improved with the following structures: (1) a main manufacturing building totaling approximately 100,000 square feet, consisting of the original 78,000-square-foot structure constructed in 1962 and a 22,000-square-foot addition completed in 1978; (2) a warehouse building of approximately 35,000 square feet, constructed in 1985; and (3) a maintenance/utility building of approximately 6,500 square feet, constructed in 1972. Approximately 3.2 acres of the property are paved (parking, loading, and drive areas), and an approximately 0.8-acre stormwater detention basin is located at the northeast corner of the property.

**Current and Historical Use.** The Subject Property has been used solely for warehousing operations since December 2019, operated by Breckenridge Manufacturing Co. (Breckenridge), an Ohio corporation that has owned the property since 1961. Prior to December 2019, the property was used for metal stamping, vapor degreasing (using trichloroethylene, or TCE, from 1961 to 1992), electroplating operations involving hexavalent chromium and nickel (from 1965 to 2004), and painting. Prior to 1961, the property was in agricultural use (row crops) with a single residential farmhouse owned by the Halstead family.

**Findings.** Based on the records review, site reconnaissance, interviews, and evaluation of findings performed in accordance with ASTM E1527-21, Greenfield identified the following environmental conditions:

**Recognized Environmental Condition No. 1 --- Former TCE Degreasing Operations and Abandoned Waste TCE Drum.** Trichloroethylene was used as a degreasing solvent in the northwest portion of the main manufacturing building from 1961 through approximately 1992. A photoionization detector (PID) reading of 42.3 ppm was recorded at a crack in the floor slab of the former degreasing area, indicating residual volatile organic compound vapors. No closure documentation was located for the degreasing area following the cessation of TCE use. An abandoned 55-gallon drum labeled \"WASTE TRICHLOROETHYLENE\" was observed behind the maintenance building, approximately half full, with a rusted bottom and evidence of past leakage onto the surrounding soil. A 1988 chemical spill involving degreasing solvent was documented in Toledo Fire Department records. These conditions indicate a potential release of chlorinated solvents to soil and groundwater in the northwest portion of the main building and behind the maintenance building, and constitute a recognized environmental condition (REC).

**Recognized Environmental Condition No. 2 --- Former Electroplating Operations (Hexavalent Chromium).** Electroplating operations involving hexavalent chromium and nickel compounds were conducted from 1965 to 2004 in the south wing of the main manufacturing building. Visible blue-green staining on concrete floors and walls is consistent with chromium residues. An active in-ground sump in the former electroplating room contains approximately four inches of liquid with a visible iridescent sheen. Hazardous waste disposal documentation was available only for the final year of operations (2004): six manifests documenting the disposal of 14,200 pounds of waste under RCRA codes D007 (chromium) and F006 (electroplating sludge). No manifests or disposal records were produced for any of the preceding 39 years of operation (1965--2003). A 2003 Ohio EPA inspection documented improper hazardous waste storage, resulting in a Notice of Violation. The absence of disposal documentation for nearly four decades, the visible contamination, and the liquid waste in the sump indicate a potential release of chromium and related metals. This constitutes a REC.

**Historical Recognized Environmental Condition No. 1 --- Halstead Farmhouse 500-Gallon Heating Oil UST.** Historical records indicate that a 500-gallon heating oil underground storage tank (UST) served the Halstead farmhouse in the northwest portion of the property. The farmhouse was demolished in 1961. The tank was likely removed when the farmhouse was demolished in 1961, and this condition is historical in nature. This constitutes a historical recognized environmental condition (HREC).

**De Minimis Condition No. 1 --- Norfolk Southern Railroad Herbicide Application.** Historical herbicide application along the Norfolk Southern Railroad right-of-way west of the Subject Property is a typical railroad maintenance practice and is unlikely to affect the Subject Property. This condition is characterized as de minimis.

**Data Gaps.** Certain data gaps were identified during this assessment and are discussed in Section 8.0 of this report. The identified data gaps are not expected to significantly affect the findings and conclusions of this assessment.

**Recommendations.** A Phase II ESA is recommended to further assess the conditions underlying REC No. 1 and REC No. 2. The Phase II investigation should include subsurface soil and groundwater sampling in the vicinity of the former degreasing area, the abandoned TCE drum, and the former electroplating room. Characterization sampling of the sump liquid in the former electroplating room is also recommended.

**[2.0 Introduction]{.underline}**

**2.1 Purpose.** This Phase I Environmental Site Assessment was performed in accordance with the ASTM International Standard Practice E1527-21, *Standard Practice for Environmental Site Assessments: Phase I Environmental Site Assessment Process*, on behalf of Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC (the User). The purpose of this assessment is to identify recognized environmental conditions (RECs), controlled recognized environmental conditions (CRECs), historical recognized environmental conditions (HRECs), and de minimis conditions in connection with the Subject Property located at 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Lucas County, Ohio 43611.

As defined in ASTM E1527-21 §3.2.78, a **recognized environmental condition** (REC) is the presence or likely presence of any hazardous substances or petroleum products in, on, or at a property: (1) due to a release to the environment; (2) under conditions indicative of a release to the environment; or (3) under conditions that pose a material threat of a future release to the environment. The term is not intended to include de minimis conditions that generally do not present a material risk of harm to public health or the environment and that generally would not be the subject of an enforcement action if brought to the attention of appropriate governmental agencies.

A **controlled recognized environmental condition** (CREC) is a recognized environmental condition resulting from a past release of hazardous substances or petroleum products that has been addressed to the satisfaction of the applicable regulatory authority, with hazardous substances or petroleum products allowed to remain in place subject to the implementation of required controls.

A **historical recognized environmental condition** (HREC) is a past release of any hazardous substances or petroleum products that has occurred in connection with the property and has been addressed to the satisfaction of the applicable regulatory authority or meets unrestricted use criteria established by a regulatory authority, without subjecting the property to any required controls.

This assessment was conducted in order to satisfy the All Appropriate Inquiries (AAI) rule established by the United States Environmental Protection Agency at 40 C.F.R. Part 312, thereby providing the User with protections from CERCLA liability under the innocent landowner defense, the contiguous property owner defense, and the bona fide prospective purchaser defense, as set forth in CERCLA §101(35)(B), §107(q), and §107(r), respectively.

**2.2 Scope of Services**

The scope of this Phase I ESA follows the requirements of ASTM E1527-21 and encompasses the following components:

> • **Records Review:** A review of standard federal, state, tribal, and local environmental record sources, as defined in ASTM E1527-21 §8.2, to evaluate environmental conditions at and in the vicinity of the Subject Property. The regulatory database review was conducted by Environmental Data Resources, Inc. (EDR) and is documented in an EDR report dated September 15, 2024, included as Appendix A.
>
> • **Historical Sources Review:** A review of historical use information, including Sanborn fire insurance maps, aerial photographs, city directories, and other reasonably ascertainable historical sources, to evaluate the historical uses of the Subject Property and adjoining properties.
>
> • **Site Reconnaissance:** A visual and physical inspection of the Subject Property and its immediate environs to observe current conditions and identify evidence of recognized environmental conditions. The site reconnaissance was conducted on October 2, 2024, by Jason Mulvaney, Staff Scientist, Greenfield Environmental Consultants Inc.
>
> • **Interviews:** Interviews with current and past owners, operators, occupants, and local government officials, as well as others with knowledge of the property, to obtain information relevant to the identification of recognized environmental conditions.
>
> • **Evaluation and Report Preparation:** Evaluation of all information gathered during the above components, and preparation of this report documenting findings and conclusions.

No non-scope services were performed as part of this assessment. Specifically, no environmental sampling or quantitative analysis was conducted, no asbestos or lead-based paint surveys were performed, no wetlands delineation was completed, and no regulatory compliance audit was undertaken. No radon testing or mold assessment was included. The User did not request any non-scope considerations under ASTM E1527-21 §13.

**2.3 Limitations and Exceptions**

This Phase I ESA is a qualitative assessment based on review of reasonably ascertainable records, visual observations during the site reconnaissance, and information obtained through interviews. No sampling, testing, or quantitative analysis of environmental media (soil, groundwater, surface water, soil vapor, or building materials) was performed as part of this assessment. The findings and opinions expressed herein are based on information available at the time of the assessment and are subject to the inherent limitations of professional judgment and the availability and quality of records and information reviewed.

Greenfield Environmental Consultants Inc. maintains professional liability insurance with a policy limit of \$5,000,000 per claim and in the aggregate, underwritten by Pinnacle Specialty Insurance Group, Policy No. PSI-ENV-2024-08827. A certificate of insurance is available upon request.

In accordance with ASTM E1527-21 §4.6, this report is considered valid for a period of 180 days from the date of the site reconnaissance (October 2, 2024), that is, through and including March 31, 2025. If a transaction is consummated after this 180-day period, an update of this Phase I ESA may be required to maintain the protection afforded by the AAI rule.

This report has been prepared solely for the use of Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC (the User) and Great Lakes Capital Bank (the authorized reliance party). No other party may rely upon this report without the prior written consent of Greenfield Environmental Consultants Inc.

**2.4 Special Terms and Conditions**

No deviations from the standard scope of ASTM E1527-21 were identified or implemented in connection with this assessment. The User did not request any non-scope considerations or additional services beyond those specified in the standard practice.

**[3.0 Site Description]{.underline}**

**3.1 Location and Legal Description**

The Subject Property is located at 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Lucas County, Ohio 43611. The property is situated within the Lakeshore Industrial Drive corridor, an established industrial and commercial area in the northern portion of Toledo, approximately 2.5 miles south of the Lake Erie shoreline and approximately 6 miles northeast of downtown Toledo. The property consists of three contiguous parcels identified by the Lucas County Auditor as follows:

> • Parcel No. 18-04562-001
>
> • Parcel No. 18-04562-002
>
> • Parcel No. 18-04562-003

The total area of the three parcels is approximately 14.7 acres. The parcels are generally rectangular in configuration and are bounded by Lakeshore Industrial Drive to the north, adjacent industrial properties to the east, commercial properties to the south, and the Norfolk Southern Railroad right-of-way to the west. A site location map is included as Figure 1, and a site layout plan is included as Figure 2.

**3.2 Site and Vicinity Description**

The Subject Property is improved with three principal structures:

1\. **Main Manufacturing Building:** Approximately 100,000 square feet total, consisting of the original 78,000-square-foot building constructed in 1962 and a 22,000-square-foot addition completed in 1978. The building is a single-story steel-frame structure with a concrete slab-on-grade foundation and metal panel exterior walls. The building historically housed metal stamping, vapor degreasing, electroplating, and painting operations. Since December 2019, the building has been used solely for warehousing.

2\. **Warehouse Building:** Approximately 35,000 square feet, constructed in 1985. The building is a pre-engineered metal building on a concrete slab-on-grade foundation. The building is in active use for warehousing and storage of palletized goods.

3\. **Maintenance/Utility Building:** Approximately 6,500 square feet, constructed in 1972. The building houses mechanical and HVAC equipment, electrical distribution panels, and a small workshop area used for maintenance of facility equipment.

Approximately 3.2 acres of the property are covered by asphalt and concrete paving, including parking areas, loading docks, and drive lanes. A stormwater detention basin of approximately 0.8 acres is located in the northeast corner of the property. The remaining areas of the property consist primarily of unmaintained grass and gravel surfaces.

The property is currently owned and operated by Breckenridge Manufacturing Co., an Ohio corporation formed in 1961. Gerald Breckenridge III serves as President. Since December 2019, the property has been used exclusively for warehousing. No active manufacturing operations are conducted on the property.

**3.3 Current Use of Adjoining Properties**

The following describes the current uses of properties immediately adjoining the Subject Property:

**North (4900 Lakeshore Industrial Drive):** The property to the north is occupied by the former Lake Erie Dry Cleaning facility. The dry cleaning operation was conducted from approximately 1972 through 2011. At the time of the site reconnaissance, the building appeared vacant and secured. No current operations were observed. The property is separated from the Subject Property by Lakeshore Industrial Drive and a narrow grassed buffer, with the nearest building approximately 120 feet from the Subject Property boundary.

**East (Vacant Industrial Lot):** The property to the east is a vacant lot, formerly occupied by Toledo Coatings & Adhesives Inc., which operated at this location from approximately 1958 through 1997. The former structures have been demolished, and the lot is largely unvegetated with scattered debris and remnant building foundations. The property is separated from the Subject Property by a chain-link fence.

**South (Lakeshore Freight Services):** The property to the south is an active commercial trucking terminal operated by Lakeshore Freight Services. The facility includes a dispatch office, truck maintenance garage, and a paved staging/loading yard. No known environmental issues are associated with this property.

**West (Norfolk Southern Railroad):** The western boundary of the Subject Property is adjacent to the Norfolk Southern Railroad right-of-way, which consists of an active rail corridor with a single set of tracks on a ballasted roadbed. The right-of-way extends approximately 100 feet in width.

An adjoining properties map is included as Figure 3.

**3.4 Physical Setting**

The Subject Property is located within the Lake Erie coastal lowlands physiographic region. The topography of the site and surrounding area is generally flat, with a gentle slope from north-northwest to south-southeast. Surface elevations at the Subject Property range from approximately 586 feet above mean sea level (AMSL) at the northern boundary to approximately 583 feet AMSL at the southern boundary.

The subsurface geology in the vicinity of the Subject Property is characterized by glacial deposits overlying Silurian-age limestone bedrock. The glacial deposits consist predominantly of glacial till (a heterogeneous mixture of clay, silt, sand, and gravel), with localized sand and gravel lenses. The depth to bedrock in this area is typically 15 to 40 feet below ground surface.

Shallow groundwater is present in the sand and gravel deposits within the glacial till. Based on regional hydrogeologic data and available well logs, the depth to the water table is estimated at approximately 8 to 15 feet below ground surface. The direction of shallow groundwater flow in the vicinity of the Subject Property is generally to the south-southeast, consistent with the regional topographic gradient toward Maumee Bay and Lake Erie. A groundwater flow direction map is included as Figure 4.

The property is served by the City of Toledo municipal water supply. No potable water wells are known to exist on the Subject Property. The nearest surface water body is an unnamed drainage ditch located approximately 500 feet south of the Subject Property, which is tributary to Maumee Bay and ultimately to Lake Erie.

**[5.0 Records Review]{.underline}**

**5.1 Standard Environmental Record Sources**

**5.1.1 Federal and State Database Review.** A regulatory environmental database search was conducted by Environmental Data Resources, Inc. (EDR) and is documented in the EDR Database Report dated September 15, 2024, included as Appendix A. The database search covered the Subject Property and surrounding properties within the ASTM E1527-21 specified approximate minimum search distances. The following summarizes the findings relevant to this assessment.

**Subject Property Listings:**

The Subject Property at 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive was identified in the following databases:

> • **RCRA --- Small Quantity Generator (SQG):** The Subject Property is listed as a RCRA Small Quantity Generator under EPA ID No. OHD044891237. This generator status is consistent with the property\'s historical use for metal stamping and finishing operations that generated hazardous waste, including chromium-bearing electroplating wastes and spent solvents. The SQG designation alone does not constitute a REC, as it reflects a regulatory status rather than evidence of a release.
>
> • **Ohio Underground Storage Tank (UST) Database:** The Subject Property is listed as having one registered underground storage tank --- a 10,000-gallon diesel fuel UST, Ohio Facility ID 48-22-10-0087. The tank was installed in 1978 and was upgraded with cathodic corrosion protection in 1998. The most recent compliance inspection was conducted in May 2022 by the Lucas County Health Department, and no violations were noted. The UST is properly registered and maintained in accordance with applicable Ohio regulations.

The Subject Property was **not** identified in the following databases: CERCLIS/SEMS (Superfund Enterprise Management System), National Priorities List (NPL), Ohio Master Sites List, CORRACTS (Corrective Action Report), ERNS (Emergency Response Notification System), or any other federal or state enforcement or remediation database.

**Adjacent and Nearby Property Listings:**

The following adjacent and nearby properties were identified in the regulatory databases within the ASTM-specified search distances:

> • **Lake Erie Dry Cleaning, 4900 Lakeshore Industrial Drive (North, approximately 120 feet from the Subject Property boundary):** This property is listed on the Ohio EPA Master Sites List as a confirmed release site, VAP Identification No. NW-0923. The property is also listed in the SEMS database. According to the database records and supporting Ohio EPA documentation, a documented tetrachloroethylene (PCE) groundwater contamination plume has been identified at this site. A 2014 investigation report associated with the VAP enrollment identified PCE at a concentration of 87 micrograms per liter (µg/L) at monitoring well MW-7, which is located approximately 120 feet north of the Subject Property boundary. The applicable Ohio groundwater standard for PCE is 5 µg/L; the detected concentration exceeds the standard by more than 17 times. The documented plume migration direction is to the south-southeast, which is consistent with the regional groundwater flow direction described in Section 3.4. A Covenant Not to Sue was issued in 2018 under the Ohio VAP for the dry cleaner parcel only; the covenant does not address off-site contamination. The Subject Property is located within a designated groundwater management zone established by Ohio EPA in connection with this PCE plume.
>
> • **Toledo Coatings & Adhesives Inc. (East, adjacent):** This property is listed in the CERCLIS/SEMS archive under EPA ID No. OHD987654321. The property was the subject of a preliminary assessment conducted by EPA; however, EPA determined that no further action was warranted, and the site was archived in 1999. No documented contamination plume is identified as migrating from this property toward the Subject Property based on available records.
>
> • **Lakeshore Freight Services (South, adjacent):** No listings were identified for this property in any of the searched federal, state, or local environmental databases.
>
> • **Norfolk Southern Railroad (West, adjacent right-of-way):** No listings specific to the railroad right-of-way adjacent to the Subject Property were identified in the searched databases.

A summary of the environmental database findings is presented in Table 1 and detailed in the full EDR report included as Appendix A.
