**5.3 Historical Use Summary --- Subject Property**

Based on the records review, the historical use of the Subject Property is summarized chronologically as follows:

**1948--1960 (Agricultural / Residential):** The property was used for agricultural purposes (row crops) by the Halstead family. A single-family farmhouse was located in the northwest portion of the property, served by a 500-gallon heating oil underground storage tank.

**1961 (Transition):** The Halstead farmhouse was demolished, and the property was acquired by Breckenridge Manufacturing Co. Construction of the main manufacturing building commenced.

**1961--1992 (Manufacturing --- with TCE Degreasing):** The property was used for metal stamping operations, supported by vapor degreasing using trichloroethylene (TCE). Degreasing operations were conducted in the northwest corner of the main manufacturing building.

**1965--2004 (Electroplating):** Electroplating operations using hexavalent chromium and nickel compounds were conducted in the south wing of the main manufacturing building. Various hazardous wastes were generated, including chromium-bearing sludges, spent plating solutions, and wastewater.

**1972:** The maintenance/utility building was constructed on the south side of the property.

**1978:** The 22,000-square-foot addition to the main manufacturing building was completed. A 10,000-gallon diesel fuel UST was installed near the maintenance building.

**1985:** The 35,000-square-foot warehouse building was constructed on the east portion of the property.

**1992:** Breckenridge Manufacturing ceased use of TCE for degreasing and switched to aqueous cleaning methods. TCE drums were reportedly removed. No closure documentation was located for the degreasing area, and no soil or groundwater confirmation samples are known to have been collected.

**2003:** Ohio EPA conducted a compliance inspection and documented improper hazardous waste storage, resulting in a Notice of Violation (File No. NW-2003-0471) and a consent order requiring a facility-wide waste audit.

**2004:** Electroplating operations ceased. Plating tanks were drained, and electroplating sludge was disposed of by Tri-County Environmental Hauling Inc., documented by six hazardous waste manifests totaling 14,200 pounds under RCRA codes D007 (chromium) and F006 (electroplating sludge). No disposal manifests were produced for any prior year of electroplating operations (1965--2003).

**2019--Present:** Manufacturing operations ceased in December 2019. The property has been used solely for warehousing.

A summary of the historical use timeline is presented in Table 2.

**5.4 Historical Use Summary --- Adjoining Properties**

**North --- Lake Erie Dry Cleaning (4900 Lakeshore Industrial Drive):** The property to the north was operated as a dry cleaning facility from approximately 1972 through 2011. Tetrachloroethylene (PCE) was used as the primary dry cleaning solvent. A documented PCE groundwater contamination plume has been identified at this site, with concentrations significantly exceeding Ohio groundwater standards. The facility enrolled in the Ohio Voluntary Action Program (VAP), and a Covenant Not to Sue was issued in 2018 for the dry cleaner parcel only. An environmental covenant restricts groundwater use and requires maintenance of a vapor mitigation system on the dry cleaner parcel.

**East --- Toledo Coatings & Adhesives Inc.:** The property to the east was operated as a coatings and adhesives manufacturing facility from approximately 1958 through 1997. The property was the subject of a preliminary assessment under CERCLIS; EPA determined no further action was warranted, and the site was archived in 1999.

**South --- Lakeshore Freight Services:** The property to the south has been used as a commercial trucking terminal since approximately 2005. No historical environmental concerns were identified in the records review.

**West --- Norfolk Southern Railroad:** The western boundary of the Subject Property is adjacent to an active Norfolk Southern Railroad right-of-way. Railroads commonly apply herbicides along rights-of-way for vegetation management. No specific environmental records were identified for the railroad right-of-way adjacent to the Subject Property.

**[6.0 Site Reconnaissance]{.underline}**

**6.1 Methodology and Conditions**

A site reconnaissance of the Subject Property was conducted on October 2, 2024, by Jason Mulvaney, Staff Scientist, Greenfield Environmental Consultants Inc. The reconnaissance was performed under the supervision and direction of Lauren Haddad, P.E., Project Manager. The weather conditions at the time of the site visit were clear, with an ambient temperature of approximately 58°F. No precipitation occurred on the day of the visit or during the preceding 48 hours.

The site reconnaissance was conducted in the presence of Gerald Breckenridge III, President of Breckenridge Manufacturing Co., who accompanied Mr. Mulvaney throughout the inspection. Mr. Breckenridge provided full interior and exterior access to all buildings and the property perimeter. No areas of the property were restricted or inaccessible during the reconnaissance.

A MiniRAE 3000 photoionization detector (PID) was used during the reconnaissance for screening of volatile organic compound (VOC) vapors in areas of concern. Photographic documentation was obtained throughout the site visit. Representative photographs are described in Appendix D.

**6.2 Exterior Observations**

**6.2.1 General Site Conditions.** The Subject Property was observed to be in generally fair condition, consistent with a property that has transitioned from active manufacturing to warehouse use. The paved parking and loading areas (approximately 3.2 acres of asphalt and concrete surfaces) exhibited typical wear, including minor cracking, patching, and surface deterioration. No unusual staining, discoloration, or evidence of spills or releases was observed on paved surfaces during the exterior reconnaissance.

The stormwater detention basin in the northeast corner of the property appeared functional. The basin contained a shallow depth of water at the time of the visit. No unusual staining, sheen, odors, discoloration, or other evidence of contamination was observed at or around the detention basin.

Unmaintained grass and gravel areas on the south and east sides of the property were generally consistent with properties not actively landscaped. No evidence of polychlorinated biphenyl (PCB)-containing equipment, such as electrical transformers with PCB labels or markings, was observed exterior to the buildings. Overhead electrical service is provided from a utility pole at the eastern property boundary; the pole-mounted transformer is owned and maintained by Toledo Edison and did not display PCB warning labels.

**6.2.2 Underground Storage Tanks --- Exterior. 10,000-Gallon Diesel UST (Ohio Facility ID 48-22-10-0087).** The fill port, vent pipe, and fuel dispenser associated with the registered 10,000-gallon diesel fuel UST were observed adjacent to the maintenance/utility building. The fill port was properly capped and locked. The vent pipe appeared to be in good condition with no visible corrosion. The dispenser showed normal wear and was not in active use at the time of the visit but appeared functional. No evidence of staining, stressed vegetation, surface discoloration, or releases was observed in the vicinity of the UST fill port, vent, dispenser, or surrounding pavement. The tank is properly registered with the Ohio Bureau of Underground Storage Tank Regulations (BUSTR) and was last inspected in May 2022 with no violations noted.

**Former 500-Gallon Heating Oil UST (Halstead Farmhouse).** Based on the 1948 and 1955 Sanborn fire insurance maps, a 500-gallon heating oil UST was associated with the Halstead family farmhouse, which was located in the northwest portion of the property. The approximate former location of the farmhouse, as determined by comparison with the Sanborn maps, was visually inspected during the site reconnaissance. The area is currently an open, grassed area with no structures. No surface evidence of an underground storage tank was observed, including no fill pipes, vent pipes, or surface disturbance indicative of a tank installation or removal. No closure documentation, tank removal records, or regulatory sign-off was located during the records review for this tank.

**2,000-Gallon Waste Solvent UST (Per 1987 Sanborn Map).** The 1987 Sanborn fire insurance map depicts a 2,000-gallon waste solvent underground storage tank in the approximate area of the east side of the property, between the main manufacturing building and the eastern property boundary. The approximate area indicated on the Sanborn map was inspected during the site reconnaissance. No surface evidence of an underground storage tank was observed at this location, including no fill pipes, vent pipes, dispensers, or other surface appurtenances. The tank does not appear in the Ohio UST registration database maintained by BUSTR. No closure documentation or removal records were identified during the records review.

**6.2.3 Abandoned Waste TCE Drum.** A single 55-gallon drum was observed on the ground surface behind (south side of) the maintenance/utility building. The drum was labeled \"WASTE TRICHLOROETHYLENE\" on a partially degraded paper label affixed to the drum\'s side. The drum appeared to be approximately half full based on its weight and the sound produced when tapped. The bottom of the drum was significantly rusted, with visible corrosion and evidence of past leakage.

The soil surface beneath and immediately surrounding the drum exhibited staining over an area of approximately three feet by three feet. The staining appeared dark and oily in character, with a slightly lighter coloration at the margins. Vegetation was absent in the stained area.

When asked about the drum, Gerald Breckenridge III stated that the drum had been \"there for years\" and was \"probably from the early \'90s\" --- contemporaneous with the transition away from TCE-based degreasing. PID readings were not taken at the specific location of the drum during the site visit.

**6.2.4 Former Outdoor Drum Storage Area.** An area on the east side of the property, approximately 40 feet by 60 feet (approximately 2,400 square feet), was observed to be delineated by deteriorating concrete curbing, consistent with a former storage or staging area. The area is unpaved, with a surface consisting of compacted soil and sparse gravel. Vegetation within the curbed area was notably stressed, exhibiting yellowed and sparse growth compared to the surrounding unmaintained grass areas.

No drums, containers, or other hazardous material storage were present in the area at the time of the reconnaissance. However, the deteriorating curbing, the stressed vegetation pattern, and the location of the area --- consistent with the drum storage area visible in the 1990 and 2000 aerial photographs --- suggest that this area was historically used for outdoor storage of drums or other containers.

Gerald Breckenridge III, when asked about the area, was unable to provide specific details regarding what materials had been stored there. He acknowledged, \"We used to keep drums out there before the EPA came around.\" He did not elaborate further on the types of materials stored, the duration of storage, or any measures taken to address potential releases in the area.

**6.2.5 Monitoring Well (MW-1).** A monitoring well was observed near the northeast property boundary, approximately 30 feet inside the property line. The well was identified by a label on the protective casing as \"MW-1.\" The well consisted of a flush-mounted PVC well casing with a surface-mounted protective cover. The well cap was damaged, with a cracked PVC cover and loose bolting. The well appeared to be in a state of disrepair, with accumulated debris around the base.

No monitoring records, installation logs, well construction details, or associated analytical data were located during the records review for this well. Gerald Breckenridge III, when asked about the monitoring well, stated that he did not know who installed the well, when it was installed, or its purpose. He offered no further information regarding any monitoring activities that may have been conducted at the property.

**6.3 Interior Observations**

**6.3.1 Main Manufacturing Building --- Former Degreasing Area.** The former degreasing area is located in the northwest corner of the main manufacturing building. The area encompasses approximately 4,000 square feet and has been partially cleared of equipment. The concrete floor slab in this area exhibited visible staining, characterized by dark discoloration that appeared to penetrate into the concrete surface. Multiple cracks were observed in the floor slab, ranging from hairline fractures to cracks approximately one-quarter inch in width.

A photoionization detector (PID) reading of 42.3 parts per million (ppm) was recorded at a crack in the floor slab during the site reconnaissance. This reading is considered elevated and is indicative of volatile organic compound vapors emanating from beneath the floor slab. Background PID readings in other areas of the main building were generally less than 0.5 ppm. The elevated PID reading suggests the potential presence of residual chlorinated solvent contamination in the subsurface beneath the former degreasing area.

Former equipment footings and anchor bolts were visible in the floor, consistent with the mounting locations of degreasing equipment. The degreasing equipment was reportedly removed at an unknown date, which Breckenridge III indicated was approximately 1992 when the facility transitioned to aqueous cleaning methods. No closure documentation for the degreasing area was located during the records review, and no confirmation that the area was characterized or remediated following the removal of equipment was identified.

The interior walls adjacent to the former degreasing area exhibited staining up to approximately three feet above floor level. The staining was consistent with vapor condensation or splash residue from the former degreasing operations. The ventilation system in this area appeared to be non-functional, with disconnected ductwork and sealed exhaust ports.

**6.3.2 Main Manufacturing Building --- Former Electroplating Room.** The former electroplating room is located in the south wing of the main manufacturing building. The room encompasses approximately 8,000 square feet and has been partially repurposed for warehouse storage, with palletized goods stacked in portions of the room, particularly along the eastern wall.

The concrete floor and lower portions of the walls exhibited prominent blue-green staining, consistent with chromium residues from the former electroplating operations. The staining was most concentrated in the central and western portions of the room, where the plating tanks were historically located. The staining extended on the walls to a height of approximately four feet above floor level in some areas.

A drain in the center of the room connects to an in-ground concrete sump, approximately three feet by three feet and an estimated three feet deep. The sump contained approximately four inches of liquid at the time of the reconnaissance. The liquid surface exhibited a visible iridescent sheen, and the liquid appeared slightly discolored with a greenish tint. The source of the liquid was not determined during the site visit; it may represent accumulated condensation, infiltration, or residual process fluids. The liquid was not sampled during this Phase I ESA, consistent with the scope of ASTM E1527-21, which does not include sampling or quantitative analysis.

Former plating tank locations were identifiable by equipment mounting locations, staining patterns, and chemical degradation of the concrete floor. The concrete exhibited pitting, etching, and surface erosion consistent with prolonged exposure to acidic and alkaline plating solutions. Floor drains connecting to the sump were observed at multiple locations throughout the room.

The room\'s ventilation system was not operational at the time of the site visit. Exhaust ductwork associated with the former plating operations was still in place but appeared to be disconnected from any active air handling system.

**6.3.3 Warehouse Building.** The warehouse building (35,000 square feet, constructed 1985) was observed to be in good condition and in active use for warehousing and storage of palletized consumer products. The concrete floor was in good condition with no evidence of staining, cracking, or chemical degradation beyond normal wear. No odors or other evidence of releases were noted. No hazardous materials or chemicals were observed in storage within the warehouse building.

**6.3.4 Maintenance/Utility Building.** The maintenance/utility building (6,500 square feet, constructed 1972) houses mechanical and HVAC equipment, electrical distribution panels, and a small workshop area. Minor oil staining was observed near a floor drain in the workshop area, consistent with incidental hydraulic oil or lubricant drips associated with typical equipment maintenance activities. The staining was minor in extent and was characterized as incidental and de minimis.

No hazardous materials storage was observed inside the maintenance/utility building. It is noted that the abandoned 55-gallon drum labeled \"WASTE TRICHLOROETHYLENE\" is located outside behind (south side of) this building, as documented in Section 6.2.3.

**[7.0 Interviews]{.underline}**

**7.1 Interview with Property Owner --- Gerald Breckenridge III**

An interview with Gerald Breckenridge III, President of Breckenridge Manufacturing Co., was conducted on October 2, 2024, during the site reconnaissance. Mr. Breckenridge is a third-generation family member associated with the company and has been involved in the operations of Breckenridge Manufacturing since the mid-1980s. He is the current sole officer and primary point of contact for the property.

Mr. Breckenridge provided the following information during the interview:

**Operations History.** Mr. Breckenridge confirmed that operations at the Subject Property historically included metal stamping, vapor degreasing, electroplating, and painting. He stated that trichloroethylene (TCE) was used for degreasing from the commencement of operations in 1961 until approximately 1992, when the company transitioned to aqueous cleaning methods. He stated that TCE drums were removed when the switch occurred but could not produce any closure documentation, decommissioning records, or confirmation sampling reports for the former degreasing area.

Mr. Breckenridge confirmed that electroplating operations involved hexavalent chromium and nickel plating solutions, conducted from 1965 through 2004. He stated that when electroplating operations ceased in 2004, the plating tanks were drained and the sludge was disposed of by Tri-County Environmental Hauling Inc. He produced six hazardous waste manifests from 2004 documenting the disposal of 14,200 pounds of waste under RCRA waste codes D007 (chromium) and F006 (electroplating sludge).

When asked about hazardous waste disposal records for the electroplating operations conducted from 1965 through 2003, Mr. Breckenridge stated that such records were \"not available.\" He indicated that manifests and disposal documentation from those years had not been retained and could not be located.

**Corporate Records.** Mr. Breckenridge declined to provide access to corporate records from 1961 through 1990, including operating records, environmental records, waste disposal logs, and other documentation that may have been relevant to the assessment. He stated that these records were \"in storage somewhere\" and were not readily accessible. He did not indicate a willingness to retrieve or produce these records.

**Environmental Compliance.** Mr. Breckenridge stated that \"all the chemicals were handled properly\" throughout the history of operations at the Subject Property. However, he acknowledged that he did not have documentation to support this assertion, particularly for the period prior to his direct involvement in the company.

**1988 Chemical Spill.** When informed of the March 14, 1988 incident report from the City of Toledo Fire Department documenting a chemical spill involving degreasing solvent at the Subject Property, Mr. Breckenridge stated that he was \"not aware of any spills.\" Upon being presented with the fire department record, he stated the incident was \"a minor incident\" and \"nothing to worry about.\" He provided no additional details regarding the nature, quantity, or cleanup of the spill.

**2003 Ohio EPA NOV.** Mr. Breckenridge acknowledged that an Ohio EPA compliance inspection occurred in 2003 and that a Notice of Violation was issued. He stated the matter was \"resolved years ago\" and characterized it as a routine regulatory matter. He was unable to provide a copy of the consent order that resolved the NOV.

**Monitoring Well (MW-1).** When asked about the monitoring well labeled \"MW-1\" observed near the northeast property boundary, Mr. Breckenridge stated that he did not know who installed the well, when it was installed, or its purpose. He was unable to provide any monitoring records, well construction details, or analytical data associated with the well.

**Former Drum Storage Area.** When asked about the deteriorated curbed area on the east side of the property, Mr. Breckenridge was unable to provide specific details regarding what materials had been stored in the area. He acknowledged, \"We used to keep drums out there before the EPA came around.\" He did not elaborate on the types of materials, the duration of storage, or any cleanup actions taken.

**General Claims.** Mr. Breckenridge stated that he had no knowledge of any spills, releases, or environmental contamination at the Subject Property, and claimed no knowledge of any regulatory enforcement actions beyond the 2003 NOV. These claims are inconsistent with the documented 1988 chemical spill recorded by the Toledo Fire Department and the 2003 NOV issued by Ohio EPA.

**7.2 Interview with Local Government Officials**

**City of Toledo Fire Department.** A records request was submitted to the City of Toledo Fire Department. The response included a single incident report dated March 14, 1988, documenting a response to a \"chemical spill\" at 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive. The report describes the incident as involving an \"unknown quantity of degreasing solvent\" released from an overturned drum. The report indicates that the spill was cleaned up by Breckenridge Manufacturing employees using absorbent materials. The Fire Department did not conduct a follow-up inspection, and no additional records related to the incident were identified.

**City of Toledo Building Department.** A records request was submitted to the City of Toledo Building Department. The Building Department\'s records contained building permits consistent with the known construction history of the Subject Property (1962, 1972, 1978, and 1985) but did not include any records of environmental significance.

**Lucas County Auditor.** Parcel records obtained from the Lucas County Auditor confirmed the parcel numbers, ownership history, and property use classifications consistent with the information described in Sections 3.1 and 4.1.

**7.3 Interview with Ohio EPA**

A written records request was submitted to the Ohio EPA Northwest District Office. A response was received on September 28, 2024, and contained the following information:

> • Ohio EPA confirmed the RCRA Small Quantity Generator status of the Subject Property (EPA ID No. OHD044891237).
>
> • Ohio EPA records document a 2003 compliance inspection that resulted in the issuance of a Notice of Violation (File No. NW-2003-0471) for \"improper storage of hazardous waste.\" The NOV was resolved through a consent order requiring Breckenridge Manufacturing to conduct a facility-wide hazardous waste audit. Ohio EPA provided the 2003 inspection report. The consent order file was described as \"archived and not immediately available.\" A notation in the NOV file states that the matter was reportedly resolved; however, the actual consent order was not produced for review.
>
> • Ohio EPA confirmed that the Subject Property is located within a designated groundwater management zone established in connection with the documented tetrachloroethylene (PCE) groundwater contamination plume associated with the former Lake Erie Dry Cleaning facility at 4900 Lakeshore Industrial Drive (VAP ID NW-0923).
>
> • Ohio EPA\'s Bureau of Underground Storage Tank Regulations (BUSTR) confirmed that no tank closure records, removal records, or site assessment reports are on file for either the 2,000-gallon waste solvent UST or the 500-gallon heating oil UST identified in historical records for the Subject Property.

**7.4 Interviews with Others**

No additional interviews were conducted as part of this assessment. No attempts were made to contact former employees of Breckenridge Manufacturing Co. or other persons who may have had knowledge of historical operations, chemical usage, waste disposal practices, or environmental conditions at the Subject Property.

**[8.0 Data Failures / Data Gaps]{.underline}**

The following data gaps were identified during this Phase I ESA:

**Data Gap No. 1 --- Hazardous Waste Disposal Records (1965--2003).** Hazardous waste disposal records (manifests, shipping documents, disposal receipts, land disposal restriction notices) for the electroplating operations conducted from 1965 through 2003 were not available for review. Only six hazardous waste manifests from 2004, documenting the disposal of 14,200 pounds of waste (RCRA codes D007 and F006), were produced by the property owner. The absence of disposal documentation for the preceding 39 years of electroplating operations represents a gap in the documentation of waste disposal practices at the Subject Property. This data gap is partially mitigated by the 2004 closure documentation and by Mr. Breckenridge\'s verbal assurances that wastes were handled properly during the full period of operations. The impact of this data gap on the conclusions of this assessment is discussed in connection with REC No. 2 in Section 9.1.

**Data Gap No. 2 --- Corporate Records (1961--1990).** Corporate records from 1961 through 1990, including operating records, environmental compliance records, waste logs, and other potentially relevant documentation, were not made available by the property owner. Mr. Breckenridge indicated that these records are in storage and are not readily accessible. Reasonable efforts were made to obtain this information during the interview and records review process. The unavailability of these records limits the ability to fully characterize historical operations, chemical usage, and waste management practices during the early decades of the property\'s industrial use.

The identified data gaps are not expected to significantly affect the findings and conclusions of this assessment. The data gaps have been considered in the development of the findings and recommendations presented in Section 9.0.

**[9.0 Findings and Conclusions]{.underline}**

Based on the records review, site reconnaissance, interviews, and evaluation of information conducted in accordance with ASTM E1527-21, Greenfield identified the following environmental conditions at the Subject Property. A summary of recognized environmental conditions is presented in Table 3.

**9.1 Recognized Environmental Conditions (RECs)**

**REC No. 1 --- Former Trichloroethylene (TCE) Degreasing Operations and Abandoned Waste TCE Drum**

Trichloroethylene (TCE) was used as a vapor degreasing solvent in the northwest corner of the main manufacturing building from 1961 through approximately 1992, a period of more than 30 years. The former degreasing area exhibits visible staining on the concrete floor slab and walls. A photoionization detector (PID) reading of 42.3 ppm was recorded at a crack in the floor slab, indicating the presence of volatile organic compound vapors emanating from beneath the floor. No closure documentation, decommissioning records, or soil/groundwater confirmation sampling data were located for the degreasing area following the reported cessation of TCE use in 1992.

An abandoned 55-gallon drum labeled \"WASTE TRICHLOROETHYLENE\" was observed on the ground surface behind the maintenance/utility building. The drum is approximately half full, with a rusted bottom and evidence of past leakage. Stained soil was observed beneath the drum over an area of approximately three feet by three feet. Gerald Breckenridge III indicated that the drum has been in its present location for an extended period, possibly since the early 1990s.

A 1988 chemical spill involving \"unknown quantity of degreasing solvent\" from an overturned drum was documented in City of Toledo Fire Department records. Cleanup was reportedly performed by Breckenridge Manufacturing employees. No follow-up inspection or environmental assessment was conducted following the spill.

The prolonged use of TCE for degreasing, the elevated PID readings indicating residual VOC vapors, the undocumented closure of the degreasing area, the abandoned drum with evidence of release to soil, and the documented 1988 spill collectively indicate a potential release of chlorinated solvents to soil and potentially to groundwater in the northwest portion of the main building and the area behind the maintenance building. This condition constitutes a recognized environmental condition (REC).

**Recommendation:** A Phase II ESA is recommended, including the advancement of soil borings and the installation of temporary or permanent groundwater monitoring wells in the vicinity of the former degreasing area and the abandoned TCE drum, to characterize the nature and extent of potential chlorinated solvent contamination in soil and groundwater.

**REC No. 2 --- Former Electroplating Operations (Hexavalent Chromium)**

Electroplating operations involving hexavalent chromium and nickel plating solutions were conducted from 1965 through 2004 in the south wing of the main manufacturing building, a period of approximately 39 years. The former electroplating room exhibits prominent blue-green staining on the concrete floor and walls, consistent with chromium residues. The concrete floor shows pitting, etching, and chemical degradation indicative of prolonged exposure to acidic and alkaline plating solutions.

An in-ground concrete sump in the center of the former electroplating room contains approximately four inches of liquid with a visible iridescent sheen. The source and composition of this liquid were not determined during the Phase I ESA, as sampling is beyond the scope of ASTM E1527-21.

Hazardous waste disposal documentation was available only for the final year of operations (2004), consisting of six manifests documenting the disposal of 14,200 pounds of hazardous waste under RCRA waste codes D007 (chromium) and F006 (electroplating sludge) by Tri-County Environmental Hauling Inc. No manifests, shipping documents, or disposal records were produced for any of the preceding 39 years of operation (1965--2003), representing a significant gap in the documentation of hazardous waste management at the facility.

A 2003 Ohio EPA compliance inspection documented \"improper storage of hazardous waste\" at the facility, resulting in the issuance of a Notice of Violation (File No. NW-2003-0471). The NOV was resolved through a consent order requiring a facility-wide waste audit.

The extensive period of electroplating operations, the visible chromium residues on floors and walls, the presence of liquid waste with sheen in the sump, the near-total absence of hazardous waste disposal documentation, and the documented regulatory violation collectively indicate a potential release of hexavalent chromium and related metals to the subsurface. This condition constitutes a recognized environmental condition (REC).

**Recommendation:** A Phase II ESA is recommended, including characterization sampling of the sump liquid, advancement of soil borings through and beneath the concrete floor slab in the former electroplating area, and installation of groundwater monitoring wells to evaluate potential metals contamination in soil and groundwater.

**9.2 Controlled Recognized Environmental Conditions (CRECs)**

No controlled recognized environmental conditions (CRECs) were identified for the Subject Property as part of this Phase I ESA.

**9.3 Historical Recognized Environmental Conditions (HRECs)**

**HREC No. 1 --- 500-Gallon Halstead Farmhouse Heating Oil UST**

Historical records, including the 1948 and 1955 Sanborn fire insurance maps, indicate that a 500-gallon heating oil underground storage tank served the Halstead family farmhouse located in the northwest portion of the Subject Property. The farmhouse was demolished in 1961 when Breckenridge Manufacturing Co. acquired the property and commenced construction of the main manufacturing building.

The tank was likely removed when the farmhouse was demolished in 1961. No surface evidence of a UST (fill pipes, vent pipes, or surface disturbance) was observed at the approximate former farmhouse location during the site reconnaissance. Given the demolition of the farmhouse more than 60 years ago and the absence of any surface indicators of a remaining tank, this condition is historical in nature and, in Greenfield\'s professional judgment, does not present a current risk to the Subject Property. This condition is classified as a historical recognized environmental condition (HREC).

**9.4 De Minimis Conditions**

**De Minimis Condition No. 1 --- Norfolk Southern Railroad Herbicide Application**

Historical herbicide application has been documented as a standard maintenance practice along railroad rights-of-way, including the Norfolk Southern Railroad right-of-way that forms the western boundary of the Subject Property. Given the industrial character of the Subject Property, the limited area of potential influence along the western boundary, and the absence of any evidence of herbicide migration onto the Subject Property during the site reconnaissance, this condition is characterized as de minimis and unlikely to present a material risk of harm to public health or the environment or to the Subject Property.

**9.5 Additional Findings**

The following additional items were noted during the assessment but do not constitute RECs, CRECs, HRECs, or de minimis conditions:

> • **RCRA Small Quantity Generator Status.** The Subject Property\'s listing as a RCRA Small Quantity Generator (EPA ID No. OHD044891237) is consistent with the historical metal stamping and finishing operations conducted at the property. The generator status alone does not constitute a recognized environmental condition.
>
> • **10,000-Gallon Diesel UST.** The registered 10,000-gallon diesel fuel UST (Ohio Facility ID 48-22-10-0087) is properly registered, was upgraded with cathodic corrosion protection in 1998, and was last inspected in May 2022 with no violations noted. No evidence of a release was observed during the site reconnaissance. This UST does not constitute a REC.
>
> • **Toledo Coatings & Adhesives Inc. (Adjacent East).** The former Toledo Coatings & Adhesives site to the east was the subject of a CERCLIS preliminary assessment; EPA determined no further action was warranted, and the site was archived in 1999. No documented contamination plume has been identified as migrating from this property toward the Subject Property. This listing does not constitute a REC to the Subject Property.

**9.6 Recommendations.** Based on the findings of this Phase I ESA, Greenfield recommends that a Phase II Environmental Site Assessment be conducted to further assess the two identified recognized environmental conditions. The recommended Phase II scope of work includes the following:

1\. **Former Degreasing Area (REC No. 1):** Advancement of soil borings through the concrete floor slab in the northwest corner of the main manufacturing building, collection of soil samples for laboratory analysis of volatile organic compounds (VOCs), including chlorinated solvents (TCE and degradation products). Installation of temporary or permanent groundwater monitoring wells and collection of groundwater samples for VOC analysis.

2\. **Abandoned TCE Drum (REC No. 1):** Advancement of soil borings in the vicinity of the abandoned drum behind the maintenance building, collection of soil samples for VOC analysis, and collection of groundwater samples.

3\. **Former Electroplating Room (REC No. 2):** Collection and characterization of the sump liquid for metals, volatile organic compounds, and other parameters of concern. Advancement of soil borings through the concrete floor slab in the south wing of the main building, collection of soil samples for metals analysis (including hexavalent chromium). Installation of groundwater monitoring wells and collection of groundwater samples for metals analysis.

The Phase II investigation should be designed and conducted by a qualified environmental professional in accordance with ASTM E1903-19 or the applicable standard of practice at the time the work is performed.

*Ohio Professional Engineer Seal Affixed Hereto*

**[12.0 References]{.underline}**

1\. ASTM International. *E1527-21, Standard Practice for Environmental Site Assessments: Phase I Environmental Site Assessment Process.* West Conshohocken, PA, 2021.

2\. United States Environmental Protection Agency. *Standards and Practices for All Appropriate Inquiries.* 40 C.F.R. Part 312. November 2005 (as amended).

3\. Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and Liability Act (CERCLA), 42 U.S.C. §9601(35)(B).

4\. Ohio Revised Code, Chapter 3746, Voluntary Action Program.

5\. Ohio Revised Code, §5301.82, Environmental Covenants.

6\. Environmental Data Resources, Inc. (EDR). *EDR Radius Map Report with GeoCheck --- 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Ohio 43611.* September 15, 2024.

7\. Sanborn Map Company. Fire Insurance Maps for Toledo, Lucas County, Ohio. Various years: 1948, 1955, 1962, 1970, 1978, 1987, 1995.

8\. Ohio Environmental Protection Agency, Northwest District Office. Written correspondence dated September 28, 2024, in response to records request.

10\. Lucas County Auditor. Parcel records for Parcel Nos. 18-04562-001, 18-04562-002, and 18-04562-003.

11\. Fidelity Abstract & Title, Toledo, Ohio. Title search results for the Subject Property.

12\. Historical aerial photographs. Various years: 1950, 1960, 1970, 1980, 1990, 2000, 2010, 2020. Obtained through EDR and NETR Online.

13\. City of Toledo, Ohio. City directories, various years: 1948--2020.

**[Appendix A --- EDR Database Report Summary]{.underline}**

The following table summarizes the key findings from the Environmental Data Resources, Inc. (EDR) Radius Map Report with GeoCheck, dated September 15, 2024. The full EDR report is maintained in the project file and is available upon request.

One 10,000-gal. diesel UST (Facility ID 48-22-10-0087), installed 1978, upgraded 1998, compliant. Not listed on CERCLIS/SEMS, NPL, Ohio Master Sites List, CORRACTS, or ERNS.

  Lake Erie Dry Cleaning             4900 Lakeshore Industrial Dr.   North, \~120 ft            Ohio Master Sites List; SEMS; Ohio VAP   VAP ID NW-0923. Confirmed PCE groundwater plume. MW-7 (120 ft north of Subject Property boundary): PCE at 87 µg/L (Ohio standard: 5 µg/L). Plume migration: south-southeast. Covenant Not to Sue issued 2018 (dry cleaner parcel only). Subject Property within designated groundwater management zone.

  Toledo Coatings & Adhesives Inc.   Adjacent east                   East, adjacent             CERCLIS/SEMS archive                     EPA ID OHD987654321. Preliminary assessment; EPA no further action, 1999. No documented plume migrating toward Subject Property.

  Lakeshore Freight Services         Adjacent south                  South, adjacent            None                                     No listings identified.

  Norfolk Southern Railroad          Adjacent west                   West, adjacent             None                                     No listings identified for right-of-way adjacent to Subject Property.

**Narrative Summary:**

The EDR report identified the Subject Property in two databases: the RCRA Small Quantity Generator database and the Ohio Underground Storage Tank database. The Subject Property\'s RCRA-SQG listing is consistent with the historical generation of hazardous waste during metal stamping and finishing operations. The registered 10,000-gallon diesel UST has a compliant inspection history.

The most significant nearby listing is the Lake Erie Dry Cleaning facility at 4900 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, located approximately 120 feet north of the Subject Property boundary. This facility is listed as a confirmed release site with a documented PCE groundwater plume migrating in a south-southeast direction --- toward the Subject Property. The PCE concentration at the nearest documented monitoring point (MW-7) exceeds the Ohio groundwater standard by more than 17 times. The Subject Property is located within the designated groundwater management zone established in connection with this plume. The Covenant Not to Sue issued under the Ohio VAP in 2018 addresses only the dry cleaner parcel and does not address off-site impacts of the PCE plume.

The Toledo Coatings & Adhesives site to the east was archived by EPA after a preliminary assessment in 1999, and no documented contamination plume migrates toward the Subject Property. The properties to the south and west were not identified in any searched databases.

**[Appendix B --- Historical Source Documentation Summary]{.underline}**

**Sanborn Fire Insurance Map Summary**

The following table summarizes the key observations from the Sanborn fire insurance maps reviewed for the Subject Property and vicinity:

  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Year**                **Subject Property Depiction**                                                            **Key Notations**
  ----------------------- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  1955                    Same as 1948; no changes                                                                  500-gal. heating oil UST notation remains

  1962                    Farmhouse no longer depicted; new manufacturing building (Breckenridge Mfg. Co.)          \"Metal Stamping\"

  1970                    Manufacturing building; no additional structures                                          \"Metal Stamping,\" \"Degreasing,\" \"Electroplating\"

  1978                    Addition to main building (east side); maintenance/utility building shown                 \"10,000-gal. diesel fuel tank (underground)\" near maintenance bldg.

  1987                    Manufacturing complex with main bldg., addition, maintenance bldg., and warehouse bldg.   \"2,000-gal. waste solvent tank (underground)\" on east side of property; 500-gal. heating oil UST notation at former farmhouse location; 10,000-gal. diesel UST

  1995                    Similar to 1987; no significant changes                                                   Less detailed coverage; UST notations not explicitly repeated
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Aerial Photograph Summary**

The following summarizes the observations from historical aerial photographs reviewed for the Subject Property:

  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Approximate Year**                **Key Observations**
  ----------------------------------- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  1950                                Agricultural land; row crops; Halstead farmhouse visible (NW portion); no industrial development

  1960                                Agricultural; farmhouse visible; early site grading activity may be present

  1970                                Main manufacturing building and parking areas established; maintenance building visible

  1980                                Building addition visible (east side of main building); site configuration consistent with 1978 Sanborn

  1990                                Warehouse building present; drum/container storage area visible on east side (\~40 ft × 60 ft unpaved area with objects consistent with drums)

  2000                                Similar to 1990; drum storage area still visible but with fewer objects

  2010                                Drum storage area appears less active; vegetation encroaching

  2020                                Current configuration; no drums visible; former drum storage area appears as disturbed ground with stressed vegetation
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The drum or container storage area visible in the 1990 and 2000 aerial photographs corresponds to the curbed area observed during the site reconnaissance on the east side of the property (see Section 6.2.4). The 1987 Sanborn map notation of a 2,000-gallon waste solvent UST is located in the general vicinity of this drum storage area.

**[Appendix C --- Regulatory Correspondence Summary]{.underline}**

**Ohio EPA Northwest District Office --- Response Dated September 28, 2024**

A written records request was submitted to the Ohio EPA Northwest District Office for information pertaining to the Subject Property at 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Ohio 43611 (EPA ID OHD044891237). The Ohio EPA response, dated September 28, 2024, included the following key information:

> • **RCRA Status:** Confirmed Small Quantity Generator (SQG) under EPA ID OHD044891237.
>
> • **Inspection History:** One compliance inspection documented in 2003. The inspection identified \"improper storage of hazardous waste.\" A Notice of Violation was issued (File No. NW-2003-0471), and a consent order was entered requiring Breckenridge Manufacturing Co. to conduct a facility-wide hazardous waste audit. The consent order file was described as \"archived and not immediately available.\" A notation in the NOV file indicates the matter was reportedly resolved.
>
> • **Groundwater Management Zone:** The Subject Property is located within a designated groundwater management zone established in connection with the documented PCE groundwater contamination plume originating from the former Lake Erie Dry Cleaning facility (VAP ID NW-0923) at 4900 Lakeshore Industrial Drive.
>
> • **UST Records:** Ohio EPA\'s Bureau of Underground Storage Tank Regulations (BUSTR) confirmed the registration of one 10,000-gallon diesel fuel UST (Facility ID 48-22-10-0087) at the Subject Property. BUSTR confirmed that no closure records, removal records, or site assessment reports are on file for either the 2,000-gallon waste solvent UST or the 500-gallon heating oil UST identified in historical records for the Subject Property.

**City of Toledo Fire Department**

A records request was submitted to the City of Toledo Fire Department. The Fire Department response included one incident report dated March 14, 1988, documenting a fire department response to a \"chemical spill\" at 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive. The report describes the incident as involving an \"unknown quantity of degreasing solvent\" released from an overturned drum. The cleanup was performed by Breckenridge Manufacturing employees using absorbent materials. No follow-up inspection was documented.

**Lucas County Auditor**

Parcel records obtained from the Lucas County Auditor confirmed the parcel numbers (18-04562-001, 18-04562-002, 18-04562-003), total acreage (14.7 acres), ownership (Breckenridge Manufacturing Co.), and property use classifications (industrial/commercial) consistent with the information described in the body of this report.

**[Appendix D --- Site Photographs (Descriptions)]{.underline}**

The following photographs were taken during the site reconnaissance conducted on October 2, 2024, by Jason Mulvaney, Staff Scientist, Greenfield Environmental Consultants Inc. Photographs are maintained in the project file and are available upon request.

  6                 10/02/2024        J. Mulvaney        Stained soil beneath and surrounding the abandoned TCE drum. Staining area approximately 3 ft × 3 ft. Dark, oily appearance. Vegetation absent in stained area.

Deteriorating concrete curbing delineates an approximately 40 ft × 60 ft area. Stressed vegetation (yellowed, sparse) within the curbed area contrasts with surrounding grass.

  9                 10/02/2024        J. Mulvaney        Monitoring well \"MW-1\" near northeast property boundary. Flush-mounted PVC well casing with damaged surface cover (cracked PVC, loose bolts). Debris accumulated around base.

  10                10/02/2024        J. Mulvaney        Stormwater detention basin in northeast corner of property. Basin contains shallow water. No unusual staining, sheen, or discoloration observed.

  11                10/02/2024        J. Mulvaney        General exterior view looking south across paved parking/loading area toward warehouse building and southern property boundary. Unmaintained grass areas visible to east and south.

**[Appendix E --- Qualifications and Resumes]{.underline}**

**Greenfield Environmental Consultants Inc. --- Firm Profile**

Greenfield Environmental Consultants Inc. (Greenfield) is an environmental consulting firm headquartered at 550 Science Park Boulevard, Suite 210, Akron, Ohio 44311. The firm specializes in environmental site assessments, soil and groundwater investigation and remediation, underground storage tank management, brownfield redevelopment, and regulatory compliance consulting. Greenfield serves clients throughout Ohio and the Great Lakes region, including commercial, industrial, institutional, and governmental entities.

Greenfield maintains professional liability insurance with a policy limit of \$5,000,000 per claim and in the aggregate, underwritten by Pinnacle Specialty Insurance Group, Policy No. PSI-ENV-2024-08827.

She has managed over 300 Phase I ESAs and more than 75 Phase II investigations for commercial, industrial, and institutional properties. Ms. Haddad meets the definition of Environmental Professional as defined in 40 C.F.R. §312.10.

**Jason Mulvaney --- Field Scientist**

> • Bachelor of Science, Environmental Science

He conducts field work under the supervision of senior environmental professionals. **(b)** If the Phase I ESA or any other environmental investigation conducted during the Environmental Due Diligence Period identifies any recognized environmental conditions (\"RECs\"), as that term is defined in ASTM E1527-21, that, in Buyer\'s reasonable judgment, could result in Remediation Costs exceeding Five Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$500,000), Buyer may, by written notice delivered to Seller on or before the expiration of the Environmental Due Diligence Period (i.e., no later than 5:00 p.m. Eastern Time on October 31, 2024), elect to terminate this Agreement and receive a full refund of the Earnest Money Deposit. Such written notice shall specify in reasonable detail the RECs identified and the basis for Buyer\'s determination that Remediation Costs could exceed Five Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$500,000). Upon Seller\'s receipt of a valid termination notice under this Section 6.3(b), this Agreement shall terminate, the Earnest Money Deposit shall be returned to Buyer within ten (10) business days, and neither Party shall have any further obligation to the other under this Agreement, except for obligations that expressly survive termination.

**(c)** If Buyer fails to deliver written notice of termination pursuant to Section 6.3(b) on or before the expiration of the Environmental Due Diligence Period, Buyer shall be deemed to have waived its right to terminate this Agreement on environmental grounds under this Section 6.3, and the Parties shall proceed to Closing subject to the other conditions set forth in this Article VI. The waiver effected by this Section 6.3(c) shall be automatic and shall not require any further action or acknowledgment by either Party. For the avoidance of doubt, Buyer\'s waiver of the right to terminate under this Section 6.3 shall not constitute a waiver of Buyer\'s rights under Article VIII (Indemnification) with respect to any Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities or any breach or inaccuracy of any representation or warranty of Seller contained in this Agreement.

**(d)** Nothing in this Section 6.3 shall be construed to limit Buyer\'s ability to conduct additional environmental investigations after the expiration of the Environmental Due Diligence Period; provided, however, that the results of any such additional investigations shall not give rise to any right of termination under this Section 6.3 or any right to a refund of the Earnest Money Deposit, and Buyer shall bear the sole cost and expense of any such additional investigations. Seller agrees to provide Buyer with reasonable access to the Property for the purpose of any such additional investigations conducted between the expiration of the Environmental Due Diligence Period and the Closing Date, upon not less than forty-eight (48) hours\' prior written notice to Seller.

**(e)** The Phase I ESA shall name Buyer as the \"User\" (as that term is used in ASTM E1527-21) and shall authorize reliance by the Lender. Buyer shall deliver a copy of the Phase I ESA to the Lender promptly upon receipt thereof. In the event the Lender requires a Phase II Environmental Site Assessment or any other additional environmental investigation as a condition of the loan, Buyer shall bear the sole cost and expense of such additional investigation, and the results of such investigation shall be subject to the provisions of Sections 6.3(b) and 6.3(c) above (i.e., any termination right must be exercised, if at all, on or before the expiration of the Environmental Due Diligence Period).

**[ARTICLE VIII --- INDEMNIFICATION]{.underline}**

*(Excerpt --- Sections 8.1 through 8.4 only)*

**Section 8.1 Survival of Representations and Warranties.**

The representations and warranties of the Parties contained in this Agreement shall survive the Closing for the applicable periods set forth in Section 8.4(c). No claim for indemnification based upon a breach or inaccuracy of any representation or warranty may be asserted after the expiration of the applicable survival period, except as otherwise provided herein.

**Section 8.2 Seller Indemnification.**

**(a)** Subject to the limitations set forth in Section 8.4, from and after the Closing, Seller shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Buyer and its members, managers, officers, directors, employees, agents, successors, and assigns (collectively, the \"Buyer Indemnified Parties\") from and against any and all Losses arising out of, resulting from, or relating to:

> \(i\) any breach or inaccuracy of any representation or warranty of Seller contained in this Agreement (including, without limitation, Sections 4.12 and 4.13), whether such breach or inaccuracy exists as of the Effective Date or as of the Closing Date;
>
> \(ii\) any breach of any covenant or agreement of Seller contained in this Agreement to be performed or complied with by Seller prior to or after the Closing;
>
> \(iii\) any Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities; and
>
> \(iv\) any third-party claims arising out of or relating to the ownership, operation, or use of the Property prior to the Closing, including any claims by employees, contractors, neighboring property owners, Governmental Authorities, or other Persons.

**(b)** The indemnification obligations of Seller under this Section 8.2 shall be the sole and exclusive remedy of the Buyer Indemnified Parties with respect to the matters described herein, except in the case of fraud or intentional misrepresentation. The Parties acknowledge and agree that the indemnification provisions of this Article VIII were specifically negotiated and are an integral part of the consideration for the transactions contemplated by this Agreement, and that but for the agreement of Seller to provide the indemnification set forth in this Section 8.2, Buyer would not have agreed to enter into this Agreement.

**(c)** Seller\'s indemnification obligation under Section 8.2(a)(iii) shall apply to Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities regardless of whether such liabilities are discovered before or after the Closing; provided, however, that Seller\'s aggregate liability under this Section 8.2 shall be subject to the limitations set forth in Section 8.4, including the Indemnification Cap and the Survival Period. Buyer acknowledges that its sole recourse against Seller for Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities shall be through the indemnification provisions of this Article VIII, subject to the limitations herein.

**Section 8.3 Buyer Indemnification.**

From and after the Closing, Buyer shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Seller and its officers, directors, shareholders, employees, agents, successors, and assigns (collectively, the \"Seller Indemnified Parties\") from and against any and all Losses arising out of, resulting from, or relating to Buyer\'s ownership, operation, or use of the Property from and after the Closing, except to the extent such Losses constitute Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities for which Seller is responsible under Section 8.2.

**Section 8.4 Limitations on Indemnification.**

**(a) Basket.** Seller shall have no obligation to indemnify the Buyer Indemnified Parties under Section 8.2(a)(i) until the aggregate amount of Losses for which indemnification is sought under Section 8.2(a)(i) exceeds the Basket amount of Eighty-Seven Thousand Five Hundred Dollars (\$87,500), at which point Seller shall be obligated to indemnify the Buyer Indemnified Parties for the full amount of such Losses from the first dollar (i.e., the Basket is a true deductible that converts to first-dollar coverage once exceeded, and is not a deductible that reduces the amount otherwise recoverable). For the avoidance of doubt, the Basket shall not apply to claims for indemnification under Section 8.2(a)(ii), Section 8.2(a)(iii), or Section 8.2(a)(iv); claims under those subsections may be asserted without regard to the Basket amount.

**(b) Cap.** Notwithstanding anything in this Agreement to the contrary, the aggregate liability of Seller under this Article VIII shall not exceed the Indemnification Cap of One Million Seven Hundred Fifty Thousand Dollars (\$1,750,000). This limitation shall apply to all claims for indemnification under Section 8.2, whether arising from breaches of representations and warranties, breaches of covenants, Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities, or otherwise, and regardless of the legal theory upon which such claims are based (including, without limitation, breach of contract, tort, strict liability, or statutory liability). All payments made by Seller under this Article VIII shall be aggregated for purposes of determining whether the Indemnification Cap has been reached.

**(c) Survival Period.** The representations and warranties of Seller set forth in this Agreement (including, without limitation, Sections 4.12 and 4.13) shall survive the Closing for a period of three (3) years following the Closing Date (the \"Survival Period\"). No claim for indemnification under Section 8.2(a)(i) may be asserted after the expiration of the Survival Period unless written notice specifying in reasonable detail the nature and basis of such claim has been delivered to Seller prior to the expiration of the Survival Period. Seller\'s obligation to indemnify under Section 8.2(a)(iii) with respect to Pre-Closing Environmental Liabilities shall also expire upon the expiration of the Survival Period. Any claim for which proper notice has been given prior to the expiration of the Survival Period shall survive until final resolution of such claim, notwithstanding the subsequent expiration of the Survival Period.

**(d) Exceptions.** The limitations set forth in Sections 8.4(a), (b), and (c) shall not apply to Losses arising from fraud or intentional misrepresentation by Seller. In the event of fraud or intentional misrepresentation by Seller, Seller\'s liability shall not be subject to the Basket, the Indemnification Cap, or the Survival Period, and Buyer Indemnified Parties shall be entitled to recover the full amount of all Losses suffered or incurred as a result of such fraud or intentional misrepresentation, without limitation. The burden of establishing fraud or intentional misrepresentation shall rest with the Party asserting such claim.

**(e) Mitigation.** Each Buyer Indemnified Party shall use commercially reasonable efforts to mitigate any Losses for which it seeks indemnification under this Article VIII, including pursuing available insurance coverage and taking reasonable steps to minimize ongoing damages. The failure to use commercially reasonable efforts to mitigate shall reduce the amount of Losses recoverable hereunder by the amount that could reasonably have been avoided.

**(f) Insurance.** Seller\'s indemnification obligations under this Article VIII shall be reduced by the amount of any insurance proceeds actually received by the Buyer Indemnified Parties with respect to the applicable Losses, net of reasonable costs of collection (including premiums, deductibles, co-payments, and reasonable attorneys\' fees incurred in connection with pursuing such insurance proceeds). Buyer shall use commercially reasonable efforts to pursue available insurance coverage with respect to any Losses for which it seeks indemnification hereunder, provided that Buyer shall not be required to commence litigation against any insurer.

**GREAT LAKES CAPITAL BANK** One Lakefront Center, Suite 800 Cleveland, Ohio 44114

Telephone: (216) 555-4800 \| Facsimile: (216) 555-4801 *Commercial Real Estate Lending Division*

September 10, 2024

Mr. Marcus Choi Managing Partner Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC 200 Riverview Tower, Suite 1450 Columbus, Ohio 43215

**Re: Environmental Due Diligence Requirements --- Proposed Acquisition Loan, 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Ohio 43611 (Lucas County Parcel Nos. 18-04562-001, 18-04562-002, and 18-04562-003); Loan No. GLCB-2024-08173; Proposed Loan Amount: \$6,125,000**

Dear Mr. Choi:

**[1. Purpose]{.underline}**

I am writing in my capacity as Senior Loan Officer within the Commercial Real Estate Lending Division of Great Lakes Capital Bank (the \"Bank\") in connection with the application submitted by Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC (\"Borrower\" or \"Cornerstone\") for a commercial acquisition loan in the principal amount of Six Million One Hundred Twenty-Five Thousand Dollars (\$6,125,000) (the \"Loan\") at approximately 70% loan-to-value. The Loan proceeds, together with Borrower\'s equity contribution of Two Million Six Hundred Twenty-Five Thousand Dollars (\$2,625,000), will be used to finance the purchase of the approximately 14.7-acre former manufacturing and warehouse complex located at 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Ohio 43611 (the \"Property\") from Breckenridge Manufacturing Co. (\"Seller\") at a purchase price of Eight Million Seven Hundred Fifty Thousand Dollars (\$8,750,000).

The purpose of this letter is to set forth the Bank\'s mandatory environmental due diligence requirements that must be satisfied as conditions precedent to loan funding. These requirements are established pursuant to the Bank\'s Internal Environmental Risk Policy (Policy No. ERP-2021-04, as amended effective January 1, 2023) (the \"Environmental Risk Policy\"). Based on information provided in the loan application and in the Bank\'s preliminary review, the Property was historically used as a metal stamping, degreasing, electroplating, and painting facility from approximately 1961 through 2019. This historical use profile places the Property in the Bank\'s \"Elevated Environmental Risk\" category, which triggers enhanced due diligence requirements beyond the Bank\'s standard environmental underwriting procedures. The specific requirements are detailed in the sections that follow.

**[2. Phase I Environmental Site Assessment Requirements]{.underline}**

As a threshold condition to loan funding, the Borrower must provide the Bank with a Phase I Environmental Site Assessment (the \"Phase I ESA\") that conforms in all material respects to the current edition of ASTM International Standard Practice E1527-21, *Standard Practice for Environmental Site Assessments: Phase I Environmental Site Assessment Process*, and that satisfies the All Appropriate Inquiries (\"AAI\") rule promulgated under 40 C.F.R. Part 312 pursuant to the Small Business Liability Relief and Brownfields Revitalization Act.

The Phase I ESA must be prepared by a qualified Environmental Professional (\"EP\") as that term is defined in Section 3.2.25 of ASTM E1527-21 and 40 C.F.R. § 312.10. The EP must meet the educational, professional licensure, and experience requirements specified in the standard and the regulation. The Phase I ESA must name Great Lakes Capital Bank as an authorized reliance party in addition to Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC as the User of the report. Reliance language must appear both in the body of the report and in a separate reliance letter addressed to the Bank, if required by the environmental consultant.

The Phase I ESA must be delivered within the Bank\'s underwriting timeline described in Section 5 below and must not be older than 180 days from the date of site reconnaissance at the time of loan closing, consistent with the report shelf-life provisions of ASTM E1527-21, Section 4.6. Given the anticipated closing date of December 15, 2024, the site reconnaissance for the Phase I ESA must have occurred no earlier than June 18, 2024. A Phase I ESA with a site reconnaissance date earlier than June 18, 2024 will not be accepted unless it is updated in accordance with ASTM E1527-21, Section 4.6 requirements.

The Phase I ESA must include a thorough evaluation and identification of all recognized environmental conditions (\"RECs\"), controlled recognized environmental conditions (\"CRECs\"), historical recognized environmental conditions (\"HRECs\"), and de minimis conditions at the Property, applying the definitions set forth in ASTM E1527-21. The report must clearly categorize each identified condition using the appropriate ASTM designation and must provide sufficient supporting analysis for each classification.

In addition to the evaluation of on-site conditions, the Bank specifically requires that the Phase I ESA evaluate off-site sources of contamination that may be migrating onto or otherwise affecting the subject Property, including but not limited to contaminated groundwater plumes from adjacent or nearby properties. The Bank is aware that the subject Property is located in proximity to properties with documented environmental conditions, including but not limited to the former dry cleaning facility at 4900 Lakeshore Industrial Drive. The Phase I ESA must evaluate whether any off-site contamination, including groundwater plumes, may be migrating toward or impacting the subject Property, and any such conditions must be identified as recognized environmental conditions. The environmental consultant must review relevant regulatory databases, Ohio EPA files, and publicly available information to assess the potential for off-site plume migration, and must describe in the report the analysis undertaken and the conclusions reached regarding these off-site conditions.

The Phase I ESA must identify and evaluate all data gaps as defined in ASTM E1527-21 and must describe in reasonable detail the efforts made by the EP to address each identified data gap, as well as the significance of any data gaps that remain unresolved. The Bank will independently assess the materiality of any unresolved data gaps in connection with its underwriting review.

Finally, the Bank requires that the environmental consulting firm retained to prepare the Phase I ESA carry a minimum of Two Million Dollars (\$2,000,000) in professional liability (errors and omissions) insurance. Evidence of such coverage must be provided to the Bank upon request.

**[3. Outcome Scenarios and Conditional Requirements]{.underline}**

**3.A. Clean Phase I ESA --- No RECs Identified**

If the Phase I ESA identifies no RECs, CRECs, or significant unresolved data gaps, the Bank will accept the Phase I ESA as satisfying the environmental due diligence condition for loan funding, subject to the Bank\'s independent review and acceptance of the report by the Bank\'s environmental risk management group and, if deemed necessary, the Bank\'s outside environmental counsel. The Bank reserves the right to request supplemental information from the environmental consultant in the course of its review.

**3.B. Phase I ESA Identifying RECs --- Triggered Requirements**

If the Phase I ESA identifies one or more RECs, the Bank will **not** fund the Loan based solely on the Phase I ESA. In such event, the Borrower must satisfy **all** of the following additional requirements before the Bank will proceed to closing:

> **1. Phase II Environmental Site Assessment.** Borrower must commission and deliver a Phase II Environmental Site Assessment (the \"Phase II ESA\") conforming to ASTM E1903-19, *Standard Practice for Environmental Site Assessments: Phase II Environmental Site Assessment Process* (or the then-current edition of such standard), conducted by a qualified environmental consultant, to characterize the nature and extent of contamination associated with each identified REC. The Phase II ESA must include quantified remediation cost estimates prepared by a licensed professional engineer, including: (a) a best-case remediation cost estimate, (b) a most-likely remediation cost estimate, and (c) a worst-case remediation cost estimate. Each estimate must identify the assumed remediation methodology, applicable regulatory cleanup standards, and the estimated time to completion of remedial activities.
>
> **2. Environmental Insurance.** Borrower must obtain a Pollution Legal Liability (\"PLL\") insurance policy from a carrier rated A- (Excellent) or better by AM Best, with policy limits of not less than the greater of (x) the worst-case remediation cost estimate set forth in the Phase II ESA, or (y) Five Million Dollars (\$5,000,000), with a policy term of not less than ten (10) years from the date of loan closing. The Bank must be named as an additional insured and loss payee under the PLL policy. The Bank\'s Environmental Risk Policy requires that anticipated remediation costs be fully covered by a combination of (i) seller indemnification obligations, (ii) environmental insurance, and (iii) borrower reserves or escrow deposits. The Bank will not rely solely on seller indemnification provisions in the purchase agreement to address environmental risk, as such provisions are typically subject to caps, baskets, deductibles, and survival periods that may be insufficient to cover full remediation costs over the duration of the Bank\'s exposure. Accordingly, environmental insurance is a non-negotiable requirement where RECs are identified in the Phase I ESA.
>
> **3. Remediation Cost Reserve / Escrow.** If the most-likely remediation cost estimate set forth in the Phase II ESA exceeds Two Hundred Fifty Thousand Dollars (\$250,000), the Bank requires the Borrower to establish a remediation cost escrow account at Great Lakes Capital Bank (the \"Environmental Escrow\") in an amount equal to one hundred twenty-five percent (125%) of the most-likely remediation cost estimate. Funds deposited in the Environmental Escrow may be drawn upon only with the Bank\'s prior written approval for documented remediation expenditures supported by invoices, progress reports, and, where applicable, regulatory correspondence confirming satisfactory progress.
>
> **4. Remediation Plan.** If estimated remediation costs exceed Five Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$500,000) under either the most-likely or worst-case scenario, the Bank requires Borrower to submit a remediation plan prepared and approved by a licensed environmental professional and, where applicable, submitted to and approved by the relevant state regulatory authority (Ohio Environmental Protection Agency), demonstrating that the identified contamination can be addressed within a commercially reasonable timeframe not to exceed thirty-six (36) months from the date of loan closing.
>
> **5. Vapor Intrusion Assessment.** If the Phase I ESA or Phase II ESA identifies volatile organic compounds (\"VOCs\") in soil or groundwater at or beneath the Property, the Bank requires a vapor intrusion assessment conducted in accordance with Ohio EPA and U.S. EPA guidance, and, if the assessment identifies vapor intrusion risks warranting mitigation, a vapor mitigation plan for any occupied or occupiable structures on the Property. The vapor mitigation plan must be implemented prior to or concurrently with closing, as determined by the Bank in its discretion.

**3.C. Unacceptable Environmental Risk**

The Bank reserves the right to decline to fund the Loan if, in the Bank\'s sole and absolute judgment, the environmental conditions at the Property present an unacceptable risk to collateral value or to the Bank\'s security interest, regardless of the availability of insurance, escrow, or other mitigation measures. Without limiting the generality of the foregoing, the Bank will not fund the Loan if: (a) the worst-case remediation cost estimate exceeds fifty percent (50%) of the appraised value of the Property; (b) the Property is listed or proposed for listing on the National Priorities List (\"NPL\") maintained by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency; (c) the Property is subject to an active RCRA corrective action order or similar regulatory enforcement action that could result in a lien senior to the Bank\'s mortgage lien; or (d) there are unresolved data gaps that, in the Bank\'s judgment, prevent adequate characterization of the environmental risk associated with the Property.

**[4. AAI Compliance and CERCLA Protections]{.underline}**

The Bank requires that the Phase I ESA satisfy the AAI rule (40 C.F.R. Part 312) to support the Borrower\'s ability to assert, as applicable, the innocent landowner defense under CERCLA Section 101(35), the contiguous property owner defense under CERCLA Section 107(q), or the bona fide prospective purchaser defense under CERCLA Section 107(r). The Phase I ESA must document all inquiries of the current owner (Breckenridge Manufacturing Co.) and current occupants, all records requests submitted to federal, state, and local regulatory agencies, and all data gaps encountered during the assessment process. The Bank notes that failure to satisfy AAI requirements may expose both the Borrower (as the acquiring party) and the Bank (as the holder of a security interest in the Property) to CERCLA liability without available statutory defenses. The Bank\'s environmental counsel, Whitfield & Crane LLP, will independently review the Phase I ESA for AAI compliance and may request supplemental documentation from the environmental consultant or the Borrower as part of that review.

**[5. Delivery Timeline and Conditions Precedent]{.underline}**

The Phase I ESA must be delivered to the Bank in final, executed form no later than thirty (30) days prior to the anticipated closing date of December 15, 2024 --- that is, no later than **November 15, 2024**. If a Phase II ESA is required pursuant to Section 3.B above, the Phase II ESA, together with the PLL insurance binder and evidence of the Environmental Escrow deposit, must be delivered no later than fifteen (15) days prior to closing --- that is, no later than **November 30, 2024**. The Bank reserves the right to extend the closing date or to decline to fund the Loan if the required environmental deliverables are not provided within these timeframes. All environmental reports delivered to the Bank must be in final, executed form (not in draft) and must include the consultant\'s certification statement, the EP\'s professional qualifications, and all appendices and supporting documentation referenced in the report.

**[6. Ongoing Environmental Covenants (Post-Closing)]{.underline}**

The Borrower is advised that the loan documents will include ongoing environmental covenants requiring, among other things: (a) Borrower to notify the Bank in writing within five (5) business days of discovering any release or threatened release of hazardous substances at, on, under, or migrating to or from the Property; (b) Borrower to maintain environmental insurance (including the PLL policy, if applicable) for the full term of the Loan; (c) Borrower to comply with all applicable federal, state, and local environmental laws, regulations, and permits; (d) Borrower not to install, use, operate, or close any underground storage tanks (\"USTs\") at the Property without the Bank\'s prior written consent; and (e) Borrower to provide annual environmental compliance certifications to the Bank in such form as the Bank may reasonably require.

**[7. Reservation of Rights / Disclaimer]{.underline}**

This letter sets forth the Bank\'s current environmental due diligence requirements and does not constitute a commitment to lend or a modification of any existing commitment. The Bank\'s obligation to fund the Loan remains subject to all conditions precedent set forth in the commitment letter dated August 22, 2024 (Reference No. GLCB-CL-2024-08173), including but not limited to satisfactory appraisal, title insurance, survey, financial underwriting, and the environmental conditions described herein. The Bank expressly reserves the right to impose additional or modified environmental requirements based on the results of its review of the Phase I ESA, Phase II ESA (if applicable), or any other environmental information that comes to the Bank\'s attention during the underwriting process.

We look forward to working with Cornerstone on this acquisition and are available to discuss these requirements at your convenience. Please do not hesitate to contact me directly with any questions.

Very truly yours,

GREAT LAKES CAPITAL BANK

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**
