**RE: Records Request --- 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Ohio 43611 (Lucas County Parcel Nos. 18-04562-001, 18-04562-002, and 18-04562-003)**

Lauren Haddad, P.E. Project Manager Greenfield Environmental Consultants Inc. 550 Science Park Boulevard, Suite 210 Akron, Ohio 44311

Re: Environmental Records Request for Property at 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Lucas County, Ohio 43611; Your Reference: GEC Project No. 2024-0871

Dear Ms. Haddad:

The Ohio Environmental Protection Agency (\"Ohio EPA\"), Division of Environmental Response and Revitalization (\"DERR\"), Northwest District Office, acknowledges receipt of Greenfield Environmental Consultants Inc.\'s (\"Greenfield\") written records request dated September 9, 2024, submitted in connection with a Phase I Environmental Site Assessment being conducted pursuant to ASTM Standard Practice E1527-21 for the property located at 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Ohio 43611 (the \"Subject Property\"). Your request sought all available environmental records pertaining to the Subject Property and immediately adjacent properties maintained by Ohio EPA\'s Division of Environmental Response and Revitalization, Northwest District. This response covers records held by DERR, the Division of Hazardous Waste Management (\"DHWM\"), and the Bureau of Underground Storage Tank Regulations (\"BUSTR\"), as coordinated through the Northwest District Office. Please note that this response is provided for informational purposes only and does not constitute a legal opinion, formal determination, or advisory regarding the environmental status, condition, or regulatory standing of the Subject Property.

The responsive information is organized by topic area below.

**[1. RCRA Generator Status]{.underline}**

The Subject Property at 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Ohio 43611, is listed in Ohio EPA\'s hazardous waste handler database under EPA Identification Number OHD044891237. The facility is classified as a Small Quantity Generator (\"SQG\") of hazardous waste under the Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (\"RCRA\") and corresponding Ohio regulations codified at Ohio Administrative Code Chapter 3745-52. The registered generator of record is Breckenridge Manufacturing Co.

The most recent hazardous waste annual/biennial report on file with Ohio EPA for this facility is for reporting year 2004. That report reflects the generation and off-site shipment of hazardous wastes classified under the following RCRA waste codes:

> • **D007** --- Toxicity Characteristic (Chromium)
>
> • **F006** --- Wastewater Treatment Sludge from Electroplating Operations

Six hazardous waste manifests are on file for calendar year 2004, documenting the shipment of a combined total of approximately 14,200 pounds of hazardous waste. The designated transporter on all six manifests was Tri-County Environmental Hauling Inc. The manifests indicate that the waste was transported to a permitted treatment, storage, and disposal facility for off-site management.

Ohio EPA has no hazardous waste manifests, biennial reports, or annual hazardous waste generation reports on file for this facility for any reporting year other than 2004. This is notable in light of Ohio EPA inspection records, which indicate that Breckenridge Manufacturing Co. conducted electroplating operations at the Subject Property from approximately 1965 through 2004 --- a period of approximately 39 years for which no hazardous waste generation or shipment documentation exists in Ohio EPA\'s files. The absence of manifests and biennial reports for the period 1965 through 2003 is noted for the requestor\'s information and consideration in connection with the environmental assessment.

**[2. Compliance and Enforcement History]{.underline}**

Ohio EPA\'s Northwest District Office conducted a Compliance Evaluation Inspection (\"CEI\") of the facility operating at the Subject Property on July 17, 2003. A copy of the inspection report is enclosed with this response as **Attachment A**. The inspection report documented the following violations of Ohio\'s hazardous waste management regulations:

> \(1\) **Improper storage of hazardous waste in containers not in good condition.** Inspectors observed multiple rusted and visibly leaking 55-gallon drums in an outdoor storage area located on the east side of the Subject Property. The drums were found to contain electroplating sludge and spent chromium-bearing wastewater. Several drums exhibited corrosion-related perforations, and staining on the ground surface beneath and around the drums was observed.
>
> \(2\) **Failure to label containers of hazardous waste with the date upon which accumulation began**, in violation of Ohio Administrative Code § 3745-52-34(a)(2).
>
> \(3\) **Failure to maintain adequate records of hazardous waste determinations**, in violation of Ohio Administrative Code § 3745-52-11(d). The facility was unable to produce waste characterization analyses or written hazardous waste determination documentation for materials stored at the facility at the time of the inspection.

As a result of the violations documented during the July 17, 2003 CEI, Ohio EPA issued a Notice of Violation (\"NOV\"), File No. NW-2003-0471, on August 12, 2003. The NOV was addressed to Breckenridge Manufacturing Co. and its principal, Gerald Breckenridge III, as the owner and operator of the facility.

The NOV was subsequently resolved through a Director\'s Final Findings and Orders (consent order), which required Breckenridge Manufacturing Co. to undertake the following corrective actions:

> \(a\) Conduct a facility-wide waste audit to identify, inventory, and characterize all hazardous waste and potentially hazardous materials present at the facility;
>
> \(b\) Properly characterize and arrange for lawful disposal of all accumulated hazardous waste at the facility, including the drums identified during the July 2003 inspection;
>
> \(c\) Develop and implement a written hazardous waste management plan in conformance with the applicable requirements of Ohio Administrative Code Chapter 3745-52; and
>
> \(d\) Submit a written compliance certification to Ohio EPA\'s Northwest District Office within 120 days of the effective date of the consent order, attesting to completion of all required corrective actions.

The consent order file --- including the signed Director\'s Final Findings and Orders, any compliance certification submitted by Breckenridge Manufacturing Co., and all associated correspondence --- is currently archived at Ohio EPA\'s central records repository in Columbus and is not immediately available for review or reproduction at the Northwest District Office. If Greenfield wishes to obtain copies of the consent order and associated compliance documentation, a separate public records request should be directed to:

> Ohio EPA Central Office Division of Hazardous Waste Management Lazarus Government Center 50 West Town Street, Suite 700 Columbus, Ohio 43215
>
> Reference: File No. NW-2003-0471; Breckenridge Manufacturing Co., 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Ohio 43611

Please be advised that retrieval of archived files from Central Office records storage may require 30 to 45 business days from the date of receipt of the request.

**[3. Underground Storage Tank Records (BUSTR Coordination)]{.underline}**

Ohio EPA coordinated with the Bureau of Underground Storage Tank Regulations (\"BUSTR\") regarding underground storage tank (\"UST\") records associated with the Subject Property. BUSTR\'s records reflect the following:

One active underground storage tank is currently registered for the Subject Property: a **10,000-gallon diesel fuel underground storage tank**, Ohio UST Facility ID 48-22-10-0087. According to BUSTR\'s registration records, this tank was installed in 1978 and was upgraded with cathodic corrosion protection in 1998 to comply with the federal UST technical standards promulgated under 40 C.F.R. Part 280. The most recent compliance inspection of this UST was conducted on May 15, 2022, with no violations noted at that time.

BUSTR\'s database contains **no records of any other registered underground storage tanks** at the Subject Property. Specifically, no registration filings, installation permits, closure notifications, tank removal reports, site assessment results, or any other documentation exists in BUSTR\'s records for either of the following:

> • A **2,000-gallon waste solvent underground storage tank** at the Subject Property address; or
>
> • A **500-gallon heating oil underground storage tank** at the Subject Property address.

Ohio EPA notes that Greenfield\'s records request referenced historical Sanborn fire insurance map documentation (1987 edition) indicating the possible presence of two additional underground storage tanks at the Subject Property matching the descriptions above. However, BUSTR has no corresponding registration, installation, closure, or removal records in its database for tanks of those descriptions at this address.

The absence of UST registration records in BUSTR\'s database does not confirm or deny the physical presence or absence of these tanks at the Subject Property. It indicates only that no UST registration filings, closure notifications, tank removal reports, or corrective action records were submitted to BUSTR for tanks of those descriptions at 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Ohio 43611. Ohio EPA recommends that the requestor consider additional investigation methods --- including, but not limited to, geophysical surveys, interviews with current and former facility personnel, and review of local fire department permit records --- if the status and disposition of these tanks is relevant to the environmental site assessment.

**[4. Groundwater Management Zone and Adjacent Site Information]{.underline}**

Ohio EPA informs the requestor that the Subject Property at 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive is located within a designated **Groundwater Management Zone** (\"GMZ\") established by Ohio EPA in connection with a confirmed release of **tetrachloroethylene** (\"PCE,\" also known as perchloroethylene, CAS No. 127-18-4) from the former Lake Erie Dry Cleaning facility located at 4900 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Ohio 43611. The Lake Erie Dry Cleaning property is situated immediately north of, and hydraulically upgradient from, the Subject Property.

The Lake Erie Dry Cleaning facility is listed on Ohio EPA\'s Master Sites List under **VAP Identification Number NW-0923**. A Phase II Property Assessment and remedial investigation conducted in 2014 by the responsible party and its environmental consultant delineated a PCE groundwater contamination plume migrating in a **south-southeasterly direction** from the source area at the Lake Erie Dry Cleaning property toward and in the direction of the Subject Property. The 2014 investigation included the installation and sampling of a network of monitoring wells in the vicinity of the source area. The maximum PCE concentration detected during the 2014 investigation was **87 µg/L** (micrograms per liter) at monitoring well **MW-7**, which is located approximately **120 feet north** of the northern property boundary of the Subject Property. For reference, the applicable generic numerical groundwater quality standard for PCE under Ohio\'s Voluntary Action Program is **5 µg/L**, as set forth in Ohio Administrative Code § 3745-300-08, Table 1. The detected concentration of 87 µg/L therefore exceeds the applicable standard by a factor of approximately 17.4.

In 2018, a **covenant not to sue** was issued under Ohio\'s Voluntary Action Program (\"VAP\"), Ohio Revised Code Chapter 3746, for the Lake Erie Dry Cleaning property at 4900 Lakeshore Industrial Drive. It is important to note that the VAP covenant applies **exclusively** to the Lake Erie Dry Cleaning parcel and does not address, release, or otherwise cover off-site migration of the PCE groundwater plume onto adjacent properties, including the Subject Property. The VAP covenant does not provide any liability protection, assurance, or environmental clearance for properties beyond the boundaries of the Lake Erie Dry Cleaning parcel.

An **environmental covenant** was recorded against the Lake Erie Dry Cleaning property under the Uniform Environmental Covenants Act, Ohio Revised Code § 5301.82, which includes restrictions on the use of groundwater at that parcel and a requirement that the property owner maintain an active sub-slab vapor mitigation system in any occupied structures on the Lake Erie Dry Cleaning property.

To Ohio EPA\'s knowledge, **no investigation of PCE concentrations in groundwater beneath the Subject Property has been conducted**, and the lateral and vertical extent of PCE plume migration onto or beneath the Subject Property has not been delineated. The GMZ designation may impose restrictions on new groundwater withdrawals, new well installations, and certain land uses within its boundaries, and the requestor is advised to consult with Ohio EPA\'s Division of Drinking and Ground Waters for additional information regarding any applicable GMZ restrictions.

**[5. Other Database Listings]{.underline}**

Ohio EPA confirms that the Subject Property at 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Ohio 43611, does **not** appear on the following Ohio EPA or federal environmental databases as of the date of this letter:

> • National Priorities List (\"NPL\");
>
> • CERCLIS / Superfund Enterprise Management System (\"SEMS\") active list;
>
> • Ohio Master Sites List (state Superfund equivalent);
>
> • RCRA Corrective Action (\"CORRACTS\") database; or
>
> • Emergency Response Notification System (\"ERNS\").

For completeness, Ohio EPA notes that the adjacent property to the east of the Subject Property, formerly operated by Toledo Coatings & Adhesives Inc., is listed on the CERCLIS/SEMS archive under EPA ID OHD987654321 as a preliminary assessment site. However, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency issued a No Further Remedial Action Planned (\"NFRAP\") determination for that property in 1999, and Ohio EPA has no additional records of environmental investigation, remediation, or regulatory activity at that location subsequent to the NFRAP determination.

Lakeshore Freight Services, located to the south of the Subject Property, has no listings in any Ohio EPA environmental databases reviewed in connection with this records request.

**[6. Limitations and Disclaimers]{.underline}**

This response is based on records currently available in Ohio EPA\'s Northwest District Office files and coordinated agency databases as of the date of this letter. Records that are archived at other locations, maintained at other Ohio EPA district offices, or held by other state or federal agencies may contain additional information not reflected herein. Ohio EPA has made reasonable efforts to identify responsive records within its files; however, this response should not be construed as an exhaustive compilation of all environmental information that may exist regarding the Subject Property.

This response does not constitute a determination of liability, a no further action letter, a covenant not to sue, or any other formal determination or release under any state or federal environmental program. Ohio EPA makes no representations or warranties regarding the completeness, accuracy, or currency of information provided by regulated entities in their filings, submissions, or reports to the agency. The absence of records regarding a particular matter should not be interpreted as confirmation that such matter did not occur, nor should the presence of records be interpreted as a definitive statement regarding current conditions at the Subject Property.

Ohio EPA recommends that the requestor also consult with the following agencies, which may maintain additional records relevant to the environmental assessment of the Subject Property:

> • City of Toledo Department of Public Utilities (sewer connection and industrial pretreatment/discharge records);
>
> • Lucas County Health Department (solid waste, septage, and nuisance complaint records);
>
> • Toledo Fire Department (hazardous materials storage permits, fire inspection records, and underground storage tank permits); and
>
> • Ohio Department of Commerce, Division of State Fire Marshal (aboveground storage tank records, if applicable).

If you have any questions regarding this response or require additional information, please do not hesitate to contact me at the telephone number or email address provided below. Ohio EPA appreciates your inquiry and is committed to facilitating access to environmental records in support of due diligence activities conducted in the public interest.

Sincerely,

Thomas R. Kessler Environmental Specialist Division of Environmental Response and Revitalization Northwest District Office

Telephone: (419) 352-8461, ext. 234

Email: thomas.kessler@epa.ohio.gov

cc: Breckenridge Manufacturing Co.

> Attn: Gerald Breckenridge III
>
> 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive Toledo, Ohio 43611

**Enclosure:**

Attachment A --- Ohio EPA Compliance Evaluation Inspection Report, 4820 Lakeshore Industrial Drive, Toledo, Ohio 43611, dated July 17, 2003 (12 pages).

Note: The Director\'s Final Findings and Orders (consent order), File No. NW-2003-0471, is NOT enclosed with this response. That document is archived at Ohio EPA\'s central records repository in Columbus and must be requested separately from the Division of Hazardous Waste Management, Central Office, in accordance with the instructions provided in Section 2 of this letter.

**[1.0 EXECUTIVE SUMMARY]{.underline}**

Redland Environmental Services (Redland) was retained by Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc. (Valtec) to oversee the removal and closure assessment of three underground storage tanks (USTs) at the Valtec facility located at 2850 Millrace Road, Dayton, Ohio 45414, Montgomery County, Ohio (Tax Parcel ID: R72-04410-0025). The facility is owned and operated by Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc. The property is registered with the Ohio Environmental Protection Agency (Ohio EPA) under Facility ID 0912345678.

Tank removal activities were conducted during the week of September 13 through September 17, 2004, with additional over-excavation activities conducted on September 22, 2004. The following three USTs were removed from the site:

> • **Tank 1** --- 10,000-gallon single-wall bare steel underground storage tank, formerly containing #2 diesel fuel, installed in approximately 1965 by Dayton Forge & Die Company.
>
> • **Tank 2** --- 10,000-gallon single-wall bare steel underground storage tank, formerly containing #2 diesel fuel, installed in approximately 1965 by Dayton Forge & Die Company.
>
> • **Tank 3** --- 4,000-gallon single-wall bare steel underground storage tank, formerly containing waste oil (used crankcase oil, cutting oils, hydraulic fluids, and other waste petroleum products), installed in approximately 1972 by Millrace Industrial Park LLC.

All three tanks were located in the southern yard area of the property, south of Building 1 (Main Manufacturing Building, approximately 82,000 square feet, originally constructed in 1942). The tanks had not been in active service since approximately 1998, when Valtec transitioned to above-ground fuel storage and contracted with a waste oil hauler for off-site disposal of waste petroleum products.

All three tanks were removed from service and excavated under the regulatory authority of the Ohio EPA Bureau of Underground Storage Tank Regulations (BUSTR), in accordance with applicable requirements of Ohio Administrative Code (OAC) 1301:7-9-13. Confirmation soil samples were collected from the tank excavation sidewalls and bases in accordance with BUSTR closure assessment requirements.

The following is a summary of analytical results by tank:

**Tank 1:** Confirmation soil samples collected from the Tank 1 excavation on September 17, 2004, demonstrated diesel-range organic (DRO) concentrations below the Ohio EPA BUSTR action level of 500 mg/kg for commercial/industrial land use. The maximum DRO concentration detected was 340 mg/kg. All other analytes were also below applicable BUSTR action levels. No further action is recommended for Tank 1.

**Tank 2:** Confirmation soil samples collected from the initial Tank 2 excavation on September 17, 2004, showed DRO concentrations exceeding the BUSTR action level of 500 mg/kg at multiple sample locations. Over-excavation was conducted on September 22, 2004, and confirmation samples collected from the expanded excavation demonstrated DRO concentrations below the 500 mg/kg action level. Approximately 85 cubic yards of petroleum-impacted soil was removed and disposed of at a permitted Subtitle D landfill facility. No further action is recommended for Tank 2 following over-excavation.

**Tank 3:** Analytical results for the Tank 3 confirmation soil samples are pending at the time of this report. A total of seven confirmation samples were collected from the Tank 3 excavation on September 17, 2004, and submitted to the laboratory for analysis. Results will be provided in a supplemental report upon receipt from the laboratory.

Based on the foregoing, Redland recommends that Ohio EPA BUSTR issue closure letters for Tank 1 and Tank 2 based on satisfactory confirmation sampling results. A supplemental closure report will be submitted for Tank 3 upon receipt and evaluation of the laboratory analytical data.

**[2.0 SITE BACKGROUND]{.underline}**

**2.1 Property Description**

The subject property is a 14.7-acre industrial parcel located at 2850 Millrace Road, Dayton, Ohio 45414, in Montgomery County, Ohio. The property is identified by Montgomery County Tax Parcel ID R72-04410-0025. The site is situated in a mixed industrial and commercial area on the east side of Millrace Road, approximately 0.3 miles north of the intersection of Millrace Road and Webster Street. Surrounding land uses include light industrial and warehousing operations to the north and east, commercial retail to the south, and the Millrace Road right-of-way and additional industrial properties to the west.

The site includes five industrial buildings totaling approximately 187,000 square feet of enclosed floor area. The principal structure, Building 1 (Main Manufacturing), is an approximately 82,000-square-foot steel-framed, masonry-walled industrial building originally constructed in 1942. The three underground storage tanks that are the subject of this report were located in the paved southern yard area, south of Building 1.

**2.2 Ownership and Operational History**

The following is a summary of the relevant ownership and operational history for the property:

**Dayton Forge & Die Company (1942--1968).** Dayton Forge & Die Company constructed the original manufacturing facility and operated a forging and machining operation on the western approximately 6 acres of the property from 1942 through 1968. During this period, Dayton Forge & Die installed two 10,000-gallon bare steel underground storage tanks (Tank 1 and Tank 2) in approximately 1965 to supply #2 diesel fuel for on-site heavy equipment, including forklifts, die-setting cranes, and delivery trucks. Dayton Forge & Die ceased operations at the site in 1968.

**Millrace Industrial Park LLC (1968--1987).** Millrace Industrial Park LLC acquired the full 14.7-acre parcel in 1968 and operated the property as a multi-tenant industrial park through 1987. During this period, Millrace Industrial Park LLC installed a 4,000-gallon bare steel underground storage tank (Tank 3) in approximately 1972 for the collection and storage of waste oils generated by on-site tenant operations, including used crankcase oil, cutting oils, and hydraulic fluids. Millrace Industrial Park LLC sold the property to Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc. in 1987. Millrace Industrial Park LLC was subsequently dissolved as an Ohio limited liability company in 1992.

**Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc. (1987--Present).** Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc. (Valtec) is an Ohio corporation formed in 1987. Valtec acquired the property in 1987 and has since operated stamped metal parts manufacturing, electroplating, and powder coating operations at the facility. Valtec discontinued active use of the three USTs in approximately 1998, transitioning diesel fuel storage to above-ground storage tanks and contracting with a licensed waste oil hauler for periodic removal and off-site disposal of waste petroleum products.

**2.3 UST Registration**

All three underground storage tanks were registered with the Ohio EPA Bureau of Underground Storage Tank Regulations (BUSTR) under Ohio EPA Facility ID 0912345678. Registration records indicate that the tanks were listed as \"temporarily out of service\" beginning in approximately 1998.

**2.4 Regional Geology and Hydrogeology**

The site is situated within the Great Miami River valley and is underlain by Pleistocene-age glacial outwash deposits. The subsurface in the site vicinity consists primarily of sand and gravel with interbedded silt and clay lenses, typical of the glacial outwash sequence in the Dayton area. Based on regional hydrogeologic data and available water-level information from nearby monitoring wells and water supply wells, the depth to groundwater in the site vicinity is estimated at approximately 15 to 25 feet below ground surface (bgs). Groundwater flow direction is generally to the south-southwest, consistent with regional topography and the direction of the Great Miami River, which lies approximately 1.5 miles to the southwest. The site drains to an unnamed tributary of the Great Miami River, located approximately 650 feet southwest of the property boundary. The glacial outwash aquifer in this area is classified as a significant regional aquifer and is used as a source of drinking water by municipalities and private well owners in the region.

**[3.0 TANK DESCRIPTIONS]{.underline}**

The following subsections provide detailed descriptions of each of the three underground storage tanks removed during the closure activities.

**3.1 Tank 1 --- 10,000-Gallon Diesel UST**

> • **Tank Identification:** Tank 1
>
> • **Contents:** #2 diesel fuel
>
> • **Capacity:** 10,000 gallons
>
> • **Construction Material:** Single-wall bare steel (no interior lining or cathodic protection)
>
> • **Approximate Installation Date:** 1965 (installed by Dayton Forge & Die Company)
>
> • **Dimensions:** Approximately 8 feet in diameter by 27 feet in length (horizontal cylindrical configuration)
>
> • **Location:** Southern yard area, approximately 40 feet south of the southwest corner of Building 1 (Main Manufacturing). The tank was oriented with its long axis running east-west.
>
> • **Associated Piping:** Steel suction piping, single-wall, approximately 60 linear feet extending from the tank to a former dispenser island located in the southern yard area. The dispenser had been removed prior to the tank closure activities.
>
> • **Last Known Use Date:** Approximately 1998
>
> • **Condition at Removal:** The tank exhibited moderate external corrosion consistent with its age and lack of corrosion protection. Pitting was observed along the bottom and lower third of the tank shell. No visible through-wall perforations were observed. Approximately 2 inches of diesel sludge and water remained in the tank bottom at the time of removal. The backfill material surrounding the tank consisted of native sand and gravel with minor silt.

**3.2 Tank 2 --- 10,000-Gallon Diesel UST**

> • **Tank Identification:** Tank 2
>
> • **Contents:** #2 diesel fuel
>
> • **Capacity:** 10,000 gallons
>
> • **Construction Material:** Single-wall bare steel (no interior lining or cathodic protection)
>
> • **Approximate Installation Date:** 1965 (installed by Dayton Forge & Die Company)
>
> • **Dimensions:** Approximately 8 feet in diameter by 27 feet in length (horizontal cylindrical configuration)
>
> • **Location:** Southern yard area, approximately 20 feet east of Tank 1 and approximately 45 feet south of Building 1. The tank was oriented with its long axis running east-west, parallel to Tank 1.
>
> • **Associated Piping:** Steel suction piping, single-wall, approximately 55 linear feet extending from the tank to the same former dispenser island as Tank 1.
>
> • **Last Known Use Date:** Approximately 1998
>
> • **Condition at Removal:** The tank exhibited heavy external corrosion, significantly more severe than that observed on Tank 1. Extensive pitting and metal loss were observed along the bottom and lower half of the tank shell. Multiple visible through-wall perforations were observed, with at least three holes identified on the tank bottom. The largest perforation was approximately 2 inches in diameter. Stained soil, dark brown to black in color, was observed at the base of the excavation directly beneath the perforations. A strong petroleum odor was noted in the excavation during tank removal, and a slight sheen was observed on water that collected in the base of the excavation. The observations of through-wall corrosion and stained soil were consistent with a historical release of diesel fuel from this tank.

**3.3 Tank 3 --- 4,000-Gallon Waste Oil UST**

> • **Tank Identification:** Tank 3
>
> • **Contents:** Waste oil, including used crankcase oil, cutting oils, hydraulic fluids, and other waste petroleum products generated by on-site manufacturing operations
>
> • **Capacity:** 4,000 gallons
>
> • **Construction Material:** Single-wall bare steel (no interior lining or cathodic protection)
>
> • **Approximate Installation Date:** 1972 (installed by Millrace Industrial Park LLC)
>
> • **Dimensions:** Approximately 6 feet in diameter by 20 feet in length (horizontal cylindrical configuration)
>
> • **Location:** Southern yard area, approximately 50 feet east of Tank 2 and approximately 35 feet south of the southeast corner of Building 1. The tank was oriented with its long axis running north-south.
>
> • **Associated Piping:** Steel gravity-feed piping from Building 1 floor drains, approximately 40 linear feet in total length, connecting to the north end of the tank. A separate fill port was located on the north side of the tank, accessible from the yard surface.
>
> • **Last Known Use Date:** Approximately 1998
>
> • **Condition at Removal:** The tank exhibited moderate to heavy external corrosion, with extensive pitting along the bottom and lower third of the tank shell. One visible through-wall perforation was identified on the tank bottom, approximately 1.5 inches in diameter. A dark, viscous, oily residue remained in the tank bottom at the time of removal, approximately 4 inches in depth. This residue was consistent with accumulated waste oil sludge. Stained soil, dark black in color and with a strong petroleum/waste oil odor, was observed at the base of the excavation beneath the tank, particularly in the area directly below the through-wall perforation. The staining extended into the lower approximately 2 feet of the south and west excavation sidewalls. The backfill material surrounding the tank consisted of native sand and gravel that was visibly discolored in the immediate vicinity of the tank bottom.

**[4.0 REMOVAL PROCEDURES]{.underline}**

**4.1 Pre-Removal Activities**

Ohio EPA BUSTR was notified of the planned underground storage tank removal on August 20, 2004, in accordance with the notification requirements of Ohio Administrative Code (OAC) 1301:7-9-13. The notification was submitted under BUSTR Notification Number BUSTR-2004-0912345678-01. BUSTR acknowledged receipt of the notification and confirmed that an inspector would be available to observe the closure activities.

A private utility locate was conducted on September 10, 2004, by Ohio Utility Protection Service (OUPS) and a private utility locating contractor to identify and mark all subsurface utilities in the planned excavation areas. Identified utilities included a 4-inch natural gas service line, a 6-inch sanitary sewer lateral, and electrical conduits serving the former dispenser island. All identified utilities were marked and protected or disconnected prior to excavation.

Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc. arranged for a licensed waste hauler, Clean Harbors Environmental Services, to pump and transport residual tank contents and tank rinsate for off-site disposal at a permitted facility. Waste manifests documenting the disposal of all residual liquids and rinsate are included in Appendix B (not reproduced in these excerpts).

A licensed tank removal contractor, Midwest Tank & Excavation, Inc. of Trotwood, Ohio, was retained to perform the physical excavation and removal of the three underground storage tanks. Midwest Tank & Excavation mobilized equipment to the site on September 13, 2004.

**4.2 Tank Removal --- September 13--17, 2004**

**September 13, 2004:** Residual liquid contents were pumped from all three tanks by Clean Harbors Environmental Services using a vacuum truck. The following approximate volumes of residual material were removed:

> • Tank 1: Approximately 50 gallons of diesel sludge and water
>
> • Tank 2: Approximately 75 gallons of diesel sludge and water
>
> • Tank 3: Approximately 120 gallons of waste oil and sludge

All residual contents were containerized in DOT-approved 55-gallon drums and transported under hazardous waste manifest by Clean Harbors Environmental Services for disposal at an approved treatment and disposal facility. Following removal of residual contents, each tank was triple-rinsed with water, and the rinsate was similarly containerized and manifested for off-site disposal.

**September 14--15, 2004:** Excavation and removal of Tank 1 and Tank 2 were conducted using a tracked excavator. Each tank excavation measured approximately 12 feet wide by 32 feet long by 12 feet deep. Native backfill material that was not visually impacted was stockpiled on-site for potential reuse as backfill material. Soil that was visually stained with petroleum or exhibited a petroleum odor was segregated and stockpiled on 6-mil polyethylene sheeting. Associated piping was removed from each tank excavation and set aside for recycling. An Ohio EPA BUSTR inspector was present during the Tank 1 and Tank 2 removal activities on September 14, 2004.

**September 16, 2004:** Excavation and removal of Tank 3 was conducted. The Tank 3 excavation measured approximately 10 feet wide by 25 feet long by 10 feet deep. During excavation, stained and odorous soil was observed at the excavation base and along the lower approximately 2 feet of the south and west sidewalls. The staining was dark black in color and had a strong petroleum/waste oil odor. This soil was segregated from clean material and stockpiled on polyethylene sheeting. The gravity-feed piping from Building 1 was cut and capped at the building foundation wall.

**September 17, 2004:** Confirmation soil samples were collected from all three tank excavations by Redland field personnel. Sampling procedures and results are described in detail in Section 5. All three excavations were left open and secured with temporary construction fencing pending receipt of analytical results. Removed tanks were loaded onto flatbed trailers by Midwest Tank & Excavation, Inc. and transported to a licensed scrap metal recycling facility for processing.

**4.3 Over-Excavation --- Tank 2**

Based on field observations during the September 14--15 removal activities --- including heavy soil staining, strong petroleum odor, visible free product sheen on soil, and the through-wall perforations observed on the Tank 2 shell --- Redland anticipated that confirmation sample results for Tank 2 might exceed BUSTR action levels. Upon receipt of the initial Tank 2 analytical results (see Section 5.3), which confirmed DRO concentrations above the 500 mg/kg action level at four of seven sample locations, Redland directed Midwest Tank & Excavation, Inc. to conduct additional over-excavation at the Tank 2 location.

Over-excavation was conducted on September 22, 2004. The Tank 2 excavation was extended approximately 4 feet deeper (from approximately 12 feet bgs to approximately 16 feet bgs) and approximately 3 feet laterally on the south and east sidewalls, where the highest concentrations had been detected. Confirmation samples were collected from the over-excavation sidewalls and base on September 22, 2004, as described in Section 5.4.

In total, approximately 85 cubic yards of petroleum-impacted soil was removed from the Tank 2 excavation area, including soil from both the original excavation and the over-excavation. All impacted soils were loaded into end-dump trucks and transported by Midwest Tank & Excavation, Inc. to a permitted Subtitle D landfill facility for disposal. Disposal documentation is provided in Appendix B (not reproduced in these excerpts).

**4.4 Backfill and Surface Restoration**

Following receipt of satisfactory analytical results for the Tank 1 and Tank 2 excavations (see Sections 5.2 and 5.4), backfill and surface restoration activities were conducted in October 2004.

The Tank 1 excavation was backfilled with clean imported granular fill (ODOT Item 304 aggregate base) compacted in 12-inch lifts using a vibratory plate compactor to achieve approximately 95 percent of maximum dry density. The upper 6 inches of the excavation area was surfaced with hot-mix asphalt consistent with the surrounding yard pavement.

The Tank 2 excavation was similarly backfilled with clean imported granular fill compacted in lifts, with a hot-mix asphalt surface placed over the former excavation area.

The Tank 3 excavation was temporarily backfilled with stockpiled native soils pending receipt of analytical results and further evaluation. The temporary backfill was placed without compaction control, and an asphalt surface was placed over the former Tank 3 excavation area as part of the overall site surface restoration conducted in October 2004. If over-excavation is determined to be necessary for the Tank 3 area based on the pending analytical results, the asphalt surface and temporary backfill will need to be removed to facilitate additional soil removal.

**[5.0 SOIL SAMPLING AND ANALYTICAL RESULTS]{.underline}**

**5.1 Sampling Methodology**

Confirmation soil samples were collected from each tank excavation in accordance with the Ohio EPA BUSTR Closure Assessment Guidance (revised January 2002). The sampling program was designed to characterize residual soil conditions at the base and sidewalls of each excavation following tank and impacted-soil removal.

For each tank excavation (original excavation), a total of seven (7) soil samples were collected from the following locations:

> • **Excavation base:** Three samples --- center of base, east end of base, and west end of base
>
> • **Excavation sidewalls:** Four samples --- midpoint of north sidewall, midpoint of south sidewall, midpoint of east sidewall, and midpoint of west sidewall

For the Tank 2 over-excavation, an additional five (5) samples were collected from the following locations:

> • **Over-excavation base:** Three samples --- center of base, southeast corner, and southwest corner
>
> • **Over-excavation sidewalls:** Two samples --- midpoint of south sidewall, midpoint of east sidewall

All soil samples were collected from the exposed excavation face at a depth of approximately 6 inches into undisturbed soil using a decontaminated stainless steel hand auger. Between each sample collection, the hand auger was decontaminated using an Alconox wash followed by a distilled water rinse. Samples were placed directly into laboratory-supplied containers, including 4-ounce glass jars with Teflon-lined caps for volatile organic analysis and 8-ounce wide-mouth glass jars for semi-volatile and metals analysis. All sample containers were immediately labeled, placed on ice in insulated coolers, and maintained at approximately 4°C during transport.

Samples were transported under chain-of-custody documentation to Great Lakes Analytical Laboratory in Dayton, Ohio, a state-certified analytical laboratory (Ohio EPA Certification No. CL-0278). Samples were analyzed for the following parameters:

> • Diesel-Range Organics (DRO) by Ohio EPA Method 9034
>
> • Gasoline-Range Organics (GRO) by Ohio EPA Method 9033
>
> • BTEX compounds (benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, and total xylenes) by EPA Method 8021B
>
> • Lead by EPA Method 6010B (ICP-AES)

Analytical results were compared to the applicable Ohio EPA BUSTR Action Levels for Commercial/Industrial Land Use, as specified in the BUSTR Closure Assessment Guidance. Complete laboratory analytical reports, including quality assurance/quality control data, are provided in Appendix C (not reproduced in these excerpts).

**5.2 Analytical Results --- Tank 1**

Confirmation soil samples were collected from the Tank 1 excavation on September 17, 2004. A total of seven samples were collected and submitted to Great Lakes Analytical Laboratory. Analytical results are summarized in Table 5-1 below.

**Table 5-1: Soil Analytical Results --- Tank 1 Excavation (September 17, 2004)**

  ------------------------ ---------------- -------------------- ----------------- ----------------- --------------------- --------------------- -------------------------- --------------------- ------------------
  T1-B-C                   Base, Center     11.5                 340               12.0              \<0.005               0.08                  0.03                       0.15                  18.5

  T1-B-E                   Base, East End   11.0                 180               8.2               \<0.005               \<0.005               \<0.005                    0.06                  14.2

  T1-B-W                   Base, West End   11.5                 210               6.5               \<0.005               \<0.005               0.02                       0.09                  16.0

  T1-SW-N                  North Sidewall   8.0                  45                \<5.0             \<0.005               \<0.005               \<0.005                    \<0.005               11.3

  T1-SW-W                  West Sidewall    8.0                  95                \<5.0             \<0.005               0.02                  \<0.005                    0.04                  13.1

All Tank 1 confirmation soil sample results were below the applicable Ohio EPA BUSTR action levels for commercial/industrial land use. The maximum DRO concentration detected was 340 mg/kg at the base center location (Sample T1-B-C), which is below the BUSTR action level of 500 mg/kg. Gasoline-range organics, BTEX compounds, and lead were all well below their respective action levels at all sample locations. Elevated DRO concentrations at the base relative to the sidewalls are consistent with the minor diesel sludge observed in the tank bottom during removal, but all concentrations are within acceptable limits. Based on these results, no further action is recommended for the Tank 1 excavation.

**5.3 Analytical Results --- Tank 2 (Initial Excavation)**

Confirmation soil samples were collected from the initial Tank 2 excavation on September 17, 2004. A total of seven samples were collected and submitted to Great Lakes Analytical Laboratory. Analytical results are summarized in Table 5-2 below.

**Table 5-2: Soil Analytical Results --- Tank 2 Initial Excavation (September 17, 2004)**

  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Sample ID**            **Location**     **Depth (ft bgs)**   **DRO (mg/kg)**   **GRO (mg/kg)**   **Benzene (mg/kg)**   **Toluene (mg/kg)**   **Ethylbenzene (mg/kg)**   **Xylenes (mg/kg)**   **Lead (mg/kg)**
  ------------------------ ---------------- -------------------- ----------------- ----------------- --------------------- --------------------- -------------------------- --------------------- ------------------
  T2-B-C                   Base, Center     12.0                 **1,250**         42.0              0.12                  1.45                  0.38                       3.20                  28.5

  T2-B-E                   Base, East End   11.5                 **820**           28.5              0.06                  0.72                  0.15                       1.80                  22.0

  T2-B-W                   Base, West End   12.0                 **680**           18.0              \<0.005               0.35                  0.08                       0.95                  19.3

  T2-SW-N                  North Sidewall   8.0                  180               7.5               \<0.005               0.06                  \<0.005                    0.12                  14.5

  T2-SW-S                  South Sidewall   8.5                  **590**           22.0              0.04                  0.55                  0.12                       1.40                  21.8

  T2-SW-E                  East Sidewall    8.0                  310               12.0              \<0.005               0.18                  0.05                       0.45                  16.2

  T2-SW-W                  West Sidewall    8.0                  220               9.0               \<0.005               0.10                  \<0.005                    0.30                  15.0

  **BUSTR Action Level**   ---              ---                  **500**           **100**           **0.9**               **17**                **10**                     **70**                **400**
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Multiple Tank 2 initial excavation samples exceeded the Ohio EPA BUSTR DRO action level of 500 mg/kg. Specifically, the base center sample (T2-B-C) contained DRO at 1,250 mg/kg, the base east sample (T2-B-E) at 820 mg/kg, the base west sample (T2-B-W) at 680 mg/kg, and the south sidewall sample (T2-SW-S) at 590 mg/kg. These elevated concentrations are consistent with field observations of heavy staining, petroleum odor, and free product sheen in the area beneath the through-wall perforations identified on the Tank 2 shell. The south sidewall exceedance indicates lateral migration of diesel fuel in the native sand and gravel backfill. GRO, BTEX, and lead concentrations, while elevated relative to Tank 1 results, did not exceed their respective BUSTR action levels. Based on these results, over-excavation was conducted as described in Section 4.3 and confirmation sampling results are presented in Section 5.4 below.

**5.4 Analytical Results --- Tank 2 (Over-Excavation Confirmation)**

Following over-excavation at the Tank 2 location on September 22, 2004, a total of five confirmation soil samples were collected from the expanded excavation and submitted to Great Lakes Analytical Laboratory. Analytical results are summarized in Table 5-3 below.

**Table 5-3: Soil Analytical Results --- Tank 2 Over-Excavation (September 22, 2004)**

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Sample ID**            **Location**      **Depth (ft bgs)**   **DRO (mg/kg)**   **GRO (mg/kg)**   **Benzene (mg/kg)**   **Toluene (mg/kg)**   **Ethylbenzene (mg/kg)**   **Xylenes (mg/kg)**   **Lead (mg/kg)**
  ------------------------ ----------------- -------------------- ----------------- ----------------- --------------------- --------------------- -------------------------- --------------------- ------------------
  T2-OE-B-C                Base, Center      16.0                 280               8.5               \<0.005               0.04                  \<0.005                    0.10                  15.8

  T2-OE-SW-S               South Sidewall    12.0                 190               6.0               \<0.005               \<0.005               \<0.005                    0.06                  13.2

  T2-OE-SW-E               East Sidewall     12.0                 150               \<5.0             \<0.005               \<0.005               \<0.005                    \<0.005               11.5

  T2-OE-B-SE               Base, SE Corner   16.0                 240               7.0               \<0.005               0.03                  \<0.005                    0.08                  14.0

  T2-OE-B-SW               Base, SW Corner   16.0                 210               6.2               \<0.005               \<0.005               \<0.005                    0.05                  13.8

  **BUSTR Action Level**   ---               ---                  **500**           **100**           **0.9**               **17**                **10**                     **70**                **400**
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

All Tank 2 over-excavation confirmation sample results were below the applicable Ohio EPA BUSTR action levels for commercial/industrial land use. The maximum DRO concentration detected in the over-excavation samples was 280 mg/kg at the base center location (Sample T2-OE-B-C), which is well below the BUSTR action level of 500 mg/kg. GRO, BTEX, and lead were similarly below their respective action levels at all over-excavation sample locations. The significant reduction in DRO concentrations from the initial excavation results (maximum 1,250 mg/kg) to the over-excavation results (maximum 280 mg/kg) confirms that the over-excavation successfully removed the majority of petroleum-impacted soil in the Tank 2 area. Based on these results, no further action is recommended for the Tank 2 excavation following over-excavation.

**5.5 Analytical Results --- Tank 3**

Confirmation soil samples were collected from the Tank 3 excavation on September 17, 2004, using the same sampling methodology and analytical parameters described in Section 5.1. A total of seven (7) samples (T3-B-C, T3-B-E, T3-B-W, T3-SW-N, T3-SW-S, T3-SW-E, T3-SW-W) were collected and submitted to Great Lakes Analytical Laboratory under chain-of-custody.

Analytical results for the Tank 3 confirmation samples had not been received from the laboratory at the time of preparation of this report. Results will be presented in a supplemental report upon receipt.

\[*Editor\'s/Reviewer\'s Note: A handwritten annotation in the margin of the original document at this location reads: \"Lab results pending --- will supplement.\" No supplemental report has been located in the project files.*\]

**[6.0 CONCLUSIONS AND RECOMMENDATIONS]{.underline}**

Based on the tank removal activities, field observations, and available analytical data described in this report, Redland Environmental Services offers the following conclusions and recommendations for each of the three underground storage tanks removed from the 2850 Millrace Road facility:

**Tank 1 --- 10,000-Gallon Diesel UST.** Confirmation soil sampling conducted on September 17, 2004, demonstrated that residual petroleum hydrocarbon concentrations in soil at the Tank 1 excavation are below the applicable Ohio EPA BUSTR action levels for commercial/industrial land use. No DRO, GRO, BTEX, or lead concentrations exceeded the applicable standards at any of the seven sample locations. The tank excavation has been backfilled with clean imported granular fill and surfaced with asphalt. Redland recommends that Ohio EPA BUSTR issue a No Further Action (NFA) closure letter for Tank 1. No further investigation or remediation is recommended for the Tank 1 area.

**Tank 2 --- 10,000-Gallon Diesel UST.** Initial confirmation soil sampling conducted on September 17, 2004, indicated DRO concentrations exceeding the BUSTR action level of 500 mg/kg at four of seven sample locations within the Tank 2 excavation, with a maximum concentration of 1,250 mg/kg at the base center. Over-excavation was conducted on September 22, 2004, extending the excavation approximately 4 feet deeper and 3 feet laterally on the south and east sidewalls. Confirmation samples collected following over-excavation demonstrated that all residual petroleum hydrocarbon concentrations are below the applicable BUSTR action levels. Approximately 85 cubic yards of petroleum-impacted soil was removed from the Tank 2 area and disposed of at a permitted Subtitle D landfill facility. The tank excavation has been backfilled with clean imported granular fill and surfaced with asphalt. Redland recommends that Ohio EPA BUSTR issue a No Further Action (NFA) closure letter for Tank 2. No further investigation or remediation is recommended for the Tank 2 area.

**Tank 3 --- 4,000-Gallon Waste Oil UST.** Closure assessment for Tank 3 is pending receipt of analytical results. Redland will submit a supplemental closure report for Tank 3 upon receipt and evaluation of the laboratory data. Based on field observations during tank removal (see Section 3.3 and Section 4.2), petroleum-impacted soil was observed at the base and sidewalls of the Tank 3 excavation. Stained soil with a strong petroleum/waste oil odor was present at the excavation base and along the lower approximately 2 feet of the south and west sidewalls. The extent of impacts cannot be fully evaluated until analytical results are available. It is possible that over-excavation may be required for Tank 3, depending on the analytical results. The Tank 3 excavation has been temporarily backfilled with native soils and surfaced with asphalt pending receipt of the analytical data and determination of whether additional soil removal is warranted.

**General.** All tank removal and closure activities were conducted in general accordance with Ohio EPA BUSTR requirements under OAC 1301:7-9-13. Waste materials, including residual tank contents, rinsate, and petroleum-impacted soils, were disposed of at properly permitted facilities in accordance with applicable federal and state waste disposal regulations. Documentation of waste disposal, including waste manifests and disposal receipts, is provided in Appendix B (not reproduced in these excerpts).

**Groundwater.** Groundwater was not encountered in any of the three tank excavations during the removal and over-excavation activities. The maximum excavation depth achieved was approximately 16 feet below ground surface at the Tank 2 over-excavation. This is consistent with the estimated depth to groundwater of 15 to 25 feet bgs in the site vicinity, as described in Section 2.4. However, given the proximity of the excavation depths to the estimated seasonal high water table, and particularly in light of the elevated DRO concentrations initially detected at the Tank 2 base (at approximately 12 feet bgs), the potential for petroleum impacts to groundwater cannot be ruled out at this time. If future site activities or regulatory requirements necessitate a groundwater assessment, installation of monitoring wells in the former tank area may be warranted.

**[LIMITATIONS AND QUALIFICATIONS]{.underline}**

This report was prepared by Redland Environmental Services for the exclusive use of Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc. and the Ohio EPA Bureau of Underground Storage Tank Regulations (BUSTR) for the purpose of documenting the removal and closure assessment of three underground storage tanks at 2850 Millrace Road, Dayton, Ohio 45414. The services described herein were performed in a manner consistent with the level of care ordinarily exercised by members of the environmental consulting profession currently practicing in the same locality under similar conditions.

The conclusions and recommendations presented in this report are based on the conditions observed at the time of field activities (September 13--22, 2004) and on the laboratory analytical data available at the time of report preparation (November 12, 2004). Subsurface conditions are inherently variable, and actual conditions at locations other than specific sample points may differ from those characterized in this report. Redland Environmental Services makes no warranty or guarantee, express or implied, regarding the completeness or accuracy of any information provided by others, or regarding future site conditions or future regulatory actions that may be required at the property.

This report does not address environmental conditions beyond the scope of the three underground storage tanks described herein, nor does it constitute an assessment of overall site environmental conditions.

This report will be supplemented upon receipt of the Tank 3 analytical data from Great Lakes Analytical Laboratory.

No third-party reliance on this report is intended or authorized without the prior written consent of Redland Environmental Services and Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc.

**[CERTIFICATION]{.underline}**

This report was prepared under my direction and supervision. The data and findings presented herein accurately represent the work performed during the closure assessment of three underground storage tanks at 2850 Millrace Road, Dayton, Ohio 45414. The information contained in this report is, to the best of my knowledge, accurate and complete as of the date of preparation, subject to the limitations and qualifications stated herein.

REDLAND ENVIRONMENTAL SERVICES

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

Name: David Renner, P.E.

Title: Project Manager

Ohio Professional Engineer Registration No. PE-54821

Date: November 12, 2004

**\[END OF EXCERPTS\]**

 

*Editor\'s Note: The foregoing pages constitute excerpts from the Redland Environmental Services Underground Storage Tank Closure Report dated November 12, 2004 (Redland Project No. RES-04-0287), as provided by Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc. during the due diligence document production for the proposed acquisition by Cornerstone Industrial Partners LLC. Appendices A through E of the original report were not produced as part of the seller\'s document delivery. No supplemental report addressing Tank 3 analytical results was located in the seller\'s project files, in the files of Redland Environmental Services, or in publicly available Ohio EPA records as of the date of this compilation.*

**[Section 1: Executive Summary]{.underline}**

GeoSearch Data Systems was retained by Terravista Environmental Consulting Inc. to perform a regulatory environmental database search for the property located at 2850 Millrace Road, Dayton, Ohio 45414 (the \"Target Property\"), and surrounding areas. The search was conducted in accordance with the standard search radii specified in ASTM E1527-21, *Standard Practice for Environmental Site Assessments: Phase I Environmental Site Assessment Process*. This report is intended to support a Phase I Environmental Site Assessment being prepared by Terravista Environmental Consulting Inc. for the Target Property.

The following is a summary of the findings of this environmental database search:

**Target Property Listings:** The Target Property was identified on three (3) regulatory databases: the RCRA Small Quantity Generator (SQG) database, the Ohio EPA Spills/Releases database, and the Ohio EPA Underground Storage Tank (UST) registration database. The Target Property was also identified on the Ohio EPA Voluntary Action Program (VAP) database, with a No Further Action (NFA) letter issued for a portion of the property. All Target Property listings are associated with Valtec Manufacturing Holdings Inc.

**Adjoining Property Listings:** One (1) adjoining property was identified on searched databases. Triton Metals Processing Inc., located at 2900 Millrace Road, Dayton, Ohio 45414, immediately east of the Target Property, was identified on the CERCLIS/SEMS database and the RCRA Large Quantity Generator (LQG) database.

**Sites Within Standard Search Radii:** Two (2) additional sites were identified within applicable ASTM standard search radii beyond the Target Property and adjoining properties. These include a former gasoline station at 2780 Millrace Road (LUST database, closed) and a former dry cleaner at 145 Commerce Park Drive (Ohio EPA Master Sites List, under review with confirmed tetrachloroethylene groundwater plume).

A total of four (4) sites, including the Target Property, were identified across all searched databases within the applicable ASTM E1527-21 search radii.

Note: This report does not include a search of the Ohio Department of Natural Resources (ODNR) Division of Water Resources well log database or any private/domestic water supply well databases. **[Section 4: Adjoining Property Summary]{.underline}**

**Properties Immediately Adjoining the Target Property Identified on Searched Databases**

**[Section 5: Summary of Sites Within Standard Search Radii]{.underline}**

**Sites Identified Within ASTM Standard Search Radii (Beyond Target Property and Adjoining Properties)**

Two (2) additional sites were identified within the applicable ASTM E1527-21 standard search radii beyond the Target Property and immediately adjoining properties. These sites are summarized in the table below and described in the individual entries that follow.

Specifically, no NPL sites were identified within 1.0 mile, no RCRA CORRACTS facilities were identified within 1.0 mile, no Delisted NPL sites were identified within 0.5 mile, no RCRA TSD facilities were identified within 0.5 mile, no additional CERCLIS/SEMS or CERCLIS NFRAP sites were identified within 0.5 mile beyond those reported above, and no state landfill, solid waste, or brownfield sites were identified within 0.5 mile.

**[Section 6: Orphan Sites Summary]{.underline}**

GeoSearch Data Systems identifies \"orphan sites\" as database entries that could not be mapped to a specific geographic location due to insufficient, ambiguous, or incomplete address information in the source government database. During the course of this search, one (1) orphan site entry was identified:

> • **\"Dayton Manufacturing Facility, Dayton, OH\"** --- This entry appeared in the ERNS database with no specific street address or geographic coordinates. The entry references a telephone notification received in 1998 regarding a minor chemical spill. No additional identifying information was available in the database record. Due to the lack of a mappable address, this entry could not be plotted relative to the Target Property and could not be evaluated for proximity or relevance to the search area.

No other orphan sites were identified within the search area that could potentially be associated with the Target Property or adjoining properties.

**[Section 7: Groundwater Flow and Topographic Summary]{.underline}**

The following regional groundwater and topographic information is provided as standard supplemental data included in GeoSearch Data Systems environmental database search reports. This information is derived from publicly available sources, including the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) 7.5-minute topographic quadrangle maps, USGS hydrogeological publications, and regional groundwater resource studies. This information has not been independently verified through site-specific investigation and is intended for general reference purposes only.

**Topography:** The Target Property and surrounding area are situated on relatively flat terrain with a slight southwesterly slope, reflecting an approximate grade of 2%. Site elevation is approximately 740 to 745 feet above mean sea level (AMSL) based on the USGS Dayton North 7.5-minute topographic quadrangle.

**Regional Groundwater Flow Direction:** Regional groundwater flow in the vicinity of the Target Property is generally toward the south-southwest, based on USGS data, regional potentiometric surface mapping, and published hydrogeological assessments of the Great Miami River buried valley aquifer system.

**Estimated Depth to Groundwater:** Regional data indicate that the depth to the water table in the vicinity of the Target Property is approximately 15 to 25 feet below ground surface (bgs). Actual depth to groundwater may vary due to local geological conditions, seasonal fluctuations, and proximity to surface water features.

**Aquifer Information:** The Target Property is underlain by the Great Miami Buried Valley Aquifer system, a glacially derived sand and gravel aquifer of regional significance. The site is not located within a federally designated Sole Source Aquifer area.

**Public Water Supply:** Municipal water service in the vicinity of the Target Property is provided by the City of Dayton, Department of Water. Public water supply production wells are located approximately 4 miles south of the Target Property along the Great Miami River corridor.

**Private Water Supply Wells:** No private water supply well search was conducted as part of this report. See Section 2 for a listing of databases not included in the scope of this engagement. If information regarding the presence or absence of private or domestic water supply wells in the vicinity of the Target Property is required, a separate well search should be requested.

**[Section 8: Limitations and Disclaimer]{.underline}**

This environmental database search report was prepared by GeoSearch Data Systems from publicly available government databases and regulatory records. The following limitations and conditions apply to the use of this report:

1\. This report was prepared using data obtained from federal, state, and local government sources that are believed to be reliable as of the report date of March 28, 2025. GeoSearch Data Systems does not guarantee the accuracy, completeness, or timeliness of data maintained by government agencies. Database records may contain errors, omissions, or outdated information. Data currency varies by agency and database; update frequencies are determined by the respective government data providers and are beyond the control of GeoSearch Data Systems.

2\. This report is an environmental database search report only. It is not a Phase I Environmental Site Assessment, and it should not be relied upon as such. This report does not include a site reconnaissance, interviews, historical research, or any field investigation component.

3\. The databases searched and the search radii applied in the preparation of this report are identified in Section 2. Databases not listed in Section 2 were not searched. The scope of this report is limited to the databases and search radii specified therein.

4\. Government databases may not reflect the most current regulatory actions, enforcement orders, permit modifications, or site status changes. Users of this report should verify the current status of any listed site directly with the applicable regulatory agency if current status is material to their evaluation.

5\. This report was prepared solely for the use of Terravista Environmental Consulting Inc. and its client in connection with the environmental evaluation of the Target Property at 2850 Millrace Road, Dayton, Ohio 45414. This report may not be relied upon by any third party without the prior written consent of GeoSearch Data Systems.

6\. The absence of a database listing in this report does not constitute a guarantee or warranty that recognized environmental conditions, controlled recognized environmental conditions, or historical recognized environmental conditions do not exist at, on, beneath, or in the vicinity of the Target Property. Environmental conditions may exist that are not reflected in the databases searched.

7\. This report does not include a search for private or domestic water supply wells, injection wells, or agricultural wells. If such information is required for the Phase I Environmental Site Assessment or for any other purpose, a separate well search report should be requested from GeoSearch Data Systems.

8\. GeoSearch Data Systems\' liability in connection with this report is limited to the fee paid for the preparation of this report. In no event shall GeoSearch Data Systems be liable for any indirect, incidental, special, consequential, or punitive damages arising from the use of or reliance on this report.

**[Appendix Reference / Closing]{.underline}**

Full database detail pages, individual site detail reports, government database record printouts, and mapping figures are provided in the complete GeoSearch Data Systems report (approximately 187 pages), available under separate cover. This summary has been prepared for inclusion in or reference by the Phase I Environmental Site Assessment being prepared by Terravista Environmental Consulting Inc. for the Target Property at 2850 Millrace Road, Dayton, Ohio 45414.

A site location map showing the Target Property, adjoining properties, and all database-listed sites within the applicable ASTM E1527-21 search radii is included in the full report. Refer to Figure 1 (Site Location Map) and Figure 2 (Database Search Results Map) in the complete report.

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

"""Validate a .docx for delivery.

Checks ZIP integrity, XML well-formedness for every part, [Content_Types].xml
presence, and relationship consistency (no dangling rIds). Does NOT do XSD
schema validation in v1 — defer until ECMA-376 XSDs are vendored fresh from
ECMA-International.

Usage: python validate.py file.docx
Exit 0 = valid; non-zero = errors printed to stderr.
"""
import sys
import zipfile
from pathlib import Path
from lxml import etree


def validate(path: Path) -> list[str]:
    errors = []

    if not path.exists():
        return [f"File not found: {path}"]
    if not zipfile.is_zipfile(path):
        return [f"Not a valid ZIP: {path}"]

    with zipfile.ZipFile(path) as z:
        names = set(z.namelist())

        if "[Content_Types].xml" not in names:
            errors.append("Missing [Content_Types].xml")
            return errors

        # XML well-formedness for every .xml and .rels part
        for name in names:
            if name.endswith(".xml") or name.endswith(".rels"):
                try:
                    etree.fromstring(z.read(name))
                except etree.XMLSyntaxError as e:
                    errors.append(f"Malformed XML in {name}: {e}")
